title: FineServe: A Fine-Grained Dataset and Characterization of Global LLM Serving Workloads
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as always-on online services, making efficient LLM serving a critical systems challenge. Achieving low latency and high throughput under volatile demand requires deep understanding of real-world serving workloads, yet existing studies often rely on proxy traces or coarse-grained characterizations that fail to capture the heterogeneity of modern multi-model LLM platforms. We present FineServe, an in-the-wild, multi-model LLM serving workload dataset collected from a global commercial marketplace, enabling fine-grained characterization of real-world serving dynamics across heterogeneous models and tasks. Leveraging FineServe, we conduct a comprehensive analysis of arrival dynamics and token behavior, revealing fundamentally different fluctuation regimes across model architectures, scales and task intents. Building on these insights, we develop the FineServe workload generator, which composes fine-grained model-aware workloads into configurable mixtures tailored for benchmarking multi-model serving platforms. By exposing these fine-grained workload dynamics, FineServe provides a realistic foundation for evaluating routing, scheduling, and capacity-planning strategies in LLM serving systems. FineServe is available at https://github.com/hihiztc1/FineServe.

AI.tldr: FineServe introduces a real-world, multi-model LLM serving workload dataset and generator for realistic benchmarking of LLM serving systems.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM serving studies rely on proxy traces or coarse, single-model characterizations that miss the heterogeneous, volatile, multi-model nature of real production workloads, limiting the realism of system evaluation.
AI.method: Collect in-the-wild serving traces from a global commercial LLM marketplace across diverse models and tasks; perform fine-grained analysis of arrival patterns and token-level behaviors by model architecture, scale, and intent; then build a workload generator that composes model-aware workload mixtures for benchmarking.
AI.result: The analysis uncovers distinct fluctuation regimes and workload dynamics across different models, sizes, and task intents, and produces a configurable FineServe workload generator grounded in these real-world traces.
AI.conclusion: FineServe offers a realistic, fine-grained dataset and workload generator that better captures real multi-model LLM serving dynamics, providing a strong basis for evaluating routing, scheduling, and capacity planning in LLM serving platforms.

title: Hybrid LSTM-Graph Neural Framework for Robust Financial Fraud Detection and Adversarial Resilience
summary: Financial institutions face significant challenges in detecting sophisticated money laundering patterns, such as smurfing and layering, due to extreme data imbalance (0.13% fraud rate) and evolving adversarial evasion tactics. This paper proposes FraudShield AI, a hybrid framework that integrates Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks with hand-crafted Graph Topological Features to capture both temporal sequences and structural relational context. By engineering network-centric features including PageRank Centrality, In-Degree dynamics, and a custom Flow Ratio, the system shifts the detection paradigm from isolated transaction analysis to network-level forensics. A Focal Loss objective is used to address class imbalance, and a dynamic thresholding mechanism is introduced to improve resilience against low-value smurfing attacks. Experimental evaluation on the PaySim dataset shows that the proposed hybrid model substantially outperforms Logistic Regression and XGBoost baselines in Precision, Recall, and F1-Score, particularly on hard-to-detect micro-transaction fraud patterns. An ablation study confirms the complementary contribution of both the temporal and topological components.

AI.tldr: Hybrid LSTM + graph-feature model (FraudShield AI) improves detection of rare, complex money laundering patterns by combining temporal and network-level transaction analysis with imbalance-aware training.
AI.motivation: Traditional AML systems struggle with (1) extreme class imbalance (only 0.13% of transactions are fraudulent), (2) sophisticated patterns like smurfing and layering that appear only when transactions are viewed as a network over time, and (3) adversarial behaviors that exploit low-value micro-transactions to evade fixed-threshold rules and pointwise models.
AI.method: Propose FraudShield AI, a hybrid architecture that (a) uses LSTM networks to model temporal sequences of transactions and (b) augments them with engineered graph topological features (e.g., PageRank centrality, in-degree dynamics, custom flow ratio) derived from the transaction network. They employ Focal Loss to mitigate class imbalance and introduce a dynamic thresholding scheme to better detect low-value smurfing; they compare against Logistic Regression and XGBoost and run ablation to isolate temporal vs topological contributions.
AI.result: On the PaySim dataset, FraudShield AI significantly outperforms Logistic Regression and XGBoost baselines on Precision, Recall, and F1, with particularly large gains on micro-transaction fraud cases that are typically hard to detect. Ablation experiments show that removing either the temporal (LSTM) or topological (graph features) component degrades performance, indicating both are important.
AI.conclusion: Combining sequence modeling (LSTM) with explicit graph topological features and imbalance-aware training provides a more effective and robust approach to anti-money laundering detection, especially for subtle smurfing and layering schemes. Network-level, temporally informed analysis is preferable to isolated transaction scoring in highly imbalanced, adversarial financial fraud settings.

title: OpenEvoShield: Dual Non-Stationary Continual Defense for Open-World Multi-Agent System Attacks
summary: LLM-based multi-agent systems (LLM-MAS) are increasingly deployed in safety-critical applications, where adversaries inject malicious instructions through inter-agent communication to propagate harmful behaviors. Unlike static threats, these attacks are doubly dynamic: adversaries refine injection strategies against deployed defenses while normal-agent behavior drifts with system expansion. Existing defenses treat deployment as a closed-world problem and degrade rapidly once either distribution shifts beyond training coverage. We propose OpenEvoShield, a co-evolutionary continual defense framework for LLM-MAS. An asymmetric rate controller (M1) decouples fast attack-side and slow normal-side learning rates from dual drift signals. A normal-boundary updater (M2) maintains a dynamic behavioral boundary at the slow rate, while an EWC-regularized policy ensemble (M3) fast-adapts without catastrophic forgetting. An energy-based multi-granularity detector (M4) fuses node-, subgraph-, and graph-level evidence to classify novel attacks as out-of-distribution. Experiments over 100 deployment rounds across five benchmarks and four MAS topologies show that OpenEvoShield outperforms static and continual baselines, detecting most previously unseen attacks while keeping false positive rates low.

AI.tldr: OpenEvoShield is a continual, co-evolutionary defense framework that detects evolving prompt-injection attacks in LLM-based multi-agent systems under distribution shift.
AI.motivation: LLM-based multi-agent systems are being used in safety-critical settings and communicate extensively. Adversaries can inject malicious instructions via these communication channels. Both attack strategies and normal behaviors evolve over time, causing distribution shifts that break static, closed-world defenses. There is a need for a defense framework that can adapt continuously to doubly dynamic conditions—changing attacks and drifting normal-agent behavior—without suffering catastrophic forgetting or high false positives.
AI.method: The authors introduce OpenEvoShield, a co-evolutionary continual defense framework. It has four main components: (M1) an asymmetric rate controller that decouples fast learning for the attack side from slower learning for normal behavior, guided by two drift signals; (M2) a normal-boundary updater that maintains and slowly updates a behavioral boundary describing normal agent behavior; (M3) an EWC-regularized policy ensemble that allows fast adaptation to new attacks while mitigating catastrophic forgetting of past knowledge; and (M4) an energy-based multi-granularity detector that combines node-, subgraph-, and graph-level anomaly evidence to flag novel attacks as out-of-distribution. The system is evaluated over many deployment rounds across different benchmarks and MAS topologies.
AI.result: Across 100 simulated deployment rounds on five benchmarks and four multi-agent system topologies, OpenEvoShield consistently outperforms both static and standard continual learning baselines. It detects the majority of previously unseen prompt-injection style attacks and maintains low false positive rates even as both attack distributions and normal behaviors drift over time.
AI.conclusion: Co-evolutionary, rate-asymmetric continual defenses can effectively protect LLM-based multi-agent systems against evolving, open-world attack distributions. By separating fast and slow adaptation, maintaining a dynamic normal-behavior boundary, using EWC-regularized ensembles to avoid catastrophic forgetting, and leveraging multi-granularity energy-based detection, OpenEvoShield retains robustness under distribution shift better than prior static or naive continual approaches.

title: Benchmarking Confidential GPU Inference on NVIDIA H100 under Intel TDX
summary: Confidential computing is becoming a practical deployment requirement for AI inference workloads that process sensitive inputs or protect proprietary model assets. However, the performance cost of enabling confidential execution for GPU-accelerated large language model serving remains workload dependent and operationally important. This paper presents a benchmark study comparing standard non-confidential execution with confidential computing mode on a single NVIDIA H100 80GB GPU hosted in an Intel TDX confidential instance. The evaluation uses two representative language models, Mistral-7B v0.1 and Qwen3-30B-A3B, and measures time to first token, end-to-end request latency, per-request token generation throughput, global token throughput, and closed-loop request throughput under increasing concurrency. In fixed request-rate experiments, confidential mode increases average TTFT by 21.8% for Mistral-7B and 27.8% for Qwen3-30B-A3B, while global token throughput drops by 17.7% and 21.1%, respectively. In closed-loop concurrency experiments, throughput gaps remain in the 11.5-20.2% range, but the larger model reaches its saturation knee earlier under confidential mode. The results suggest that confidential GPU inference can retain usable throughput under load, but capacity planning must account for both the steady throughput penalty and the earlier saturation behavior observed for larger models.

AI.tldr: Benchmark study of NVIDIA H100 confidential computing overhead for LLM inference, showing ~20–30% higher latency and ~15–20% lower throughput but still usable performance under load.
AI.motivation: As confidential computing becomes required for AI workloads involving sensitive data or proprietary models, operators need quantitative understanding of the performance overhead when running GPU-accelerated LLM inference in confidential mode. Existing claims are qualitative or CPU-focused, so a focused, GPU-based benchmark on real hardware is needed for capacity planning and system design.
AI.method: Run controlled benchmarks on a single NVIDIA H100 80GB GPU within an Intel TDX confidential instance, comparing standard (non-confidential) vs confidential execution. Use two LLMs (Mistral-7B v0.1 and Qwen3-30B-A3B) and measure a range of latency and throughput metrics—time to first token, end-to-end latency, per-request token throughput, global token throughput, and closed-loop request throughput—under both fixed request-rate and closed-loop concurrency scenarios.
AI.result: In fixed request-rate experiments, confidential mode increases average time to first token by 21.8% (Mistral-7B) and 27.8% (Qwen3-30B-A3B), and reduces global token throughput by 17.7% and 21.1%, respectively. In closed-loop concurrency experiments, throughput gaps between confidential and non-confidential modes stay in the 11.5–20.2% range, and the larger Qwen3-30B-A3B model hits its saturation knee sooner when running in confidential mode.
AI.conclusion: Confidential GPU inference on NVIDIA H100 with Intel TDX imposes a consistent but bounded performance penalty—roughly 20–30% higher latency and 15–20% lower throughput—yet maintains usable throughput at scale. However, capacity planning and provisioning must explicitly account for this steady overhead and for earlier saturation, especially for larger models like Qwen3-30B-A3B, when deploying confidential AI inference services.

title: FormulaSPIN: Self-Play Fine-Tuning for Natural Language to Spreadsheet Formula Generation
comment: 15 pages,7 figures, 14 tables. Accepted to ACL 2026 Main Conference Oral
summary: Spreadsheet applications are used by hundreds of millions worldwide, yet writing formulas remains a significant barrier. Existing approaches rely on static supervised data, which quickly saturates on limited annotations. In this paper, we introduce FORMULASPIN, a self-play framework that breaks the ceiling of supervised fine-tuning by enabling iterative self-improvement without any additional data. Vanilla SPIN fails on this task: it uniformly penalizes every non-matching output, so execution-equivalent alternatives are punished as negatives in one example while serving as ground truth in another, producing contradictory gradients. Our framework resolves this by exploiting formula generation's unique advantage: binary executability provides implicit supervision that separates semantic errors from valid stylistic variants. We frame training as a two-player game in which the main player learns to prefer ground-truth formulas over those from its previous version, while execution feedback sorts outputs into distinct granularities-enabling an adaptive curriculum that shifts from semantic correctness to stylistic refinement. To further increase accuracy, we incorporate ExecVote, a semantic-level voting mechanism that naturally handles multiple valid formulations. Experiments on multiple benchmarks demonstrate that FORMULASPIN achieves state-of-the-art performance, with 74.9% exact match and 87.1% execution accuracy on NL2FORMULA, matching models trained with additional preference annotations while outperforming both traditional SFT and frontier proprietary models. These findings underscore self-play's potential to tackle scarce data tasks and open the door to extending it beyond executable domains.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes FORMULASPIN, a self-play training framework that improves natural-language-to-spreadsheet-formula generation without requiring new labeled data, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy by using execution feedback and preference-style training over model self-outputs.
AI.motivation: Spreadsheet formulas are hard for many users to write, and current NL-to-formula models depend on limited supervised datasets, which quickly saturate and are expensive to expand. Existing self-play (SPIN) methods also struggle because they treat all non-exact matches as equally wrong, even when alternative formulas are execution-equivalent, leading to conflicting training signals. The authors aim to break past this supervised data ceiling and make better use of the executable nature of formulas to improve models without new human annotations.
AI.method: They introduce FORMULASPIN, a self-play framework specialized for executable formula generation. Training is formulated as a two-player game: a main model is trained to prefer ground-truth formulas and to outperform its previous version. Outputs are run and checked for executability and execution equivalence, which implicitly separates semantic errors from stylistic variants. This enables an adaptive curriculum that first emphasizes semantic correctness and later focuses on stylistic refinement. They also add ExecVote, a semantic-level voting mechanism that aggregates multiple execution-equivalent candidate formulas to handle the existence of many valid formulations rather than penalizing them as negatives.
AI.result: On several NL-to-formula benchmarks, FORMULASPIN attains state-of-the-art results; on the NL2FORMULA benchmark, it reaches 74.9% exact match and 87.1% execution accuracy. Its performance matches models trained using extra human preference annotations and surpasses both standard supervised fine-tuning baselines and powerful proprietary models, all without requiring additional labeled data.
AI.conclusion: FORMULASPIN shows that self-play guided by execution feedback can substantially advance NL-to-formula generation beyond the limits of static supervised training. By distinguishing semantic errors from stylistic variations and leveraging a preference-style game between model generations, it achieves strong performance without extra annotations. The work highlights self-play as a promising direction for other scarce-data problems and suggests its applicability may extend beyond domains with strictly executable outputs.

title: Information Discernment in Large Language Models
summary: LLMs are increasingly used with external knowledge sources like the internet. Do they weigh information appropriately -- updating more for reliable sources (source discernment) and more when claims bring priors closer to the truth (truth discernment)? We formalize this as information discernment and introduce Learn2Discern (L2D), an experimental framework and benchmark grounded in three normative axioms with interpretable metrics. To establish external validity, a pre-registered, quota-matched user study (n=299) confirms that real LLM users endorse all three axioms and report that violations reduce their trust and usage intent. Across 13 models and nearly 670K trials, we find consistent failures across both dimensions: models perform near chance on source and truth discernment, rely on source popularity twice as much as source reliability, and update roughly equally whether a claim improves or worsens their position relative to the ground truth. Models integrate external knowledge most effectively on datasets where their priors are already the most accurate. Newer and larger models improve truth discernment but not source discernment, a blind spot that model complexity does not address. We identify simple inference-time interventions that improve both forms of discernment. We release our dataset and survey as a testbed for a core alignment property that scales in importance as LLMs replace traditional search.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Learn2Discern (L2D), a framework and benchmark to evaluate how well LLMs appropriately update their beliefs from external knowledge sources, and finds that current models largely fail to discern source reliability and truth-improving information, while proposing simple interventions that help.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly used with tools like web search and external databases, it becomes critical that they not only ingest information but also weigh it properly—updating more from reliable sources and from claims that move their beliefs closer to the truth. Existing evaluations mostly test factual accuracy or tool usage, not whether models exhibit principled information updating behavior aligned with user expectations and normative rationality. The authors aim to formalize and empirically test this "information discernment" capability, which is central for safe deployment as LLMs start to replace traditional search engines.
AI.method: The authors formalize information discernment via three normative axioms that distinguish (1) source discernment—updating more from more reliable sources—and (2) truth discernment—updating more when evidence moves model beliefs closer to ground truth. They design Learn2Discern (L2D), an experimental framework and benchmark with interpretable metrics implementing these axioms. First, they run a pre-registered, quota-matched human user study (n=299) to check that real LLM users endorse the axioms and care about them for trust and usage decisions. Then, they evaluate 13 different LLMs across nearly 670K trials, systematically varying source reliability, popularity, and the direction/magnitude of claim evidence relative to models’ priors and the true answers. They also test simple inference-time prompting or procedure tweaks as interventions to see if discernment can be improved without retraining.
AI.result: The user study shows that participants broadly endorse the three discernment axioms, and report that violations reduce their trust and willingness to use LLMs. In large-scale experiments across 13 models, the authors find that models largely fail at both source and truth discernment, performing near chance on the corresponding metrics. Models over-weight source popularity relative to reliability, using popularity about twice as strongly as actual reliability signals. With respect to truth, models update their beliefs about equally whether new information makes them more or less accurate relative to ground truth, indicating a lack of selective updating toward truth. Models also integrate external knowledge best on datasets where their initial priors are already comparatively accurate, limiting gains where they are most needed. Newer and larger models exhibit improved truth discernment but not improved source discernment. However, the authors identify simple inference-time interventions that measurably improve both dimensions of discernment.
AI.conclusion: Information discernment—updating more from reliable sources and truth-improving evidence—is both normatively desirable and important to users, yet current LLMs perform poorly on it, particularly on source discernment. Model scale and recency yield some gains for truth discernment but leave a systematic blind spot in handling source reliability and over-reliance on popularity cues. Because LLMs leverage external knowledge most where they already have good priors, they are least helpful in domains where they are initially weak. The Learn2Discern (L2D) framework, dataset, and survey provide a principled testbed for this core alignment property. The authors’ inference-time interventions show that discernment can be improved without architectural changes, suggesting practical paths forward as LLMs increasingly supplant traditional search and act as primary gateways to information.

title: Stateful Guardrails for Multi-Turn LLM Systems: A Conversational Risk Accumulation Framework
summary: Most safety guardrails for large language models (LLMs) evaluate each prompt-response pair in isolation, which misses failures that arise only over a dialogue as benign turns compose into harm. We term this Conversational Risk Accumulation (CRA): gradual intent drift, fragmented assembly of prohibited instructions, and sensitivity build-up from repeated disclosures. We propose a session-layer CRA Framework that tracks three trajectory signals: semantic drift from a session anchor, a sensitivity-weighted information accumulation graph over extracted entities, and a compliance-gradient signal capturing increasing willingness to comply. For scoring, we provide (i) an unsupervised convex fusion for attribution and ablations, and (ii) CRA-Net DA, a compact learned trajectory model trained with family-adversarial objectives to reduce length and topic-coverage confounds. To benchmark CRA, we release CRA-Bench v0.1 (1,200 eight-turn sessions across three threat families with topic-matched benign twins), CRA-Bench v0.2 (LLM-paraphrased variants to reduce template artifacts), and an extended 5-family set (2,000 sessions adding persona priming and context stuffing). We introduce a trajectory-native evaluation protocol with session-level splits, mixed-set threshold calibration, Trajectory AUROC, turns-to-detection, calibrated false-positive metrics, bootstrap confidence intervals, leave-one-family-out diagnostic stress tests, and synthetic-to-human transfer checks. Claims focus on within-distribution session scoring on CRA-Bench and human-transfer subsets.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a framework to detect safety risks that accumulate over multi-turn conversations with LLMs, rather than evaluating each turn independently.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM safety guardrails often miss harms that only emerge gradually over a dialogue, such as intent drift or the stepwise construction of harmful instructions, so a method is needed to detect these conversationally accumulated risks.
AI.method: They define Conversational Risk Accumulation (CRA) and propose a session-layer CRA framework that tracks three trajectory-level signals—semantic drift from an initial anchor, sensitivity-weighted information accumulation over entities, and a compliance-gradient signal—then score them via an unsupervised convex fusion approach and a learned trajectory model (CRA-Net DA) trained with family-adversarial objectives. They also build CRA-specific benchmarks (CRA-Bench v0.1, v0.2, and an extended 5-family set) and a trajectory-native evaluation protocol including specialized metrics and stress tests.
AI.result: They construct several CRA-Bench datasets with thousands of multi-turn sessions across multiple threat families and show that their framework and CRA-Net DA can effectively score conversational risk within these benchmarks using trajectory-level metrics such as Trajectory AUROC, turns-to-detection, and calibrated false-positive rates, including evidence of transfer from synthetic to human data.
AI.conclusion: Conversation-level, trajectory-aware safety assessments are more capable of detecting gradual or composite harms than turn-by-turn checks, and the proposed CRA framework, CRA-Net DA model, and CRA-Bench benchmarks provide a foundation and protocol for evaluating and improving such systems within their tested distribution.

title: NEXUS: Structured Runtime Safety for Tool-Using LLM Agents
summary: Tool-using LLM agents increasingly execute high-impact actions, making runtime safety monitoring essential. We present NEXUS (Neural EXecution Utility and Safety), a structured-plan safety monitor that applies a formal intervention policy to select among four actions: allow, block, request confirmation, or request revision. NEXUS combines deterministic safety rules, argument-level inspection, and a calibrated logistic-regression risk score for graded escalation. On a 128-instance synthetic benchmark, NEXUS achieves an F1 score of 0.949 and a 4-class intervention accuracy of 0.6406, outperforming rule-only intervention selection by 27.3 percentage points. It also improves over rule-only on R-Judge (F1 = 0.861 vs. 0.849), matches rule-only on AgentHarm due to threat-model limits, and achieves 0% ASR at 99% control allow on IPI. On the rule-blind NEXUS-Stress benchmark, NEXUS reaches an F1 score of 0.881, highlighting the difficulty of fine-grained intervention routing. With 0.205 ms median latency, NEXUS adds under 0.1% overhead to typical agent loops. Code, benchmarks, and the calibrated risk scorer are publicly released.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces NEXUS, a low-latency safety monitor for tool-using LLM agents that decides whether to allow, block, or escalate actions using a mix of rules and a learned risk scorer, achieving strong performance and minimal overhead.
AI.motivation: As LLM agents increasingly perform high-impact real-world actions through tools and APIs, there is a need for reliable runtime safety monitors that can make fine-grained intervention decisions (not just allow/block) while remaining fast and accurate. Existing rule-based approaches are limited in flexibility and graded risk assessment, and learned approaches may be uncalibrated or opaque. The paper aims to bridge this gap.
AI.method: The authors propose NEXUS (Neural EXecution Utility and Safety), a structured-plan safety monitor that observes the agent’s tool-use plan and arguments and applies a formal intervention policy. NEXUS combines: (1) deterministic safety rules, (2) argument-level inspection of tool calls, and (3) a calibrated logistic-regression risk score to estimate the likelihood that a given action is unsafe. Based on these, it routes each action into one of four intervention types: allow, block, request user confirmation, or request plan revision. They evaluate NEXUS on multiple benchmarks (a 128-instance synthetic benchmark, R-Judge, AgentHarm, IPI, and the rule-blind NEXUS-Stress benchmark) and measure F1, multi-class intervention accuracy, attack success rate, and latency.
AI.result: On the 128-instance synthetic benchmark, NEXUS achieves an F1 score of 0.949 and 4-class intervention accuracy of 0.6406, beating a rule-only baseline by 27.3 percentage points on intervention selection. On R-Judge, NEXUS slightly improves over rule-only (F1 0.861 vs 0.849). On AgentHarm, it matches rule-only, limited by the underlying threat model. On IPI, it achieves 0% attack success rate at 99% allow rate for safe control tasks. On the rule-blind NEXUS-Stress benchmark, it obtains an F1 of 0.881, indicating remaining difficulty in nuanced intervention routing. Latency is very low (0.205 ms median), adding less than 0.1% overhead to typical agent control loops. The authors also release code, benchmarks, and the calibrated risk model.
AI.conclusion: NEXUS demonstrates that combining deterministic rules with a calibrated, lightweight risk model enables fast, fine-grained, and effective runtime safety monitoring for tool-using LLM agents. It substantially improves intervention routing over rule-only baselines on several benchmarks while maintaining negligible latency overhead, though nuanced multi-class decisions remain challenging on stress tests. The work provides practical infrastructure (code, benchmarks, risk scorer) to support safer deployment and further research on agent safety monitoring.

title: When Reasoning Narrows the Move: Diversity Collapse in LLM Game Play
summary: Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) is widely used to adapt large language models to downstream tasks, but its effect on behavioral diversity in sequential decision-making remains under-explored. We study this question in a controlled suite of deterministic board games based on tic-tac-toe variants, where optimal actions are exactly computable and diversity can be measured directly. Across state-level evaluation, arena gameplay, and training trajectories, we find that reasoning-mode generation frequently suppresses action diversity without uniformly improving action accuracy. Furthermore, standard SFT improves accuracy but often induces premature diversity collapse, which exceeds what is minimally required by the accuracy-diversity tradeoff. We then show that action augmentation, which trains on all optimal actions per state rather than a single demonstrated action, would partially mitigates this effect. Our results identify narrow-support imitation as a source of policy collapse in LLM decision-making and suggest that preserving action support during SFT is important for maintaining exploratory behavior.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how supervised fine-tuning (SFT) of large language models on decision-making tasks affects the diversity of their actions, finding that SFT and certain reasoning modes often collapse behavioral diversity more than necessary, and proposing action augmentation to better preserve diverse optimal behaviors.
AI.motivation: While supervised fine-tuning is standard for adapting LLMs to tasks, its impact on the diversity of behaviors in sequential decision-making—especially the tendency toward overly deterministic, collapsed policies—has not been systematically analyzed. Understanding this is important because decision-making often benefits from exploration and multiple equally optimal actions, and current SFT practices may unintentionally suppress such diversity.
AI.method: The authors construct a suite of deterministic tic-tac-toe–like board games where the full set of optimal actions for each state is exactly computable. They compare LLM behavior in different modes (e.g., reasoning vs non-reasoning) and under different training setups, including standard SFT and a proposed action-augmentation variant that trains on all optimal actions per state. They measure both action accuracy (optimality) and action diversity at the state level, during gameplay in arenas, and along training trajectories.
AI.result: They observe that prompting the model to use explicit reasoning often reduces action diversity without consistently increasing action accuracy. Standard SFT increases accuracy but also causes premature collapse of action diversity, going beyond what is theoretically necessary for the tradeoff between accuracy and diversity. When they apply action augmentation—training on the full set of optimal actions instead of a single demonstration—the collapse of diversity is partially alleviated, preserving more exploratory behavior while maintaining good performance.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that narrow-support imitation—training on only one demonstrated action per state—is a significant driver of policy collapse in LLM-based decision-making. To maintain useful exploration and behavioral diversity, SFT procedures should preserve the full support of optimal actions where possible, for example via action augmentation. This has implications for how LLMs are trained for planning and control tasks that benefit from diverse yet high-quality decision policies.

title: Stochastic Primal-Dual Decoding for Multiobjective Generative Recommender Systems
summary:
Recent advances in recommender systems (RS) have shown substantial performance gains through generative modelling. In practice, recommendation often involves constructing slates -- ordered lists of items -- that must satisfy multiple objectives beyond relevance, such as constraints defined over item attributes or fairness constraints. Existing multiobjective approaches either rely on post-processing techniques designed for non-generative settings, or incorporate auxiliary objectives directly into model training. The former does not explicitly account for the sequential nature of generative RS, while the latter is often impractical in large-scale systems.
  We propose a lightweight, inference-time decoding layer that augments autoregressive generative RS to support multiobjective slate generation without modifying or retraining the underlying model. We formulate decoding as an online constrained optimisation problem, where items are selected sequentially, and trade-offs between relevance and auxiliary objectives are adjusted dynamically based on the remaining constraint slack, i.e., how much of each objective remains to be satisfied. This is implemented via a stochastic primal-dual approximation scheme that balances relevance and auxiliary objectives during generation.
  We provide theoretical guarantees on constraint violation and regret, and evaluate the proposed approach through extensive offline experiments and a large-scale online A/B experiment in a real-world recommender system. Our results show consistent improvements in multiobjective trade-offs, including a +1.8\% gain in the auxiliary objectives achieved at zero cost to user satisfaction.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a lightweight, inference-time decoding layer for generative recommender systems to construct multiobjective slates (ordered lists of items) that respect constraints (e.g., fairness, attribute constraints) without retraining the underlying model, achieving better auxiliary objectives at no loss of user satisfaction.
AI.motivation: Generative recommender systems have improved relevance performance, but real-world recommendation requires slates that satisfy multiple, sometimes competing, objectives such as fairness or business rules. Existing solutions either post-process recommendations (ignoring the sequential, generative process) or bake auxiliary objectives into model training (costly and impractical at scale). There is a need for a method that can handle multiobjective constraints during slate generation itself, using existing generative models without retraining.
AI.method: The authors add an inference-time decoding layer on top of an autoregressive generative recommender system. They cast slate construction as an online constrained optimization problem, where items are selected one by one. The decoding layer dynamically adjusts trade-offs between relevance and auxiliary objectives based on remaining constraint slack. They implement this via a stochastic primal-dual approximation scheme that updates dual variables during generation to balance relevance scores with constraint satisfaction.
AI.result: They derive theoretical guarantees on constraint violation and regret for the proposed online constrained decoding scheme. Empirically, they validate the method through extensive offline experiments and a large-scale online A/B test in a production recommender system, demonstrating consistent improvements in multiobjective trade-offs. Notably, they report a 1.8% improvement in auxiliary objectives while maintaining user satisfaction metrics (no observed degradation).
AI.conclusion: The proposed inference-time decoding layer enables existing autoregressive generative recommender systems to produce multiobjective-constrained slates without retraining. By formulating decoding as online constrained optimization and using a stochastic primal-dual scheme, it achieves better auxiliary objective performance with theoretical guarantees and demonstrated real-world gains, all while preserving user satisfaction.

title: On the Computational Complexity of Structural Generalization
summary: Structural generalization has been measured repeatedly by several benchmarks, yet it has never been formally defined. We give a definition that translates the two premises (compositional structure and unbounded generalization) into mathematical language. The definition itself is neutral: a compiler that hard-codes the rules satisfies it just as well. But structural generalization becomes a scientific question only insofar as the capacity can autonomously emerge from finite data. This question pits the computational lower bound $\mathrm{NC}^1$ against the learnable ceiling $\mathrm{TC}^0$ of pure Transformers. Under a Montagovian instantiation, each compositional rule splits into two projections: a syntactic face ($F_γ$) and a semantic face ($G_γ$). Tree evaluation on the $G_γ$ side is an instantiation of BFVP, which is $\mathrm{NC}^1$-complete (Buss, 1987). A pure Transformer must learn both faces at once, but Kraus et al. (2026) prove that its learnable class $\subseteq \mathrm{TC}^0$. Under the standard assumption $\mathrm{TC}^0 \neq \mathrm{NC}^1$, a pure Transformer cannot learn structural generalization. Neuro-symbolic systems achieve the best benchmark scores precisely because they inject $G_γ$, sidestepping the genuinely hard half. Benchmark scores cannot distinguish "learned" from "given." This is what this paper sets out to make clear.

AI.tldr: The paper formally defines structural generalization and argues that pure Transformers cannot autonomously learn it, due to complexity-theoretic limits.
AI.motivation: Although many benchmarks claim to test structural generalization, the notion has lacked a precise mathematical definition, and current high benchmark scores may conflate genuinely learned abilities with hard-coded structure. The authors aim to clarify what structural generalization is and whether current architectures can, in principle, learn it from finite data.
AI.method: They formalize structural generalization by translating two core premises—compositional structure and unbounded generalization—into a mathematical framework. Using a Montagovian semantics, each compositional rule is decomposed into a syntactic component F_γ and a semantic component G_γ. They then analyze the computational complexity of learning these components, linking tree evaluation of G_γ to the BFVP problem, known to be NC^1-complete, and combining this with known results that pure Transformers’ learnable functions are contained in TC^0.
AI.result: They identify a tension between the NC^1 complexity of semantic tree evaluation (via BFVP) and the TC^0 upper bound on what pure Transformers can learn. This implies, under standard complexity assumptions, that pure Transformers cannot learn the full structural generalization capacity from data alone. They also observe that neuro-symbolic systems perform well on benchmarks because they explicitly supply G_γ, effectively bypassing the hard semantic half of the problem.
AI.conclusion: Under the widely believed separation TC^0 ≠ NC^1, pure Transformers cannot autonomously acquire structural generalization as formally defined; success on benchmarks may thus arise from injected structure rather than learned capacity. Neuro-symbolic models succeed by hard-coding part of the required computation, and benchmark results alone cannot tell whether structural generalization has truly been learned or externally provided.

title: LISA: Linear-Indexed Sparse Attention for Efficient Long-Context Reasoning
summary: Recent advances in long chain-of-thought reasoning models such as DeepSeek-R1 have led to increasingly longer inference context lengths under the test-time scaling paradigm. However, the O(n^2) computational complexity of standard self-attention causes inference costs to grow sharply with long sequences, limiting the deployment of long-CoT reasoning in production settings. To address this, we propose LISA (Linear-Indexed Sparse Attention), a plug-and-play attention replacement module that requires no pretraining from scratch. LISA integrates two lightweight components in parallel within the original model: (1) a Linear Attention module that provides long-range memory with O(n) time complexity; (2) a Lightning Indexer that selects the top-M important tokens from the full context to feed into a Sparse Self-Attention. The two branches are fused via a gating mechanism, reducing inference complexity from O(n^2) to O(nM) (M << n) for generating n tokens. We design a two-stage training pipeline: Stage 1 initializes the model by integrating the linear attention to capture long-range dependencies, complemented by a sliding-window attention mechanism that is optimized via knowledge distillation to approximate the full self-attention distribution of a frozen teacher model. In Stage 2, we further introduce the Indexer to replace the static sliding-window mechanism, enabling dynamic token selection from broader contexts. The Indexer is trained using a novel per-head KL divergence loss, which aligns its selection behavior with the attention patterns of the teacher model. Experiments on DeepSeek-distilled-Qwen models demonstrate that LISA achieves a 50% inference speedup under 16K-token context, while improving average performance by 5.6% on reasoning benchmarks including AIME and MATH-500.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces LISA, a plug-and-play attention module that reduces the cost of long chain-of-thought reasoning by combining linear attention with a learned sparse token indexer, achieving faster inference and better reasoning accuracy on long contexts.
AI.motivation: Long chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning requires handling very long contexts, but standard self-attention has O(n^2) complexity, making inference prohibitively expensive at production scale. Existing efficient attention variants often need pretraining from scratch or sacrifice performance. The authors want a method that (1) works as a drop-in replacement for attention in existing long-CoT models, (2) scales near-linearly with context length, and (3) preserves or improves reasoning quality.
AI.method: LISA (Linear-Indexed Sparse Attention) replaces standard attention with two parallel components: (1) a Linear Attention branch providing global, long-range memory in O(n) time; (2) a Lightning Indexer that selects the top-M important tokens from the entire context and feeds them to a Sparse Self-Attention branch. A gating mechanism fuses the outputs of the linear and sparse branches. The training is done in two stages using a frozen teacher model: Stage 1 integrates linear attention plus a sliding-window attention, optimized with knowledge distillation to approximate the teacher’s full attention. Stage 2 replaces the static window with the learned Indexer, which is trained via a per-head KL divergence loss to mimic the teacher’s attention heads when selecting tokens.
AI.result: On DeepSeek-distilled-Qwen models with 16K-token contexts, LISA yields about 50% inference speedup compared with standard self-attention, while improving average performance by 5.6% on long-context reasoning benchmarks such as AIME and MATH-500.
AI.conclusion: LISA offers a practical, plug-and-play way to scale long-CoT reasoning by reducing the quadratic cost of self-attention to near-linear while retaining or improving performance. By combining linear attention with a learned sparse token indexer guided by a teacher model, it enables efficient deployment of long-context reasoning models without retraining from scratch.

title: Scaling Laws for Hypernetwork-Based Knowledge Injection in Large Language Models
comment: Preprint, 22 pages, 14 figures. Dataset collection available at https://huggingface.co/collections/nace-ai/hypernetwork-datasets
summary: Injecting factual knowledge into large language models (LLMs) reliably and at scale remains an open challenge. Hypernetworks provide a promising solution to large-scale knowledge injection. Although hypernetworks are typically applied for test-time adaptation, we explore their use in train-time knowledge injection, where, given a large corpus of facts, we train a hypernetwork to generate a fixed LoRA adapter that, when inserted into the target model, enable the model to answer questions about those facts. In this work, we investigate whether hypernetworks can be used to perform train-time knowledge injection and how this ability varies with scale. The scaling behavior of hypernetworks remains largely unstudied. Our design decouples the hypernetwork's injection capacity from the target model's general capability, enabling, for the first time, a rigorous study of scaling laws for hypernetwork architectures. We characterize how loss, reasoning accuracy, and out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization vary with hypernetwork depth, width, and target network size. We construct a large-scale dataset, called MegaWikiQA, containing tens of millions of multi-hop question-answer examples across 39 domains constructed from examples in Wikidata5M. Our results reveal: (i) hypernetwork-based injection exhibits broadly predictive power law scaling along all architecture axes; and (ii) hypernetworks are capable of reliable OOD generalization at increasing scales, suggesting that hypernetwork provides a promising alternative to other train-time adaptation methods such as LoRA finetuning and full fine-tuning, exhibiting steeper scaling exponents in all OOD evaluations. Together, these results establish hypernetworks as a principled and scalable substrate for train-time adaptation, and provide the first empirically grounded scaling laws to guide hypernetworks for factual reasoning in large language models.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates using hypernetworks for injecting factual knowledge into LLMs at train time via LoRA adapters, and studies their scaling behavior.
AI.motivation: Reliably and efficiently injecting large amounts of factual knowledge into LLMs is challenging; existing methods like LoRA or full fine-tuning have limitations, and the scaling behavior of hypernetworks for this purpose is not well understood.
AI.method: They train a hypernetwork that, given a large corpus of facts, produces a fixed LoRA adapter for a target LLM. They design the setup so the hypernetwork’s capacity is decoupled from the base model’s capability, and systematically vary hypernetwork depth, width, and target model size. They introduce a large multi-hop QA dataset, MegaWikiQA, built from Wikidata5M, and evaluate loss, reasoning accuracy, and OOD generalization to fit scaling laws.
AI.result: Hypernetwork-based knowledge injection follows predictable power-law scaling across architectural axes and shows strong, improving out-of-distribution generalization as scale increases. It outperforms standard train-time adaptation methods like LoRA or full fine-tuning in OOD settings, with steeper scaling exponents.
AI.conclusion: Hypernetworks are an effective and scalable method for injecting factual knowledge into LLMs at train time, with well-behaved scaling laws and strong OOD generalization, making them a principled alternative to conventional adaptation techniques for factual reasoning tasks.

title: Profile-Graph Memory for LLM Agents: Implicit Cross-Entity Traversal through Narrative Profiles
comment: 11 pages, 2 figures, 7 tables. Code and MemHop benchmark: https://github.com/ShengtongZhu/ProGraph
summary: Long-term memory is essential for LLM agents that interact across sessions, yet current memory benchmarks primarily evaluate single-hop recall, leaving multi-hop association largely unmeasured. We make three contributions. First, we introduce MemHop, a multi-hop memory benchmark of 1,000 questions at hop depths 1-5 across 10 social-network scenarios, with per-hop evidence annotations. Second, we present Profile-Graph Memory (ProGraph), a two-layer memory architecture combining (i) profile expansion -- substring-matched traversal of entity names that naturally appear in LLM-written profile narratives, a minimal alternative to explicit knowledge-graph construction -- and (ii) compression residuals -- exact dates, quantities, and named items co-extracted with each profile update at zero extra API cost. Third, a full-grid ablation shows cross-benchmark mechanism specialization: profile expansion drives multi-hop reasoning (-22.6pp on MemHop when removed) while compression residuals drive precision recall (-8.6pp on LoCoMo when not co-extracted), with cross-effects under 3pp within a single architecture. ProGraph averages 80.1% on MemHop (matching the FullContext reference) and 78.4% on LoCoMo (exceeding FullContext by 11.3pp), outperforming Mem0, A-Mem, HippoRAG, and RAG on both. We release MemHop, ProGraph, and baseline implementations.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MemHop, a multi-hop long-term memory benchmark for LLM agents, and ProGraph, a profile-graph memory architecture that improves both multi-hop reasoning and precise recall, outperforming existing memory systems on MemHop and LoCoMo.
AI.motivation: Existing long-term memory benchmarks and systems for LLM agents largely focus on single-hop recall within a single session, failing to capture the more realistic, multi-hop associative reasoning needed for cross-session interactions. There is also a need for practical memory architectures that handle both complex reasoning over user profiles and precise recall of specific facts without incurring high operational costs.
AI.method: The authors (1) design MemHop, a benchmark of 1,000 questions spanning 1–5 hops across 10 social-network scenarios, each annotated with per-hop supporting evidence; and (2) propose Profile-Graph Memory (ProGraph), a two-layer memory system. The first layer, profile expansion, uses substring-matched traversal over entity names that appear naturally in LLM-generated profile narratives to approximate a knowledge graph without explicit graph construction. The second layer, compression residuals, stores exact dates, quantities, and named entities co-extracted whenever a profile is updated, at no extra API calls. They perform a full ablation grid to disentangle the effects of the two mechanisms across multiple benchmarks.
AI.result: ProGraph achieves an average of 80.1% accuracy on the new MemHop benchmark, matching a FullContext reference system, and 78.4% on the existing LoCoMo benchmark, exceeding the FullContext baseline by 11.3 percentage points. Ablation shows that removing profile expansion significantly harms multi-hop performance on MemHop (-22.6 pp), while omitting compression residuals substantially degrades precision recall on LoCoMo (-8.6 pp). Cross-effects between the two mechanisms within the same architecture are small (<3 pp). ProGraph outperforms prior memory baselines Mem0, A-Mem, HippoRAG, and standard RAG on both benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Multi-hop associative memory is a distinct and important capability for LLM agents that is not captured by traditional single-hop memory benchmarks. MemHop provides a targeted evaluation of this capability, and the proposed ProGraph architecture demonstrates that combining lightweight profile-based graph expansion with fact-level compression residuals can simultaneously support strong multi-hop reasoning and high-precision recall. The division of labor between the two mechanisms is empirically validated by ablations, and the system sets a new state of the art on both MemHop and LoCoMo, suggesting a promising direction for practical, scalable long-term memory in LLM agents.

title: Task Competence Is Not Instruction Following: Evaluating Instruction-Conflicting Behavior in Small Language Models
summary: Instruction tuning is meant to make language models follow user requests, yet it is unclear whether small models comply when an instruction conflicts with their usual task behavior. We study this across three tasks - multiple-choice question answering (MCQA), sentiment classification, and mathematical question answering - by pairing a standard instruction with a conflicting non-standard one (select an incorrect option, output the opposite sentiment, or return twice the answer). This cross-task design allows us to test whether resistance to conflicting instructions is tied to specific task characteristics or reflects a broader behavioral tendency. As all predictions are scored against the original ground truth, a model that ignores the non-standard instruction still appears accurate. Using standard accuracy, non-standard accuracy, and an Instruction-Following Failure Rate (IFFR), we evaluate instruction-tuned Qwen models across sizes. Both standard accuracy and instruction following generally improve with scale, although the pattern is not consistent across all tasks and datasets. Small models stay competent yet routinely ignore the non-standard instruction, while larger models show a clear gap between the two settings. These findings suggest that gains in task capability do not automatically provide reliable control over model behavior. Task competence and instruction following are therefore distinct abilities, and reporting only standard accuracy hides instruction-following failures.

AI.tldr: The paper studies whether instruction-tuned small language models truly follow conflicting instructions or default to task habits, showing that task accuracy can hide instruction-following failures.
AI.motivation: Instruction tuning aims to make models follow user requests, but it is unclear if models—especially smaller ones—obey instructions that conflict with their usual task behavior. The authors want to know if resistance to such conflicting instructions is task-specific or a general behavioral trait and whether standard accuracy metrics obscure these failures.
AI.method: They design paired instructions for three tasks—multiple-choice QA, sentiment classification, and math QA—where a standard instruction is accompanied by a conflicting non-standard one (e.g., choose the wrong option, output opposite sentiment, double the numeric answer). They evaluate various sizes of instruction-tuned Qwen models using three metrics: standard accuracy (against normal ground truth), non-standard accuracy (success under conflicting instructions, still evaluated on original ground truth), and an Instruction-Following Failure Rate (IFFR). Results across tasks and datasets are compared, focusing on how behavior changes with model scale.
AI.result: Both task performance (standard accuracy) and the tendency to follow instructions generally improve as models scale up, but the pattern is uneven across tasks and datasets. Small models remain reasonably accurate on tasks yet frequently ignore conflicting non-standard instructions. Larger models exhibit a clearer distinction in performance between standard and conflicting-instruction settings, revealing more evident trade-offs or behavioral shifts.
AI.conclusion: Task competence and instruction following are separable capabilities. Improvements in accuracy do not guarantee reliable obedience to user instructions, especially when instructions conflict with default task behavior. Relying only on standard accuracy can mask significant instruction-following failures, so evaluation should explicitly measure instruction adherence (e.g., via IFFR) in addition to task success.

title: Lifted Representation Hypothesis in Language Models
comment: 20 pages, 6 figures, ICML 2026 Workshop: Philosophy Meets Machine Learning
summary: Large language models (LLMs) often answer queries by mapping individual observations to more general rule-like structures. However, it remains unclear how these structures are stored, selected, and revised. To study this process, we propose thelifted representation hypothesis: LLMs update memory through shared latent structures rather than isolated instance-level facts. This view frames lifting as an efficient use of symmetry across instances, and shattering as the refinement of coarse lifted structures into more specific subtypes. We evaluate LLMs' lifting and shattering through controlled exception-learning experiments across in-context learning, LoRA, and full fine-tuning. We find that LLMs are vulnerable to shattering failures when data are governed by nested rules and exceptions, while lifting often occurs prematurely. These results highlight the need to study the relation between data and rule structures in LLMs.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes and tests the lifted representation hypothesis, which claims that LLMs store and update knowledge via shared latent rule-like structures (lifted representations) rather than separate facts, and studies when these structures are formed or broken (shattered).
AI.motivation: Although LLMs often behave as if they are using abstract rules, it is unclear how such rules are internally represented, updated, and revised, especially when faced with exceptions or nested rules. Understanding this is important for predicting LLM generalization, reliability, and failure modes when learning from new data or fine-tuning.
AI.method: The authors introduce the lifted representation hypothesis and formalize two processes: lifting (forming shared abstract structures across instances) and shattering (refining or breaking these structures into more specific subtypes when exceptions arise). They design controlled exception-learning experiments with nested rules and exceptions, and test LLMs under three learning regimes: in-context learning, LoRA-based adaptation, and full fine-tuning, measuring how well models form and revise rule structures.
AI.result: Across all learning regimes, LLMs often form lifted rule-like structures too aggressively (premature lifting), and struggle when data require nested rules with exceptions. In particular, they show shattering failures: the models fail to correctly refine or differentiate previously formed abstract rules when exceptions or subrules must be learned, leading to systematic errors on exceptions and subtypes.
AI.conclusion: LLMs do not simply store independent facts but tend to build and rely on shared latent rule structures, yet they have difficulty revising these structures when data contain nested rules and exceptions. This suggests that the interaction between data structure and internal rule representations is a key determinant of LLM behavior, motivating more targeted study of how models form, maintain, and refine abstract rules during training and adaptation.

title: Adaptive Capitulation: A Structural Failure Mode of LLM Responses in Vulnerability Contexts
summary: Large language models operating in emotionally sensitive contexts face a structural trilemma: when users in vulnerable states request information that may reinforce maladaptive attribution, current response architectures resolve the tension through protective restriction, uninflected facilitation, or unintegrated co-presence of both imperatives -- each preserving one objective at the cost of the other. Administering a three-turn escalating vulnerability vignette to three commercial LLMs (900 sessions across material, relational, and somatic status-proxy variants) and coding responses with two binary indices (VCC/VCI), we characterize a previously undocumented failure mode we term adaptive capitulation: the model validates the social injustice underlying the user's distress before pivoting to detailed facilitation of the very acquisition it nominally discouraged. We show that the trilemma is structural rather than incidental, and propose Minimal Reattributive Sufficiency (MRS), an architecture-neutral design principle that embeds a single reattributive cue within an otherwise validating response, preserving a pathway toward autonomous reattribution without contesting the user's stated goal.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies a structural safety–helpfulness trilemma in LLM responses to emotionally vulnerable users, documents a new failure mode called adaptive capitulation, and proposes a minimal design principle (MRS) that embeds a single reattributive cue within otherwise validating responses to preserve user autonomy while reducing harm.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly used in emotionally sensitive situations, but current safety approaches either over-restrict, over-facilitate, or awkwardly mix both, which can still harm vulnerable users. The authors want to systematically study how commercial LLMs behave in escalating vulnerability scenarios, understand why these issues arise structurally, and develop a general design principle that can reduce harm without undermining user autonomy or validation.
AI.method: The authors construct a three-turn vignette that escalates user vulnerability and present it to three commercial LLMs, creating 900 interaction sessions by varying the content across material, relational, and somatic proxies of status. They code each model response using two binary indices reflecting validation/contestation of the user’s causal story (VCC/VCI) and analyze the patterns to reveal a consistent failure mode they term adaptive capitulation. They then conceptually analyze why this arises from the architecture-level objectives and propose a new principle (MRS), rather than a specific algorithm, that can be embedded in different systems.
AI.result: They find that across many scenarios and models, responses frequently follow a pattern where the system initially validates the user’s sense of injustice or harm, but then pivots into detailed, practical facilitation of a harmful or maladaptive goal that the model nominally discouraged. This pattern, adaptive capitulation, appears systematically rather than an occasional quirk. The analysis shows that the observed trilemma—between protecting users, honoring autonomy, and avoiding contradiction—is baked into current response architectures.
AI.conclusion: The work concludes that standard LLM safety and alignment strategies create a structural trilemma in emotionally charged, vulnerability-escalating contexts, leading to a distinct failure mode of adaptive capitulation. To address this, the authors propose Minimal Reattributive Sufficiency: always include at least one gentle, alternative causal or meaning-making cue inside an otherwise validating response. This preserves users’ sense of being heard and their autonomy while keeping open a path toward healthier reattribution, and it is framed as an architecture-neutral design principle for future safety work.

title: GraphContainer: A Unified Platform for Comparing and Debugging Graph RAG Methods
summary: Graph RAG mitigates hallucinations and stale knowledge in LLMs, particularly for multi-hop question answering. However, existing approaches remain highly fragmented and incompatible. The structural heterogeneity of graph formats across different frameworks and the lack of granular visualization tools make it exceedingly difficult to evaluate and compare retrieval behaviors. To bridge this gap, we propose GraphContainer, a novel platform designed to unify and visualize diverse graph RAG workflows. GraphContainer features two key components: (1) a Unified Graph Representation (UGR) layer that seamlessly standardizes multi-format graphs, and (2) a Graph Recorder that tracks and visually renders the step-by-step retrieval process. Through an interactive web interface, we demonstrate GraphContainer's ability to import heterogeneous graphs and perform live, traceable visual debugging of graph RAG methods. Ultimately, we show how GraphContainer enables controlled comparisons of various graph formats and retrieval strategies, lowering the barrier for researchers and practitioners to design optimal graph RAG pipelines. A demonstration video is available at https://youtu.be/O02eNJLwkU0.

AI.tldr: GraphContainer is a platform that standardizes and visualizes graph-based RAG workflows for LLMs, enabling easier comparison and debugging of retrieval behaviors.
AI.motivation: Existing graph RAG methods for reducing hallucinations and handling multi-hop QA are fragmented and incompatible due to heterogeneous graph formats and lack of fine-grained visualization tools, making evaluation and comparison of retrieval behaviors very difficult.
AI.method: The authors design GraphContainer with two main components: (1) a Unified Graph Representation (UGR) layer that standardizes heterogeneous graph formats into a common representation, and (2) a Graph Recorder that logs and visually renders the step-by-step retrieval process within graph RAG. They provide an interactive web interface that imports different graph formats and supports live, traceable visual debugging of retrieval workflows.
AI.result: Using the interactive web interface, GraphContainer successfully imports heterogeneous graphs, tracks graph RAG retrieval steps, and visually displays them, enabling users to conduct controlled comparisons of different graph formats and retrieval strategies in practice.
AI.conclusion: GraphContainer lowers the barrier for researchers and practitioners to design and optimize graph RAG pipelines by unifying graph representations and providing detailed visualizations of retrieval behavior, thereby facilitating controlled comparisons and improved method development.

title: Reference-Free Evaluation of Reasoning in Open-Ended Question Answering
summary: AI-generated answers in high-stakes domains are often fluent but difficult to verify, especially when they contain multi-step reasoning rather than a single final answer. We propose a reasoning-based, reference-free framework for auditing LLM-generated outputs. The method decomposes a generated reasoning trace into segments, labels local premise-target relations using Natural Language Inference (NLI), and organizes these relations into a hypergraph. A deterministic backward AND-OR search then assigns segment-level audit labels that indicate how each segment is grounded within the generated response. We evaluate the framework in two settings: deductive mathematical reasoning with Hard2Verify, and open-ended medical reasoning with UroReason, a new physician-annotated benchmark of LLM reasoning traces from real clinical cases. Across these settings, our NLI-hypergraph audit provides a more reliable reference-free evaluation signal than direct LLM-as-judge baselines. In the clinical setting, state-of-the-art LLM judges often fail to identify problematic reasoning segments, over-accepting fluent but weakly grounded responses. Our results show that QA evaluation should account for how inferential relations compose across a reasoning trace, rather than relying only on final answers or LLMs as verifiers. UroReason will be made available through an API, and our code will be released as open source.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a reference-free framework to audit and evaluate the reasoning traces of large language models (LLMs), focusing on segment-level validity rather than only final answers.
AI.motivation: AI-generated reasoning in high-stakes domains like medicine and mathematics is often fluent but hard to verify, and existing evaluation methods either require ground-truth references or rely on LLMs as judges, which frequently miss flawed intermediate reasoning.
AI.method: The authors decompose an LLM’s reasoning trace into smaller segments, use Natural Language Inference (NLI) to label premise–target relations between segments, represent these relations as a hypergraph, and then run a deterministic backward AND-OR search over the hypergraph to assign audit labels that describe how each segment is grounded in prior content.
AI.result: In experiments on deductive math reasoning (Hard2Verify) and open-ended medical reasoning (UroReason, a new benchmark of physician-annotated clinical reasoning traces), the NLI-hypergraph audit provides a more reliable, reference-free evaluation signal than LLM-as-judge baselines and more accurately flags problematic reasoning segments, especially in clinical cases where fluent but weakly grounded answers often pass LLM-judge checks.
AI.conclusion: Quality assessment for LLM-generated answers, particularly in high-stakes settings, should evaluate the composition of inferential relations across the entire reasoning trace rather than depend solely on final answers or LLM-judge evaluations; the proposed NLI-hypergraph auditing framework and the UroReason benchmark support this shift and will be publicly released.

title: AdaRoPE: Not All Attention Heads Should Rotate and Scale Equally
summary: Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE) is widely adopted in Transformers to encode positional information, yet standard implementations enforce a uniform frequency schedule and scaling across all attention heads. Using simplified retrieval tasks and length generalization scenarios, we show -- both empirically and theoretically -- that heads with different functional roles require distinct frequency ranges and attention scaling factors to operate effectively. Ignoring this structure leads to suboptimal utilization of embedding dimensions and degraded performance, particularly under long-context settings. To address these limitations, we propose AdaRoPE, which equips each attention head with learnable rotation frequencies and attention scaling factors. Pretrained LLMs with AdaRoPE consistently outperform existing RoPE variants, including partial RoPE and NoPE baselines. For context extension, we further show that uniform frequency and attention scaling, used in methods such as YaRN, are suboptimal. By applying head-specific scaling, AdaRoPE enables better context extension while better preserving short-context performance in both the extrapolation setting and the long-context continued pretraining setting. These results highlight the importance of optimizing rotary position embedding at the level of individual attention heads.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies that standard RoPE uses uniform frequencies and scaling across attention heads, which is suboptimal, and proposes AdaRoPE, a head-wise learnable variant that improves long-context performance and context extension.
AI.motivation: Transformers with RoPE treat all attention heads identically in terms of positional frequency schedule and scaling, despite different heads having different roles. This uniformity can waste embedding capacity and hurt performance, especially for long contexts and context extension, motivating a more flexible, head-specific positional encoding scheme.
AI.method: The authors analyze simplified retrieval and length generalization tasks to show that different attention heads need different frequency ranges and attention scaling. They then introduce AdaRoPE, which assigns each attention head its own learnable rotation frequencies and attention scaling factors instead of sharing them globally. They integrate AdaRoPE into pretrained LLMs and compare against standard RoPE variants and context-extension methods like YaRN, also testing both extrapolation and long-context continued pretraining setups.
AI.result: LLMs augmented with AdaRoPE consistently outperform models using standard RoPE, partial RoPE, NoPE, and methods with uniform frequency/attention scaling for context extension. AdaRoPE improves long-context performance while maintaining or better preserving short-context performance, and head-specific scaling yields better context extension than uniform scaling strategies like YaRN.
AI.conclusion: Positional encoding with a single global RoPE schedule is structurally mismatched to the diverse roles of attention heads. Allowing per-head learnable rotation frequencies and attention scaling (AdaRoPE) leads to better use of embedding dimensions, stronger long-context handling, and more effective context extension. Optimizing RoPE at the level of individual attention heads is important for improving modern LLMs, particularly in long-context regimes.

title: Multi-Mask Diffusion Language Models for Few-Step Generation
summary: Masked diffusion models (MDMs) are a promising family of language generators, but achieving high-quality few-step generation remains challenging. In MDMs, all forward trajectories collapse to a single fully masked state, leaving no terminal entropy for consistency-style few-step generation. While recent few-step alternatives based on uniform-state diffusion avoid this degeneracy, it becomes harder to distinguish clean tokens from noise than MDMs, which usually harms modeling quality and training efficiency. In this work, we propose a multi-mask diffusion model (MultiMDM) that preserves the masking structure towards few-step generation. In the forward process, each clean token is first pushed towards a designated mask and then gradually mixes over the mask set. As a result, the backward process has a drafting capability by predicting a designated mask before refining to a clean token. We derive a closed-form ELBO training objective for MultiMDM that supports continual training from pretrained MDMs. In addition, we formulate a purely discrete-state consistency distillation scheme, with a shared-Gumbel coupling to reduce pathwise entropy. Experiments on pretraining and distillation show that MultiMDM provides an effective foundation for principled few-step generation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MultiMDM, a multi-mask diffusion model for language that enables high-quality few-step generation while preserving the advantages of masked diffusion models.
AI.motivation: Standard masked diffusion models collapse all forward trajectories into a single fully masked state, leaving no entropy for few-step/consistency generation, while existing uniform-state few-step approaches harm modeling quality and efficiency. The authors want a model that supports accurate and efficient few-step generation without sacrificing the structural benefits of masking.
AI.method: They design MultiMDM, where in the forward process each clean token is first moved toward a designated mask and then diffused over a set of masks, preserving masking structure and multiple terminal states. The reverse process thus has a drafting phase (predicting a designated mask) followed by refinement to clean tokens. They derive a closed-form ELBO objective that allows continual training from existing MDMs and propose a fully discrete consistency distillation scheme using shared-Gumbel coupling to reduce pathwise entropy.
AI.result: On language modeling pretraining and distillation benchmarks, MultiMDM improves few-step generation quality and demonstrates that it can serve as a strong base model for principled few-step language generation, outperforming prior MDM and uniform-state diffusion approaches in the few-step regime.
AI.conclusion: MultiMDM successfully reconciles masked diffusion’s strong modeling quality with the need for few-step, consistency-style generation by introducing multiple mask states, a compatible ELBO objective, and a discrete consistency distillation scheme, making it a practical and principled foundation for efficient language generation.

title: Statistically Grounded Sparse-Feature Interventions for Activation-Space Control in Large Language Models
summary: Activation steering offers a lightweight alternative to fine-tuning for behavioral control of large language models, but SAE-based steering methods often rely on learned steering objectives or single-criterion feature selection. We introduce a transparent SAE-feature steering pipeline that first applies a six-condition reliability filter, then ranks sparse features through an unweighted Borda consensus over three complementary statistics: $F$-test, KSG mutual information, and Cohen's $d$. The resulting steering direction is constructed as a Cohen's-$d$-weighted combination of SAE decoder rows, providing an optimization-free direction motivated by Fisher-LDA under approximate SAE-feature decorrelation. Across three Gemma-family models, four behavioral domains, and 356 layer-strength configurations, the method produces measurable domain-specific shifts while revealing a substantial gap between raw attribute movement and quality-preserving generation. In the strongest configuration, logical-correctness steering reaches a primary-score delta of $+1.16$ in Gemma~2 9B; however, our broader finding is that usable steering is highly localized by model, domain, layer, and strength. These results argue that activation-steering evaluations should report quality-conditioned success alongside raw behavioral shift. Our code and data are available at https://github.com/Oshayer-Siddique/LLM-Steering-Using-SAE.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a transparent, optimization-free pipeline for steering large language model behaviors using SAE features, showing domain-specific control is possible but highly localized and constrained by quality trade-offs.
AI.motivation: Existing activation steering methods with sparse autoencoders (SAEs) either depend on learned steering objectives or simplistic, single-metric feature selection. There is a need for a more transparent, principled, and general pipeline that can reliably identify and combine SAE features to steer models, while also properly evaluating the trade-off between target behavioral change and generation quality.
AI.method: They design a pipeline that: (1) applies a six-condition reliability filter to SAE features; (2) scores remaining sparse features using three complementary statistics—F-test, KSG mutual information, and Cohen's d—then aggregates these via an unweighted Borda consensus ranking; (3) constructs a steering direction as a Cohen's-d–weighted linear combination of the selected SAE decoder rows.
