This direction is optimization-free and is motivated by Fisher Linear Discriminant Analysis under the assumption of approximate decorrelation among SAE features. They then test this steering across three Gemma-family LLMs, four behavioral domains, and 356 combinations of layer and steering strength.
AI.result: The method produces clear, measurable shifts in targeted behavioral domains across all tested Gemma-family models and domains. In the best case—logical correctness steering on Gemma 2 9B—the method achieves a primary metric improvement of +1.16. However, systematic experiments reveal a large gap between raw changes in internal attribute representations and actual improvements in quality-preserving generation, and strong dependence on the specific model, domain, layer, and steering strength chosen.
AI.conclusion: A transparent, optimization-free SAE-based steering pipeline can induce domain-specific behavioral shifts in LLMs, but effective and usable steering is highly localized and fragile with respect to model, domain, layer, and strength. Therefore, evaluations of activation steering must go beyond raw attribute movement and explicitly consider quality-conditioned success. The work provides code and data to facilitate further research and standardization in steering evaluation.

title: SLPO: Scaling Latent Reasoning via a Surrogate Policy
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards has become the predominant recipe for eliciting test-time scaling in explicit Chain-of-Thought reasoners. Yet this scaling path remains computationally costly, since every intermediate step must be decoded as a language token. Latent reasoning instead carries intermediate computation as continuous vectors and already matches or surpasses explicit CoT at far shorter horizons. Despite this promise, latent reasoners remain largely imitation-bound, while explicit CoT has already moved past imitation via outcome-reward RL. Latent trajectories lack a tractable per-step likelihood and an adaptive stopping interface under fixed thinking budgets, so outcome rewards cannot elicit latent test-time scaling. We introduce Surrogate Latent Policy Optimization (SLPO) to bring outcome-reward RL to autoregressive latent reasoners: an empirical surrogate policy density over latent transitions for trajectory-level credit assignment, and a correctness-supervised stopping head that outcome-reward optimization refines into a variable-horizon policy. Across continuous and soft thinking settings, SLPO improves Pass@$k$ under parallel sampling and allocates longer latent computation to harder instances with higher deterministic accuracy.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Surrogate Latent Policy Optimization (SLPO), an RL method that enables test-time scaling for latent reasoning models using outcome rewards instead of explicit Chain-of-Thought tokens.
AI.motivation: Explicit Chain-of-Thought with verifiable rewards enables test-time scaling but is computationally expensive because it requires decoding every reasoning step as a token. Latent reasoning, which keeps intermediate computation in vector form, is more efficient and already strong, but is mostly limited to imitation learning since outcome-reward RL cannot be directly applied due to lack of per-step likelihoods and good stopping mechanisms. The authors aim to bring the benefits of outcome-reward RL and adaptive compute allocation to latent reasoners.
AI.method: They propose Surrogate Latent Policy Optimization (SLPO), which builds an empirical surrogate policy density over latent transitions so that trajectory-level outcome rewards can be used for credit assignment in an autoregressive latent reasoner. In addition, they introduce a correctness-supervised stopping head that is further refined by outcome-reward optimization, turning it into a variable-horizon policy that can adaptively decide how much latent computation to use per instance.
AI.result: Using SLPO on latent reasoning models in both continuous and soft thinking regimes, the method improves Pass@$k$ under parallel sampling and learns to allocate more latent computation to harder instances, resulting in higher deterministic accuracy compared to baselines or prior latent reasoning approaches.
AI.conclusion: SLPO successfully extends outcome-reward reinforcement learning to latent reasoners, enabling test-time scaling without explicit token-level Chain-of-Thought. This allows more compute-efficient reasoning that still benefits from RL-based optimization and adaptive thinking time, narrowing the gap between explicit and latent reasoning approaches.

title: Logic-Guided Data Extraction with Answer Set Programming and Large Language Models
comment: 42nd International Conference on Logic Programming, to appear in Theory and Practice of Logic Programming (TPLP)
summary: When Large Language Models (LLMs) are used for semantic data extraction from unstructured text, producing candidate relational facts from natural language, they may remain unreliable for tasks requiring complex combinatorial reasoning and global consistency. This paper proposes a logic-guided data extraction framework combining LLM-based extraction with Answer Set Programming (ASP). The LLM produces candidate facts, whereas ASP performs validation, inference, consistency checking, and control. Unlike existing pipelines that query the LLM independently for all target predicates, the proposed approach uses ASP reasoning to identify which predicates are logically admissible at each stage and to guide extraction queries. By interleaving LLM calls with ASP derivation, the framework infers logically implied facts without further extraction and detects inconsistencies early. We formalize the pipeline and prove that, under mild assumptions, it is equivalent to the baseline approach with respect to the final extracted facts, while requiring fewer LLM calls. We also introduce a caching mechanism for logic-based control queries, exploiting monotonicity of conjunctive queries over incrementally constructed fact sets to reduce solver invocations. Experiments on ASP-derived benchmarks show that the framework reduces LLM calls and improves extraction quality by mitigating spurious outputs, demonstrating the value of non-monotonic logic programming for controlled semantic extraction.

AI.tldr: They combine large language models with Answer Set Programming to make semantic data extraction more reliable, consistent, and cheaper in LLM calls.
AI.motivation: LLMs can extract relational facts from text but are unreliable for tasks that require complex combinatorial reasoning and global logical consistency; existing methods also over-query LLMs for every predicate independently, wasting calls and allowing inconsistent outputs.
AI.method: Propose a logic-guided extraction framework where: (1) an LLM first proposes candidate facts from text; (2) Answer Set Programming (ASP) is used to validate these facts, enforce consistency, perform inference, and determine which predicates are logically admissible at each step; (3) LLM calls are interleaved with ASP derivations so that only logically needed predicates are queried; (4) a caching mechanism for logic-based control queries exploits monotonicity of conjunctive queries over growing fact sets to reduce repeated solver calls. They formalize the pipeline and compare it conceptually with a baseline that queries LLMs independently for all target predicates.
AI.result: They prove theoretically that, given mild assumptions, the proposed pipeline yields the same final set of extracted facts as the baseline that queries all predicates independently, but with fewer LLM calls. Empirically, on benchmarks derived from ASP programs, the method reduces the number of LLM queries and improves extraction quality by filtering out spurious, inconsistent facts.
AI.conclusion: Interleaving LLM-based extraction with non-monotonic logic programming (ASP) for reasoning, validation, and control leads to more efficient and reliable semantic data extraction from text. Logic-guided control of LLM queries can maintain the same expressive power as naive querying while improving consistency and reducing both LLM and solver invocations.

title: Lightweight Person-Place Relation Extraction from Historical Newspapers with Dependency Graphs and Proximity Features
comment: 19 pages, 4 figures. Accepted at CLEF 2026 HIPE Shared Task. To appear in CEUR Workshop Proceedings (CEUR-WS.org)
summary: The HIPE-2026 shared task introduces person-place relation extraction from multilingual historical newspapers as a new evaluation track, classifying the at and isAt relations between pre-annotated person and location mentions in English, French, and German. Motivated by the cost of processing historical archives at scale, our team (DS@GT HIPE, team 2 in the official results) investigates how far a lightweight, interpretable system can go without any pretrained language model at the relation classification stage. Our approach builds a document-level graph from dependency parses, extracts proximity-based and part-of-speech features for each entity pair, and classifies them with small scikit-learn ensembles or compact Graph Attention Networks, keeping every submitted run under 847K parameters. On the official evaluation (Test A, the newspaper test set), our best run reached a macro recall of 0.5142, ranking 3rd on the Efficiency profile while placing mid-table on Accuracy among the 17 participating teams. Two findings stand out. First, minimum character distance alone captures most of the classification signal; adding further engineered features yields inconsistent gains and sometimes degrades performance, echoing prior evidence that argument distance dominates relation extraction. Second, document-grouped cross-validation is essential on this corpus: pair-level splits inflate scores by 25-37 percentage points because entity mentions recur across documents, a data-leakage effect that grouped cross-validation removes.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a lightweight, interpretable system for extracting person-place relations from multilingual historical newspapers without using large pretrained language models, highlighting the strong predictive power of simple character-distance features and the necessity of document-grouped cross-validation to avoid data leakage.
AI.motivation: Processing large-scale historical newspaper archives is costly, and many existing relation extraction systems rely on heavy pretrained models that are expensive to train and run. The authors aim to explore how well a lightweight, interpretable system can perform on the HIPE-2026 person-place relation extraction task, particularly in multilingual settings, while also examining what features truly drive performance and how evaluation methodologies impact reported results.
AI.method: The system builds a document-level graph from syntactic dependency parses of the text. For each pre-annotated person-location mention pair, it extracts proximity-based features (especially character distance) and part-of-speech-related features. These features are then fed into either small scikit-learn ensemble classifiers or compact Graph Attention Networks (GATs), all constrained to remain under 847K parameters. The study also compares different cross-validation strategies, including document-grouped versus pair-level splits, to understand their impact on evaluation.
AI.result: On the official HIPE-2026 Test A set (historical newspapers in English, French, and German), the best configuration achieves a macro recall of 0.5142. This performance ranks 3rd among all 17 teams on the Efficiency profile (reflecting model compactness and resource usage) and mid-range in terms of Accuracy. The experiments show that minimum character distance between entities accounts for most of the classification performance, while additional engineered features offer inconsistent or even negative contributions. Furthermore, pair-level cross-validation artificially boosts scores by 25–37 macro-recall points due to repeated entity mentions across documents, revealing substantial data leakage.
AI.conclusion: A compact, interpretable relation extraction system without large pretrained language models can achieve competitive efficiency and reasonable accuracy on multilingual historical newspaper data. The dominant predictive feature is simply the minimum character distance between person and place mentions, which questions the added value of more complex engineered features in this setting. Crucially, the work emphasizes that document-grouped cross-validation is necessary to obtain realistic performance estimates on this corpus, as naive pair-level splits suffer from severe data leakage. These insights guide both the design of efficient relation extraction systems and the construction of fair evaluation protocols for historical text processing tasks.

title: Geometry-Guided Constraint Learning for LLM Safety Classification
summary: Safety as Polytope (SaP) learns linear half-space constraints in LLM hidden space but requires per-category tuning of the constraint count K. We show that sparse autoencoder (SAE) feature extraction resolves this: K=2 becomes optimal for 12/14 categories on Qwen3.5-9B, achieving 96-99% accuracy per category on our BeaverTails classification benchmark, largely eliminating the need for exhaustive sweeps (K=4-25 with random initialization). This convergence to two planes is consistent with the Linear Representation Hypothesis, providing suggestive evidence that safety boundaries in this setting admit a low-dimensional linear description in the SAE feature space. Building on this geometric perspective, we introduce a cone constraint whose learnable aperture adapts to each category's cluster concentration, stabilized by a three-phase training

AI.tldr: They study making LLM safety constraints simpler and more efficient using geometric structures in the model’s hidden space.
AI.motivation: Existing SaP safety method needs manual tuning of how many linear constraints K per safety category, which is expensive and brittle. They want an approach that works well with a fixed, small K across categories and better reflects the underlying representation geometry.
AI.method: Use sparse autoencoder (SAE) features as the representation space for Safety as Polytope, then systematically test different numbers of half-space constraints K on the BeaverTails safety classification benchmark using Qwen3.5-9B, and analyze how performance changes. Introduce a new cone-shaped constraint whose aperture is learned and trained in a stabilized three-phase schedule.
AI.result: In the SAE feature space, setting K=2 planes is near-optimal for 12 out of 14 safety categories, reaching 96–99% classification accuracy per category, removing the need for wide random sweeps over K values (4–25). The cone constraint can adapt its aperture to each category’s cluster tightness.
AI.conclusion: SAE features make the geometry of safety-relevant representations effectively low-dimensional, allowing very simple linear boundaries (just two planes) to capture safety categories with high accuracy. This supports the Linear Representation Hypothesis and suggests that efficient, geometrically grounded safety classifiers can be trained with minimal hyperparameter tuning by using low-dimensional linear or conic decision boundaries in SAE space.

title: Beyond Relevance-Centric Retrieval: Rubric-Oriented Document Set Selection and Ranking
summary: As large language models and AI agents become the primary consumers of search results, document set quality determines the upper bound of downstream generation. Yet existing evaluation systems remain confined to scoring documents independently and aggregating via nDCG, ignoring inter-document interactions (redundancy, conflict, complementarity) and unable to answer what makes one document set better than another. To address these issues, we propose a complete evaluate-diagnose-optimize framework. We design SetwiseEvalKit, a three-level, nine-dimension document set evaluation benchmark covering both short-form and long-form scenarios, comprising approximately 28K high-quality evaluation rubrics. We systematically evaluate 12 rerankers: even the best method achieves no more than 45% coverage, cross-document coordination dimensions are universally weak, and no single method maintains top performance across both settings. Building on this, we propose Rubric4Setwise, a training-free method that converts rubric-based evaluation criteria into document set selection signals, achieving the best downstream generation performance with fewer documents and search rounds. It is the only method that maintains state-of-the-art results across both scenarios, validating the effectiveness of closing the loop from evaluation to optimization.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a framework and toolkit for evaluating and optimizing sets of search documents for LLM-based consumption, along with a new training-free method that uses evaluation rubrics to select better document sets and improve downstream generation.
AI.motivation: Current search and reranking evaluation focuses on individual documents using metrics like nDCG, which ignore interactions among documents in a set (e.g., redundancy, conflict, complementarity). As LLMs and AI agents increasingly consume multiple documents jointly, we need ways to evaluate and understand what makes one document set better than another for downstream generation quality, and to use those insights to improve retrieval and reranking systems.
AI.method: 1) Propose an evaluate-diagnose-optimize framework for document set quality in the context of LLM consumption. 2) Build SetwiseEvalKit, a benchmark with three evaluation levels and nine dimensions that capture both short-form and long-form scenarios, containing ~28K human-designed evaluation rubrics that assess document sets holistically. 3) Systematically evaluate 12 reranking methods on this benchmark to diagnose their strengths and weaknesses, especially in cross-document coordination. 4) Introduce Rubric4Setwise, a training-free method that converts rubric-based evaluation criteria into signals for selecting document sets, thereby directly optimizing for downstream generation quality.
AI.result: Experimental evaluation shows that: (a) all tested rerankers have limited coverage of the evaluation rubrics, with the best achieving at most 45% coverage; (b) dimensions requiring cross-document coordination (e.g., handling redundancy, conflicts, or complementarity) are particularly weak; and (c) no single reranker consistently performs best across both short-form and long-form scenarios. In contrast, Rubric4Setwise achieves the best downstream generation performance while using fewer documents and fewer search rounds, and is the only method that maintains state-of-the-art results across both evaluated scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Evaluating document sets requires going beyond per-document relevance metrics to explicitly model inter-document interactions and downstream generation needs. The proposed SetwiseEvalKit benchmark exposes major limitations in current rerankers, especially on cross-document coordination. By turning rich evaluation rubrics into selection signals, Rubric4Setwise closes the loop from evaluation to optimization and delivers consistent, state-of-the-art document set quality for LLM-based generation across multiple scenarios.

title: Rethinking Uncertainty Evaluation in Large Language Models
summary: Calibration is the primary criterion for evaluating LLM confidence, but it is insufficient: it admits trivially incoherent estimators, depends on the evaluation distribution, and does not test the extent to which the estimation can be interpreted as a consistent, underlying probability function. What we actually need is for LLM confidence estimates to satisfy the conditions required of coherent probabilistic beliefs. We formalize these conditions along three axes (structural coherence, faithfulness, and usefulness) and operationalize them as the C1 metrics. Widely used estimators systematically violate these conditions despite appearing well-calibrated: models assign lower confidence to logically easier questions 31\% of the time, and common interventions reducing RMSCE leave structural violations unchanged, suggesting that calibration is orthogonal to probabilistic validity. RLHF and chain-of-thought improve usefulness metrics without restoring coherence. Our results show current LLM confidence estimates cannot be interpreted as coherent probabilities; our framework provides the tools to measure and close this gap.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that standard calibration metrics are not enough to judge LLM confidence estimates and introduces a new framework (C1 metrics) to test whether LLM confidence can be viewed as coherent probabilistic beliefs.
AI.motivation: Existing practice evaluates LLM confidence mainly through calibration (e.g., RMSCE), but this allows confidence estimators that are incoherent, distribution-dependent, and not interpretable as genuine probabilities. The authors want a principled way to ensure that confidence estimates behave like consistent probabilistic beliefs.
AI.method: They formalize what it means for confidence estimates to behave like coherent probabilities along three axes—structural coherence, faithfulness, and usefulness—and turn these into quantitative C1 metrics. They then apply these metrics to widely used confidence estimators and interventions such as calibration techniques, RLHF, and chain-of-thought prompting.
AI.result: They find that popular estimators, though often well-calibrated, systematically violate coherence: e.g., models assign lower confidence to logically easier questions 31% of the time, and interventions that reduce RMSCE do not fix these structural issues. RLHF and chain-of-thought improve usefulness metrics but still fail to restore full coherence.
AI.conclusion: Current LLM confidence scores should not be interpreted as proper probabilities because they lack structural coherence and other properties required of probabilistic beliefs. The proposed C1 framework offers concrete metrics to diagnose these issues and guide the development of more coherent confidence estimation methods.

title: Learning the Arabic Dialect Continuum as a Continuous Space: A Regression Approach to Speaker Origin Prediction
summary: We present a regression-based approach to Arabic dialect geolocation that models dialectal variation as a continuous geographic space rather than discrete categories. Speaker origin is predicted as continuous latitude-longitude coordinates using a hierarchical neural architecture that fuses frame-level XLS-R-300M and Whisper-large-v3 encoder representations with phonotactic descriptors through a Transformer encoder and a learnable attention-pooled query. A spherical geodesic loss directly optimizes great-circle distance on Earth's surface, avoiding distortions inherent to planar coordinate regression. Under a leakage-free 5-fold GroupKFold protocol grouped by source recording, our model attains a pooled median localization error of 481.2 km. Auxiliary country and city heads reach 64.5% and 45.2% accuracy, respectively. A permutation Mantel test on the learned latent space provides quantitative support for the Arabic dialect continuum hypothesis. To probe true generalization, we further introduce a city-masking protocol in which two cities per fold are removed from training but retained in validation. Under this zero-shot regime, the mean error rises to 1173.3 km, a 1.32x degradation relative to seen cities. Our findings establish continuous geographic modeling as a principled framework for Arabic dialect geolocation and quantify both its strengths and the substantial headroom that remains.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a neural regression model that predicts Arabic dialect speaker locations as continuous GPS coordinates instead of discrete dialect labels, achieving ~481 km median localization error and providing evidence for an Arabic dialect continuum, while also exposing limitations in zero-shot city generalization.
AI.motivation: Traditional dialect identification treats dialects as discrete classes (e.g., country or city labels), which does not match the continuous, gradual nature of dialect variation across geography and makes it hard to model intermediate or unseen locations. For Arabic, where dialects form a continuum and cover a large, diverse region, there is a need for methods that can capture fine-grained geographic variation and provide more interpretable, spatially coherent outputs (e.g., actual map coordinates) rather than coarse labels. The authors aim to build a principled, continuous-space model of Arabic dialect geography and to rigorously quantify how well such a model localizes speakers and generalizes to unseen cities.
AI.method: The authors design a hierarchical neural architecture that predicts a speaker’s origin as continuous latitude-longitude coordinates. They combine multiple speech-derived representations: frame-level acoustic features from XLS-R-300M and Whisper-large-v3 encoders, plus phonotactic descriptors. These are fused via a Transformer encoder, and a learnable query token with attention pooling summarizes the utterance into a fixed representation. The model uses a spherical geodesic loss that directly minimizes great-circle (haversine-like) distance on Earth’s surface, avoiding errors from projecting coordinates to a plane. Evaluation uses a strict 5-fold GroupKFold setup, grouping by recording source to prevent train-test leakage. On top of the shared latent space, auxiliary classification heads predict country and city labels. The authors also run a permutation Mantel test on the learned latent representations and geographic distances to test the dialect continuum hypothesis. To assess zero-shot generalization, they introduce a city-masking protocol where two cities per fold are excluded from training but kept in validation, measuring localization error for completely unseen cities.
AI.result: In standard (seen-city) evaluation, the continuous regression model achieves a pooled median localization error of 481.2 km. The auxiliary classification heads attain 64.5% accuracy at country level and 45.2% at city level, indicating that the learned geographic embeddings remain informative for discrete dialect categories. The Mantel test shows a statistically significant correlation between distances in the learned latent space and geographic distances, supporting the view that the model captures a dialect continuum aligned with physical space. Under the proposed city-masking, zero-shot protocol, mean localization error increases to 1173.3 km, which the authors report as a 1.32× degradation relative to the seen-city scenario, highlighting a substantial drop in performance for unseen locations.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that modeling Arabic dialect variation as a continuous geographic regression problem is both feasible and empirically effective, providing finer-grained and spatially coherent localization than discrete dialect classification alone. The hierarchical architecture that fuses strong self-supervised acoustic encoders with phonotactic features and optimizes a spherical geodesic loss yields competitive localization accuracy and useful auxiliary country/city predictions. The statistically validated alignment between latent and geographic distances offers quantitative evidence for an Arabic dialect continuum. At the same time, the sharp performance degradation for masked, unseen cities reveals significant limitations in out-of-distribution generalization, indicating that more work is needed—e.g., better inductive biases, data coverage, or adaptation methods—to robustly geolocate speakers from novel regions. Overall, the paper positions continuous geographic modeling as a principled framework and baseline for future work on dialect geolocation and dialectometry in Arabic and potentially other languages.

title: Spectral-LSH: Sub-Quadratic Prompt Compression via Krylov-Projected Locality-Sensitive Hashing
summary:
Long-prompt inference remains expensive because prefill attention scales quadratically with sequence length. We propose Spectral-LSH, a training-free prompt compression method that operates before the prompt enters the language model. Spectral-LSH approximates the dominant components of an implicit attention-kernel operator using a Krylov subspace method together with random features, avoiding explicit $O(N^2)$ attention-kernel materialization. It then applies SimHash in the resulting attention eigenspace to group similar tokens and aggregate them into macro-tokens with causal positional assignments.
  We evaluate Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3, Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, and Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct on C4. Our experiments reveal a compression-ratio phase transition. Below $ρ= 4 \times$, local token redundancy is low enough that lightweight chunking typically provides the best latency--quality trade-off. Above $ρ= 8 \times$, the spectral path preserves quality that chunking loses. At $ρ= 16 \times$, Qwen2.5-7B (adaptive) reduces the PPL ratio from 353.409 to 196.963, while Qwen2.5-14B (adaptive) reduces it from 9.533 to 3.427.
  On a small long-context structured stress test containing JSON-like, code-like, and table-like inputs, local LSH also improves every metric over chunking at $8 \times$. The adaptive backend captures both regimes by using the chunk path at low compression and spectral clustering at high compression, although chunking remains the fastest backend in total latency.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Spectral-LSH, a training-free prompt compression technique that reduces long-context inference cost by clustering similar tokens into macro-tokens in an approximated attention eigenspace, achieving better perplexity–latency trade-offs than simple chunking at high compression ratios.
AI.motivation: Long-prompt (long-context) inference in LLMs is expensive because the prefill attention computation scales quadratically with sequence length, making deployment on long inputs slow and costly. Existing simple compression approaches like chunking may work at mild compression but suffer substantial quality loss at higher compression ratios. The authors seek a way to compress prompts before they enter the model while preserving model quality, especially at high compression ratios, and without requiring retraining or modification of the base model.
AI.method: They propose Spectral-LSH, a training-free, model-agnostic prompt compression method. It approximates the dominant components of an implicit attention-kernel operator using a Krylov subspace method combined with random features, thus avoiding explicit O(N^2) materialization of the attention kernel. In this approximated attention eigenspace, they then apply SimHash-based Locality-Sensitive Hashing (LSH) to group similar tokens. Tokens mapped to the same hash bucket are aggregated into macro-tokens with causal positional assignments, forming a compressed prompt that is then fed to the LLM. They also introduce an adaptive backend that chooses between a lightweight chunking path at low compression ratios and the spectral clustering path at high compression ratios.
AI.result: On C4 with Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3, Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, and Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct, they observe a compression-ratio phase transition: for compression ratios below 4×, simple chunking yields the best latency–quality trade-off, while for ratios above 8×, Spectral-LSH preserves quality better than chunking. At 16× compression, the adaptive Spectral-LSH backend for Qwen2.5-7B reduces the perplexity (PPL) ratio from 353.409 to 196.963, and for Qwen2.5-14B from 9.533 to 3.427 relative to a baseline. On a structured long-context stress test with JSON-like, code-like, and table-like inputs, local LSH outperforms chunking on every metric at 8× compression. Chunking, however, remains the fastest in total latency.
AI.conclusion: Spectral-LSH enables effective, training-free compression of long prompts by approximating attention’s dominant spectral components and clustering similar tokens in that space, thereby reducing prefill cost while maintaining model quality at high compression ratios. There exists a clear phase transition in which chunking is preferable at low compression, but spectral clustering dominates at higher compression. An adaptive strategy that switches between chunking and spectral LSH can capture the best trade-offs across regimes, though the simplest chunking remains the overall fastest backend.

title: TriAgent: Divergence-Aware Multi-Agent Committees for Cost-Efficient Financial Sentiment Analysis
summary: Production LLM-based financial sentiment analysis faces a structural cost trap: most queries are trivially classifiable, yet expensive cloud reasoners process them all, and the bill scales linearly with user count. We present TriAgent, a multi-agent committee stratified by contextual granularity -- a word-level lexicon (VADER), a sentence-level domain transformer (FinBERT), and a cross-sentence reasoner (Qwen2.5, 0.5B-14B-4bit, with Mistral-7B and Phi-3.5-mini cross-family checks). A three-way Semantic Divergence Index (SDI) measures pairwise disagreement across granularities and routes each query accordingly. Our central finding is the critic plateau: when the LLM is re-tasked as a critic over the smaller agents' outputs, F1 plateaus at ~0.87 across 1.5B-7B Qwen (bootstrap 95% CIs overlap), while a same-size 3-persona vote drops to F1=0.66, which is driven by granularity-stratified diversity. Three corollaries follow from the same SDI signal: (i) a Shared Consensus Dictionary on multilingual sentence-BERT answers 95% of Chinese queries from an English cache at F1=0.99 -- cross-border canonicalization at zero marginal cost; (ii) SDI doubles as a post-hoc LLM-hallucination detector at AUC=0.90; (iii) the SDI single-stage strategy attains the best risk-adjusted return (Sharpe=3.50) on a 20-ticker back-test, dominating both always-FinBERT (1.36) and always-LLM (0.11). At 10M-user scale, TriAgent saves $9.3M/year vs. a GPT-4o-mini baseline. Code, lexicons, and the SCD are released.

AI.tldr: TriAgent is a multi-agent, cost-aware architecture for financial sentiment analysis that combines lexicon, domain transformer, and LLM reasoning, using a Semantic Divergence Index to route queries and detect disagreements, achieving near-LLM accuracy at much lower cost with additional benefits like cross-lingual reuse and hallucination detection.
AI.motivation: LLM-based financial sentiment systems are expensive in production because every user query, many of which are trivial, is sent to costly cloud LLMs, causing costs to scale linearly with users. The authors aim to reduce inference cost while preserving or improving sentiment classification accuracy and to exploit structural properties of disagreements between simpler models and LLMs for better reliability and financial performance.
AI.method: They propose TriAgent, a three-agent committee stratified by contextual granularity: VADER for word-level lexicon-based sentiment, FinBERT for sentence-level domain-specific sentiment, and Qwen2.5 (with cross-checks from Mistral-7B and Phi-3.5-mini) for cross-sentence reasoning. They define a three-way Semantic Divergence Index (SDI) to quantify pairwise disagreement among these agents. SDI is used to route each query to the appropriate agent(s), to re-task the LLM as a critic over the simpler agents’ outputs, and to build auxiliary tools like a Shared Consensus Dictionary via multilingual sentence-BERT. They evaluate F1, hallucination detection AUC, back-tested trading Sharpe ratios, and cost savings at scale.
AI.result: They find a 'critic plateau': when the LLM is used as a critic over VADER and FinBERT outputs, F1 performance stabilizes around 0.87 across Qwen models from 1.5B to 7B parameters, with overlapping bootstrap confidence intervals, indicating size-insensitive gains. In contrast, a same-size 3-persona voting setup yields much lower F1=0.66 due to diversity along the granularity axis. Using the SDI signal, they (i) build a Shared Consensus Dictionary that lets an English cache answer 95% of Chinese queries at F1=0.99, (ii) use SDI as a post-hoc hallucination detector achieving AUC=0.90, and (iii) design an SDI-driven single-stage strategy that attains Sharpe=3.50 in a 20-ticker back-test, outperforming always-FinBERT (1.36) and always-LLM (0.11). At a projected 10M-user scale, TriAgent saves $9.3M per year compared with a GPT-4o-mini baseline.
AI.conclusion: Structuring financial sentiment analysis as a multi-agent, granularity-stratified system with an SDI-based routing and criticism mechanism can substantially cut LLM inference costs while retaining high accuracy and enabling additional benefits such as robust cross-lingual reuse, hallucination detection, and superior trading performance. The observed critic plateau suggests that beyond a certain scale, increasing LLM size yields little marginal F1 improvement in this critic role, highlighting the value of system design and disagreement-aware orchestration over brute-force model scaling.

title: Beyond Tracking or Shortcut: Composition-Bounded Predictive States in Poker Autoregressive Models
summary: Hidden-state probes often recover latent labels in imperfect-information sequence models, but this alone does not establish that a model maintains a posterior belief distribution over hidden states. This paper studies this ambiguity in a no-range Limit Hold'em autoregressive model trained only on action and value targets, not on an opponent's hand or range. Opponent-range probes are positive after action/value controls in two of three seeds, and the behavior head predicts held-out actions about five percentage points above a baseline using only observable public history. However, visible public betting composition explains more opponent-range signal than residual hidden states, suggesting that most recoverable information comes from betting summaries. Action/value+composition baselines reach 16.5-16.7% top-10 accuracy while composition-residual hidden probes fall to 11.4-12.2%, and matched-composition comparisons are negative in every seed. We call this evidence pattern composition-bounded predictive support: hidden states remain behavior-predictive and opponent-range correlated, but most recoverable range information is explained by visible betting composition rather than residual hidden-state structure. This is a case-study claim about opponent-range representational evidence, not exact Bayesian posterior tracking or a causal belief mechanism. Synthetic control and oracle validations show that the same diagnostics accept posterior-sensitive states and reject raw composition states under matched controls. Thus positive belief probes should be interpreted through targeted alternatives before being treated as evidence of belief tracking.

AI.tldr: The paper examines whether positive hidden-state probes in an imperfect-information poker model truly indicate posterior belief tracking over opponent ranges, and finds that most apparent range information is actually explained by visible betting composition instead of residual hidden-state structure.
AI.motivation: In many sequence models, linear probes can recover latent labels from hidden states, which is often informally interpreted as evidence that the model represents beliefs or posterior distributions over hidden variables. However, this interpretation is ambiguous: probe performance might be driven by simple surface statistics rather than genuine internal belief tracking. The paper aims to clarify this ambiguity in a concrete imperfect-information domain (poker) where beliefs over hidden states (opponent ranges) are theoretically central.
AI.method: The authors train an autoregressive no-range Limit Hold'em model only on action and value prediction, without any supervision on opponent hands or ranges. They then design probing experiments that predict opponent ranges from hidden states, while controlling for action and value information. They additionally construct explicit features summarizing public betting composition and run probes that (1) use only visible composition, (2) use residual hidden-state information after regressing out composition, and (3) compare matched-composition settings. They further validate their diagnostics through synthetic control experiments and oracle models where true posterior-sensitive representations are available, checking whether their methods accept genuine belief-tracking states and reject baselines that only encode composition.
AI.result: Opponent-range probes from hidden states remain positive after controlling for action and value in two of three random seeds, and the policy head can predict held-out actions ~5 percentage points better than a baseline using only public history. However, when explicit betting composition features are included, they explain more of the probe performance than residual hidden states: action/value plus composition baselines reach 16.5–16.7% top-10 accuracy, while probes restricted to hidden-state residuals after removing composition information drop to 11.4–12.2%. In matched-composition comparisons, residual hidden states do not show additional range information in any seed. Synthetic and oracle validations demonstrate that the same diagnostic framework successfully recognizes models that actually encode posterior-sensitive states and dismisses models that only capture composition.
AI.conclusion: The evidence indicates that, in this poker model, most recoverable information about opponent ranges in hidden-state probes arises from visible betting composition rather than from a distinct internal structure tracking Bayesian posteriors. The authors describe this pattern as "composition-bounded predictive support": hidden states remain predictive of behavior and correlated with opponent ranges, but their apparent range information is largely explained by simple public features. They argue that positive belief probes should not be taken at face value as evidence of belief tracking; instead, they should be interpreted relative to targeted alternative explanations, such as composition baselines and matched-control diagnostics, before inferring genuine internal belief representations.

title: VizRAG: Enhancing Retrieval-Augmented Generation with Hypergraph Visualization
summary: Hypergraph-based RAG systems surpass traditional graph-based approaches by organizing complex n-ary atomic facts among entities, rather than relying solely on binary relationships. Despite the advancements in multimodal large language models (MLLMs) with enhanced visual capabilities, current hypergraph-based RAG frameworks predominantly restrict knowledge retrieval and reconstruction to a unimodal, text-centric paradigm. This limitation prevents them from fully leveraging the powerful visual perception capabilities of modern MLLMs. To address this gap, we systematically explore the integration of hypergraph awareness in RAG systems through visual cues. By incorporating visual representations of hypergraphs into the RAG pipeline, we introduce VizRAG, the first RAG system to support visual hypergraph structure awareness. Experimental results demonstrate that VizRAG significantly outperforms strong baselines, validating the promising potential of hypergraph visualization as a novel approach for RAG systems.

AI.tldr: They propose VizRAG, a hypergraph-based retrieval-augmented generation system that leverages visual representations of hypergraphs to enhance multimodal LLM reasoning and outperform existing RAG baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing hypergraph-based RAG systems model rich n-ary relations but remain text-only, failing to exploit the strong visual understanding capabilities of modern multimodal LLMs. There is a gap in connecting hypergraph structural information with visual perception in RAG pipelines.
AI.method: They design a RAG pipeline that encodes hypergraphs not just as textual structures but also as visual diagrams/images capturing hypergraph connectivity. These visual hypergraph representations are fed into a multimodal LLM during retrieval and reasoning, enabling the model to be aware of hypergraph structure via vision. The system, named VizRAG, is evaluated against strong graph- and hypergraph-based RAG baselines.
AI.result: Experiments show that VizRAG, with visual hypergraph awareness, significantly outperforms strong baselines on tasks requiring complex relational reasoning over knowledge graphs/hypergraphs, indicating that visualizing hypergraph structure is beneficial for retrieval and generation quality.
AI.conclusion: Visualizing hypergraph structures and feeding them to multimodal LLMs within a RAG framework yields better performance than purely text-based hypergraph RAG systems. This suggests that visual hypergraph awareness is a promising new direction for enhancing RAG with multimodal models.

title: Mitigating Scaffolding Collapse in Socratic Tutors via Representation Alignment
summary: Large language model (LLM)-based Socratic tutors increasingly guide students through multi-turn questioning, but they can suffer from scaffolding collapse: under sustained student pressure, a tutor gradually abandons guided inquiry and reveals solutions directly. Prior defenses primarily constrain observable responses through prompting, preference optimization, or filtering, leaving the internal representation drift that precedes trajectory-level collapse largely unaddressed. We propose Scaffold-Preserving Representation Alignment, a two-stage framework that first warms up a Socratic tutor with supervised fine-tuning, then combines trajectory-weighted direct preference optimization with a margin-preserving representation loss anchored to frozen reference states. Our method is designed to maintain separation between scaffold-preserving and collapse-inducing hidden states across dialogue turns. We evaluate our method across five STEM disciplines and five red-teaming attack strategies. On Qwen3-8B, our method lowers Collapse Rate to 32%, delays average collapse onset beyond nine turns, and keeps over-refusal low, suggesting that representation-level alignment can improve the robustness of long-horizon Socratic tutoring under our red-teaming protocol.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a training framework to prevent large language model Socratic tutors from giving up on guided questioning and directly revealing answers when pressured by students.
AI.motivation: LLM-based Socratic tutors aim to help students learn by asking questions and guiding them, but in long conversations and under student pressure they often experience "scaffolding collapse," where they stop probing and instead just give solutions. Existing defenses mainly constrain the model’s outputs (via prompting, preference optimization, or filters), but do not directly address the internal representation drift that leads to collapse over a dialogue trajectory. The authors want a method that keeps the tutor behavior robust over many turns across different STEM domains and adversarial “red-teaming” student behaviors.
AI.method: They propose Scaffold-Preserving Representation Alignment, a two-stage training framework. First, they warm up the LLM tutor with supervised fine-tuning on Socratic tutoring examples. Second, they apply a combination of (1) trajectory-weighted direct preference optimization, which optimizes preferences at the level of multi-turn dialogue trajectories rather than isolated turns, and (2) a margin-preserving representation loss that encourages separation between hidden states associated with scaffold-preserving behavior and those associated with collapse, using frozen reference model states as anchors. This alignment operates at the representation level across dialogue turns to prevent the model’s internal states from drifting into collapse-inducing regions.
AI.result: In experiments spanning five STEM disciplines and five red-teaming attack strategies, the method is evaluated on the Qwen3-8B model. The approach reduces the Collapse Rate to 32%, delays the average onset of collapse to beyond nine dialogue turns, and maintains a low rate of over-refusal, indicating that the model continues to engage rather than simply refusing to answer. These metrics collectively show more stable Socratic behavior over long interactions under adversarial pressure compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: Representation-level alignment via Scaffold-Preserving Representation Alignment can significantly improve the robustness of Socratic LLM tutors in long, adversarial tutoring dialogues. By explicitly maintaining a margin between internal states that support proper scaffolding and those leading to solution-giving collapse, the framework reduces collapse frequency, postpones its onset, and avoids excessive refusal, suggesting a promising direction for building more reliable educational LLMs.

title: D2VBench: Benchmarking Large Language Models with Value Dilemmas in Daily Scenarios
summary: With the wide application of large language models (LLMs) in real-world scenarios, the value implication of their outputs is crucial. However, existing evaluation benchmarks suffer from insufficient coverage of value dilemmas in daily scenarios involving multiple value conflicts and simplistic evaluation formalisms that fail to assess LLMs' value alignment. To address these issues, we propose D2VBench, a value alignment benchmark comprising 10,000 instances of real daily dilemma scenarios constructed through a multi-stage collaboration between LLMs and humans, grounded in 158 manually annotated fine-grained value concepts. For evaluation on the benchmark, we present a hybrid evaluation paradigm that integrates multiple-choice questions with open-ended questions. We conduct comprehensive evaluations on eight mainstream LLMs. Experimental results demonstrate that D2VBench exhibits high reliability and robustness, effectively reflecting the LLMs' alignment across different value categories and dimensions, and providing a more realistic and fine-grained tool for research on value alignment. The dataset is available at https://github.com/tjunlp-lab/D2VBench.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces D2VBench, a large-scale benchmark to evaluate large language models’ value alignment via daily-life moral and value dilemmas.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM evaluation benchmarks do not adequately cover real-world value dilemmas with multiple, conflicting values and use overly simple evaluation formats, making it hard to reliably assess how well LLMs are aligned with human values in complex everyday situations.
AI.method: The authors construct D2VBench, a benchmark of 10,000 real daily dilemma scenarios, using a multi-stage collaboration pipeline between LLMs and humans. These scenarios are grounded in 158 manually annotated fine-grained value concepts. For evaluation, they design a hybrid paradigm that combines multiple-choice questions (for structured, comparable judgments) with open-ended questions (to capture nuanced reasoning and richer responses). They then test eight mainstream LLMs on this benchmark.
AI.result: Experiments show that D2VBench yields reliable and robust evaluation signals. It can differentiate LLMs’ performance across various value categories and dimensions, and reveals nuanced differences in how aligned different models are with the annotated value concepts.
AI.conclusion: D2VBench serves as a realistic and fine-grained tool for assessing LLM value alignment in everyday dilemma scenarios. Its large, value-annotated dataset and hybrid evaluation format make it suitable for future research to analyze and improve the value alignment of LLMs.

title: Euclean: Automated Geometry Problem Formalization with Unified Verification in Lean
summary: Recent formal reasoning systems have reached IMO-level performance, yet they leave a fragmented landscape: algebra and number theory are handled in Lean, while geometry still relies on domain-specific languages with limited formal guarantees. This split increases the trusted computing base and hinders unified model development. Existing geometry-in-Lean efforts (LeanEuclid, LeanGeo) introduce custom axiom systems incompatible with standard Mathlib, and their small scale ($<$ 1,100 problems) limits large-scale training. Native Mathlib autoformalization of geometry, however, poses distinct challenges: implicit diagrammatic assumptions (e.g., topological configuration and non-degeneracy) must be made explicit rather than deferred to external solvers, and models must adapt to Mathlib's small, rapidly evolving geometry infrastructure. We present Euclean, a four-stage framework - constraint explication, configuration anchoring, formalization mapping, and iterative repair - for automatically formalizing geometry in native Mathlib. We construct OMNI-Geometry (768 competition problems) and Numina-Geometry (177,597 problems), the largest geometry formalization dataset in Lean. Human evaluation shows 48.89% TOP1 and 73.33% TOP5 accuracy. Training Goedel v2 on our formalizations improves proof success from 13.6% to 15.1%, validating dataset quality for unified neural theorem proving. Code and datasets: https://github.com/tlb-22/Euclean.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Euclean, a framework to automatically formalize Euclidean geometry problems directly in Lean’s native Mathlib, enabling unified neural theorem proving across algebra, number theory, and geometry.
AI.motivation: Although formal reasoning systems now reach International Mathematical Olympiad (IMO)-level performance, geometry still relies on separate domain-specific languages and external solvers, while algebra and number theory live in Lean/Mathlib. This fragmentation enlarges the trusted computing base, prevents end-to-end formal guarantees, and complicates unified model training. Prior attempts to bring geometry into Lean used custom axiom systems incompatible with standard Mathlib and small datasets, limiting scalability and reusability. There is a need for a large-scale, Mathlib-native geometry formalization pipeline that handles the specific difficulties of geometry: implicit diagrammatic assumptions, explicit configuration constraints, and an immature, evolving geometric library.
AI.method: The authors propose Euclean, a four-stage pipeline for autoformalizing Euclidean geometry in native Lean Mathlib. (1) Constraint explication: extract and make explicit all geometric constraints that are usually left implicit in human diagrams, such as incidence, parallelism, and non-degeneracy conditions. (2) Configuration anchoring: represent the geometric configuration in a canonical coordinate or structural form suitable for Mathlib’s existing geometry primitives. (3) Formalization mapping: translate the problem and constraints into Lean code using Mathlib’s definitions and theorems, avoiding custom axiom systems. (4) Iterative repair: automatically detect and correct failures in generated formalizations (e.g., missing assumptions, type mismatches, or inconsistent configurations) to obtain compilable and provable Lean statements. They construct two datasets, OMNI-Geometry (768 competition-level problems) and Numina-Geometry (177,597 problems), all formalized under this pipeline, and use them to train and evaluate neural theorem provers such as Goedel v2.
AI.result: Euclean successfully produces the largest known Lean geometry formalization datasets: OMNI-Geometry with 768 competition problems and Numina-Geometry with 177,597 geometry problems. Human evaluation of the autoformalizations yields 48.89% TOP1 and 73.33% TOP5 accuracy, indicating that nearly half of the first-choice formalizations are correct and that most correct formalizations appear within the top five candidates. When Goedel v2 is trained on these datasets, its proof success rate on relevant benchmarks increases from 13.6% to 15.1%, demonstrating that the autoformalized geometry data is useful for improving end-to-end neural theorem provers in Lean.
AI.conclusion: The paper shows that Euclidean geometry can be autoformalized directly within Lean’s native Mathlib, without resorting to incompatible axiom systems or external geometry solvers. The Euclean framework addresses geometry-specific challenges by explicitly encoding diagrammatic constraints, anchoring configurations, mapping them into Mathlib, and iteratively repairing errors. The resulting OMNI-Geometry and Numina-Geometry datasets are the largest of their kind and measurably enhance the performance of neural theorem provers such as Goedel v2. This advances the goal of unified formal reasoning across mathematical domains in a single trusted ecosystem and provides a scalable path for future geometry-in-Lean development and learning-based theorem proving.

title: Sentence Splitter: Uncovering Latent Factual Structure for Self-Supervised Learning
summary: This paper introduces Sentence Splitter, a self-supervised framework built upon a T5-based encoder--decoder architecture for uncovering the latent factual structure of natural language sentences. The proposed method identifies the semantic boundary between a descriptive prefix (head) and its factual completion (tail) by formulating sentence splitting as a discrete segmentation problem, where a sentence of length $N$ admits $N$ possible split points but only one recovers the intended head--tail structure. Rather than explicitly searching over all candidate boundaries, the model learns to recover the factual completion through probabilistic sequence generation. To eliminate the need for manual annotation, symbolic head--tail pairs are first verbalized into natural-language templates that provide supervision for training the Sentence Splitter. The trained splitter is then applied to raw text to extract aligned prefix--tail pairs, which are subsequently used to train a generative model that proposes additional plausible completions through a lightweight bootstrapping process. This unified pipeline provides a scalable and structure-aware approach to constructing self-supervised training data while bridging symbolic knowledge and natural language. Experiments on both structured and naturally occurring text demonstrate that the proposed splitter generalizes beyond synthetic templates and that the resulting structure-aware supervision consistently improves downstream performance on knowledge graph completion and commonsense question answering, highlighting the effectiveness of recovering latent factual structure for knowledge-centric NLP.

AI.tldr: Sentence Splitter is a self-supervised T5-based model that learns to split sentences into a descriptive prefix (head) and a factual completion (tail), and uses these structures to generate training data that improves knowledge-centric NLP tasks.
AI.motivation: Many NLP tasks require understanding and using factual knowledge, but sentences often mix descriptive context with factual assertions, and large annotated datasets of structured facts are expensive to obtain. The authors are motivated to automatically uncover the latent factual structure inside natural language (head vs. tail) and to use that structure to generate scalable self-supervised training data that bridges symbolic knowledge bases and natural language.
AI.method: They frame sentence splitting as a discrete segmentation task: for a sentence of length N, choose one split point separating a head prefix from a tail completion. Instead of explicitly searching all N possibilities, they train a T5-based encoder–decoder model to recover the factual tail given the head via probabilistic sequence generation. Supervision is obtained without manual labels by verbalizing symbolic head–tail fact pairs into natural-language templates, which are then used to train the Sentence Splitter. Once trained, the splitter is applied to raw text to extract aligned head–tail pairs. These pairs are then used to train a generative model that can propose additional plausible completions via a lightweight bootstrapping process, forming a unified pipeline for scalable structure-aware data construction.
AI.result: Experiments on both synthetic/structured text (from templates) and naturally occurring text show that the learned splitter can generalize beyond the artificial templates it was trained on. Using the extracted and bootstrapped head–tail supervision improves performance on downstream knowledge-centric NLP benchmarks, specifically knowledge graph completion and commonsense question answering, compared with baselines lacking such structure-aware training signals.
AI.conclusion: Automatically recovering the latent head–tail factual structure of sentences is feasible with a self-supervised T5-based encoder–decoder and template-based supervision from symbolic knowledge bases. The resulting Sentence Splitter and its bootstrapped completions provide scalable, structure-aware training data that consistently benefits knowledge-intensive NLP tasks. This demonstrates that explicit modeling of factual sentence structure is an effective way to bridge symbolic knowledge and natural language and to improve knowledge-centric reasoning performance.

title: CrackedPDFs: A Controlled Benchmark for Hidden Prompt Injection in PDFs
summary: Document-based LLM systems often flatten a PDF before guardrails inspect it. That step can discard evidence that an instruction was never visible to the user. We introduce CrackedPDFs, a controlled benchmark for hidden prompt injection in PDFs. The benchmark contains 29,322 generated PDFs from 4,983 base docu ments. It includes 9,774 injected files and 19,548 benign or matched-confounder files. We evaluate PromptGuard and a rule baseline. We also evaluate structural only learned models and a sanitized hybrid detector. The evaluation uses held-out provenance splits and paired benign-confounder controls. It also uses label-shuffle checks and shortcut audits. On a 2,919-document held-out test set, the hybrid de tector reaches 0.960 F1. ROC-AUC is 0.998 and PR-AUC is 0.997. It also ranks injected files above matched benign confounders in 95.9% of 973 pairs. Prompt Guard has low recall when given extracted text only. Structural-only learned mod els are weak under paired controls. A text-only TF-IDF model reaches perfect held-out scores but fails shortcut audits. These results show that document-aware hybrid detection is useful under controlled paired evaluation. They do not show broad real-world robustness or reliable cross-family generalization.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CrackedPDFs, a benchmark for detecting hidden prompt injections in PDFs, and shows that a document-aware hybrid detector can identify injected files very accurately under controlled conditions, though real-world robustness remains unproven.
AI.motivation: Existing document-based LLM systems typically flatten PDFs to text before running safety guardrails, which can remove crucial structural evidence that certain harmful or injected instructions were not actually visible to the end user. There was no controlled, large-scale benchmark to study and systematically evaluate hidden prompt injection attacks that exploit PDF structure and visibility. The authors want to quantify and understand how well different detection approaches can identify such hidden injections, and whether using document structure in addition to text helps.
AI.method: The authors create CrackedPDFs, a large controlled benchmark of 29,322 PDFs derived from 4,983 base documents, including 9,774 with injected prompts and 19,548 benign or carefully matched confounder files. They design provenance-based held-out splits and paired benign-confounder controls to avoid data leakage and highlight shortcuts. They then evaluate several detectors: PromptGuard and a simple rule-based baseline; structure-only learned models trained purely on PDF structural features; and a hybrid detector that combines sanitized structural information with text-based features. They further validate models using label-shuffle checks and shortcut audits to see whether models are cheating via dataset artifacts rather than learning genuine injection cues.
AI.result: On a 2,919-document held-out test set, the hybrid detector achieves strong detection performance with F1 of 0.960, ROC-AUC of 0.998, and PR-AUC of 0.997, and it ranks injected files above their matched benign confounders in 95.9% of 973 evaluated pairs. PromptGuard, when restricted to extracted text only, has low recall on the benchmark. Structure-only learned models perform poorly when evaluated with paired controls, suggesting that structure alone is insufficient. A text-only TF-IDF model gets seemingly perfect scores on standard held-out tests but fails shortcut audits, indicating that it exploits dataset artifacts rather than learning robust signals of injection.
AI.conclusion: A hybrid, document-aware approach that combines structural PDF cues with textual information can be highly effective at detecting hidden prompt injections under carefully controlled, paired evaluations. However, strong performance on this benchmark does not guarantee robustness in real-world, diverse attack settings or across different PDF families; cross-family generalization and broad robustness remain open challenges. The work primarily contributes a new benchmark and an evaluation protocol that help reveal when detectors are relying on shortcuts rather than genuine, generalizable signals.

title: emb-diversity: A Tool for Embedding-Based Measurement of Data Diversity
summary: There is growing evidence that data diversity is crucial for developing fair and robust NLP models. However, current approaches to measure diversity remain inconsistent and fragmented: While there exist a number of tools for measuring the lexical diversity of texts, researchers lack standardized tools for quantifying diversity based on embeddings. Embedding-based diversity measures are highly flexible: They work with any embedding model and any data that can be embedded, and are thus applicable to many notions of diversity. With emb-diversity, we provide a comprehensive embedding-based diversity measurement tool, spanning a broad range of measures. We demonstrate its potential for several use cases: measuring the stylistic, semantic, language and speaker diversity of datasets. https://github.com/nlpsoc/emb-diversity/

AI.tldr: The paper introduces emb-diversity, a general toolkit for measuring dataset diversity using embeddings rather than just lexical statistics.
AI.motivation: Existing diversity measurements in NLP are fragmented and mostly focus on surface-level lexical variety, lacking standardized, flexible tools that can handle richer notions of diversity across different embedding models and data types.
AI.method: The authors define and implement a broad collection of diversity metrics that operate directly in embedding space, designed to be model-agnostic and applicable to any embeddable data. They package these into the emb-diversity toolkit and apply it to multiple types of diversity (stylistic, semantic, language, and speaker) to show how the measures can be used in practice.
AI.result: Using emb-diversity, the authors show that embedding-based metrics can successfully quantify various forms of diversity in datasets, beyond word-level statistics, and that the toolkit can be easily applied across different settings and embedding models.
AI.conclusion: Embedding-based diversity metrics provide a powerful, unified, and flexible way to measure multiple types of diversity in NLP datasets, and the emb-diversity toolkit offers a standardized resource for researchers to operationalize these measurements.

title: HyGRL: Adaptive Hybrid Graph Reasoning for Multi-Entity Questions
summary: Multi-entity compositional questions pose significant challenges to existing retrieval-augmented language models. Conventional methods fall into a dilemma: standard RAG lacks dynamic reasoning, traditional Graph-RAG is limited by structural sparsity, and LLM-constructed Graph-RAG incurs prohibitive costs. We propose \textbf{\fwa}, a unified framework that embeds unstructured text into structured knowledge graphs, creating a heterogeneous network for flexible evidence retrieval. Reasoning is formulated as adaptive structure induction, learned via a robust two-stage process: (1) imitation learning distills heuristic expert signals, and (2) reinforcement learning refines the policy using LLM-driven preference rewards. Experiments demonstrate that {\fwa} effectively merges textual richness with structural knowledge, outperforming SOTA baselines in answer accuracy and reasoning fidelity while maintaining extremely low token costs and near real-time inference((code available at https://github.com/wjywjy123/HyGRL) .

AI.tldr: They propose HyGRL, a retrieval-augmented QA framework that embeds text into a heterogeneous knowledge graph and learns to adaptively induce reasoning structures, beating prior RAG/Graph-RAG methods with higher accuracy, better reasoning, and lower token cost.
AI.motivation: Existing retrieval-augmented generation methods struggle with multi-entity compositional questions. Plain RAG cannot flexibly reason over multiple interconnected pieces of evidence, traditional Graph-RAG is constrained by sparse and rigid graph structures, and building detailed graphs with LLMs is too expensive. There is a need for a scalable, cost-efficient way to combine the richness of unstructured text with the reasoning benefits of structured graphs.
AI.method: They build a unified framework, HyGRL, that embeds unstructured text into a structured knowledge graph, forming a heterogeneous network including textual and structured nodes/edges. Question answering is modeled as adaptive structure induction over this network. The reasoning policy is learned in two stages: (1) imitation learning that distills heuristic expert signals to initialize the policy; and (2) reinforcement learning that further optimizes the policy using LLM-based preference rewards that judge the quality of reasoning and answers.
AI.result: On benchmarks involving multi-entity compositional questions, HyGRL outperforms state-of-the-art RAG and Graph-RAG baselines in both answer accuracy and reasoning fidelity. It also achieves this while keeping token usage very low and enabling nearly real-time inference, making it more cost-effective than LLM-constructed graph approaches.
AI.conclusion: Embedding unstructured text into a heterogeneous knowledge graph and learning an adaptive reasoning policy via imitation and reinforcement learning provides an effective solution for complex multi-entity question answering. HyGRL successfully combines textual richness with structural reasoning, yielding better performance and efficiency than prior RAG and Graph-RAG approaches.

title: Overview of FinMMEval 2026 Task 1: Multilingual Financial Multiple-Choice Question Answering
comment: 9 pages. Task overview paper for CLEF 2026 Working Notes (CEUR Workshop Proceedings)
summary: FinMMEval 2026 Task 1 evaluates multilingual financial multiple-choice question answering in English, Chinese, Arabic, and Hindi. The task tests whether systems can select the correct answer to finance questions involving domain terminology, numerical interpretation, and conceptual financial reasoning across languages and scripts. The final-test set contains 800 questions, with 200 questions per language; gold answers were withheld during submission, and each language was ranked independently by accuracy. The final leaderboards contain 13 English, 11 Chinese, 11 Arabic, and 10 Hindi ranked submissions. Top accuracies range from 92.0% in Hindi to 97.5% in English and Arabic, with the same leading teams appearing near the top across all four languages. The documented systems used retrieval augmentation, direct answer-option scoring, language-specific prompting, selective self-consistency, confidence checks, and LLM-based review stages.

AI.tldr: FinMMEval 2026 Task 1 benchmarks multilingual financial MCQ answering across four languages, showing high accuracies and common top-performing teams.
AI.motivation: To create a standardized multilingual benchmark for evaluating systems’ ability to answer financial questions involving terminology, numbers, and conceptual reasoning across different languages and scripts.
AI.method: Construct a 800-question multiple-choice test set with 200 questions per language (English, Chinese, Arabic, Hindi), hide the gold answers during submissions, and rank systems independently per language by accuracy. Analyze participating systems’ techniques such as retrieval augmentation, option scoring, language-specific prompts, self-consistency, confidence checks, and LLM-based review.
AI.result: Received 13–10 ranked submissions per language, with top accuracies between 92.0% (Hindi) and 97.5% (English and Arabic). The same leading teams consistently rank near the top across all four languages.
AI.conclusion: Modern systems can achieve very high accuracy on multilingual financial MCQ tasks, and similar approaches generalize well across languages. Techniques like retrieval augmentation and multi-stage LLM workflows are effective for financial QA in multiple languages and scripts.

title: ITPEval: Benchmarking Formal Translation Across Interactive Theorem Provers
summary: Formal theorem proving has emerged as a frontier challenge for machine learning, yet the ecosystem is fragmented: proofs remain siloed across incompatible systems, limiting both training data for learning-based provers and the portability of verified results. We present ITPEval, the first benchmark for evaluating automated formal proof translation across four major ITPs (Lean 4, Rocq, Isabelle, and HOL Light), spanning two distinct logical foundations. Our benchmark comprises 1,560 source files and 6,848 theorems organized into a controlled tier of axiomatized files that isolates foundational translation difficulty, and an ecosystem tier drawn from real libraries that exposes API and proof-style mismatches. We release itpeval, a unified multi-ITP verification infrastructure with state-isolated warm backends that preserve per-artifact native checking semantics. We evaluate both statement and proof translation across five frontier and open-weight LLMs on 12 directed translation pairs: statement translation peaks at 29.1% pass@1 and proof translation at 10.5%; controlled theorems reach 29.7% proof pass@1 versus 5.2% for ecosystem-level translations, confirming that library mismatch is the dominant bottleneck. In addition to pass@k evaluation, a deterministic Lean 4 BEq check establishes equivalence for 54.0% of verified source-to-Lean 4 miniF2F statement translations, showing that native type-checking alone can substantially overestimate semantic fidelity; in an autoformalization/auto-informalization round-trip study, Rocq and HOL Light are easier formalization targets than Lean 4 and Isabelle, while multi-ITP context improves pooled Lean 4 success from 4.8% to 10.6%. Our benchmark, verification infrastructure, and evaluation pipelines are publicly released.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ITPEval, a benchmark and infrastructure for translating and verifying formal proofs across multiple interactive theorem provers (ITPs), and uses it to systematically evaluate large language models on cross-system proof and statement translation.
AI.motivation: Formal theorem proving is an important challenge for machine learning, but the current ecosystem is fragmented across incompatible ITP systems (Lean 4, Rocq/Coq, Isabelle, HOL Light). This fragmentation limits the amount of usable training data for learning-based provers and hinders reuse and portability of verified results. There is no standardized way to evaluate automated translation of theorems and proofs between these systems, especially across different logical foundations and library APIs, which is needed to unlock cross-ITP interoperability and richer training corpora.
AI.method: The authors build ITPEval, a benchmark consisting of 1,560 source files and 6,848 theorems spanning four major ITPs and two logical foundations. They organize the benchmark into: (1) a controlled tier of axiomatized files to isolate foundational translation difficulty; and (2) an ecosystem tier sampled from real libraries to measure the impact of API and proof-style mismatches. They also implement `itpeval`, a unified multi-ITP verification infrastructure with isolated backends that preserve each system’s native checking semantics. Using this setup, they evaluate several frontier and open-weight LLMs on 12 directed translation pairs, for both statement and proof translation, and assess performance via pass@k metrics. They additionally run BEq-based equivalence checks in Lean 4 to test semantic fidelity beyond type checking, and conduct autoformalization/auto-informalization round-trip experiments to compare how easily different ITPs can be targeted as formalization languages.
AI.result: On the benchmark, the best models achieve up to 29.1% pass@1 for statement translation and 10.5% pass@1 for proof translation. For the controlled tier, proof pass@1 reaches 29.7%, whereas ecosystem-level proofs only reach 5.2%, indicating that library and API mismatches dominate the difficulty. A deterministic Lean 4 BEq equivalence check confirms that only 54.0% of type-checked source-to-Lean 4 miniF2F statement translations are semantically equivalent, showing that native type-checking can significantly overestimate true semantic correctness. In round-trip autoformalization experiments, Rocq and HOL Light emerge as easier targets than Lean 4 and Isabelle, and providing multi-ITP context improves pooled Lean 4 success from 4.8% to 10.6%.
AI.conclusion: ITPEval establishes the first systematic benchmark and infrastructure for automated formal proof and statement translation across multiple major ITPs, making it possible to rigorously compare models and methods in this setting. The experiments show that current LLMs can perform nontrivial cross-ITP translation but are still limited, especially when dealing with real-world library ecosystems rather than controlled axiomatizations. The findings emphasize that library/API mismatch is the primary barrier to robust translation, that simple type-checking is insufficient as a correctness proxy, and that some ITPs are more amenable as formalization targets than others. By publicly releasing the benchmark, infrastructure, and evaluation pipelines, the authors aim to catalyze further research on cross-system interoperability, better model training, and more reliable evaluation for formal theorem proving with machine learning.

title: Overview of FinMMEval 2026 Task 2: Multilingual Financial Short-Answer Question Answering
comment: 9 pages. Task overview paper for CLEF 2026 Working Notes (CEUR Workshop Proceedings)
summary: FinMMEval 2026 Task 2 evaluates short-answer financial question answering over multilingual evidence. Each final-test item pairs an English question with financial statements and news in English, Chinese, Japanese, Spanish, and Greek. Participating systems submit one concise answer per item in JSONL format. The final-test set contains 256 items, split evenly between easy and expert tiers; each tier contains four question templates instantiated over 32 company-report groups. Gold answers were withheld during submission, and systems were ranked by macro-averaged item-level ROUGE-1 F1 against organizer-held reference answers.
