The final leaderboard includes 12 ranked submissions. The strongest systems are closely clustered, with the top four separated by less than one percentage point in ROUGE-1 F1. The submitted system papers document retrieval-augmented generation, cross-lingual evidence handling, structured prompting, answer compression, and validation strategies.

AI.tldr: FinMMEval 2026 Task 2 benchmarks short-answer financial QA over multilingual (English, Chinese, Japanese, Spanish, Greek) evidence, using ROUGE-1 F1 on 256 expert/easy items, revealing tightly clustered top systems and diverse retrieval-augmented, cross-lingual, and prompting-based methods.
AI.motivation: To create a standardized, realistic benchmark for evaluating systems that answer concise financial questions using multilingual financial statements and news, bridging English questions with evidence in multiple languages. This addresses the need for robust, cross-lingual financial QA systems that can integrate heterogeneous sources and support global financial analysis.
AI.method: The task defines 256 test items, each an English question paired with multilingual financial evidence; items are divided into easy and expert tiers, each tier using four question templates applied to 32 company-report groups. Systems output single concise answers in JSONL. Gold answers are withheld, and evaluation uses macro-averaged item-level ROUGE-1 F1 against organizer reference answers to build a leaderboard of 12 final submissions.
AI.result: Twelve systems were submitted and ranked by macro-averaged ROUGE-1 F1. Performance at the top is very close, with the top four systems within one ROUGE-1 F1 point of each other, indicating a competitive field and no dramatically dominant approach.
AI.conclusion: FinMMEval 2026 Task 2 offers a challenging multilingual financial QA benchmark with balanced easy/expert tiers and standardized evaluation. The close clustering of top systems suggests that several retrieval-augmented and cross-lingual prompting strategies are comparably effective, and further advances may depend on improved multilingual evidence handling, answer compression, and validation techniques.

title: FORCE-Bench: A Benchmark, Dataset, and Evaluation Harness for Agentic AI in Enterprise Finance
summary: Recent advances in large language models have accelerated deployment of agentic systems in operational finance. Existing benchmarks emphasize measuring general capabilities, instruction following, or safety, but few directly address the operational finance workflows that agentic systems are now being deployed to automate. Finance professionals require agents to not only provide factually sound and properly grounded information, but also ensure that this information is verifiable and consistently adheres to rules and constraints of the operational finance domain. We introduce FORCE-Bench, which contains 251 expert-annotated queries and evaluates responses using a rubric-based framework calibrated to the requirements of the operational finance domain, across eight dimensions: accuracy, citations, clarity, depth, groundedness, recency, relevance, and structure. FORCE-Bench assesses agentic systems on three task types: financial obligation research (querying ERP systems for accounts receivable and payable data), financial entity performance research (answering time-bound questions from public filings and market data), and business brief generation (synthesising multi-source company intelligence reports). To reflect real deployment conditions, we evaluate our purpose-built agent, as well as the general-purpose agentic systems, under common tool access and latency-bounded settings. Results show that general-purpose agentic systems do not consistently meet finance-domain quality requirements under operational constraints, while the purpose-built Finance Agent for Microsoft 365 Copilot is more reliable across dimensions. We release the dataset, rubrics, harness, and analysis code as open-source to support reproducible comparison and adaptation to other enterprise finance environments.

AI.tldr: The paper presents FORCE-Bench, a domain-specific benchmark to evaluate large language model agents on real-world operational finance tasks using an expert-designed, rubric-based framework.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM and agent benchmarks focus on generic capabilities, instruction following, or safety, but do not capture the stringent workflow, quality, and verification requirements of operational finance, where outputs must be accurate, grounded in specific financial systems and documents, verifiable, and compliant with domain rules and constraints. There is a need for a benchmark that reflects how finance professionals actually use agentic systems in production.
AI.method: The authors create FORCE-Bench, a benchmark of 251 expert-annotated queries derived from operational finance workflows. They define an evaluation rubric tailored to operational finance, scoring responses along eight dimensions: accuracy, citations, clarity, depth, groundedness, recency, relevance, and structure. They design three task categories: (1) financial obligation research, which involves querying ERP systems for accounts receivable/payable data; (2) financial entity performance research, which involves time-bound questions using public filings and market data; and (3) business brief generation, which synthesizes intelligence from multiple company information sources. They then evaluate a purpose-built finance agent (Finance Agent for Microsoft 365 Copilot) and several general-purpose agentic systems under realistic conditions, including shared tool access and bounded latency.
AI.result: Under these realistic, deployment-like settings, general-purpose agentic systems often fail to satisfy the strict quality requirements of operational finance across the eight rubric dimensions. In contrast, the purpose-built Finance Agent for Microsoft 365 Copilot shows more consistent and reliable performance across those dimensions on the FORCE-Bench tasks.
AI.conclusion: Domain-specific, rubric-based evaluation is necessary to accurately assess whether agentic LLM systems meet the operational needs of enterprise finance. General-purpose agents that perform well on generic benchmarks may still fall short in real finance workflows, whereas a specialized finance agent can better satisfy domain constraints and quality expectations. The authors release FORCE-Bench, including dataset, rubrics, harness, and analysis code, as open-source infrastructure to enable reproducible benchmarking and adaptation to other enterprise finance contexts.

title: Efficient Chain-of-Modality Reasoning via Progressive Compression for Spoken Language Models
summary: Spoken language models (SLMs) enable natural human-computer interaction, but their reasoning ability still lags behind that of text-based large language models, especially on spoken mathematical question answering tasks. One important reason is that SLMs reason over purely verbalized mathematical expressions, which are harder to interpret than symbolic text. However, directly transferring text-based reasoning to SLMs is nontrivial due to architectural constraints and the additional computational requirements. To address this challenge, we propose Efficient Chain-of-Modality Reasoning (ECoM Reasoning), the first framework to introduce compressed reasoning into SLMs. By compressing the textual component so that it jointly serves as speech guidance and reasoning representation, ECoM Reasoning improves reasoning accuracy while using a smaller token budget than the standard Chain-of-Modality (CoM) architecture, which generates intermediate text before speech. To train this capability, we further propose Progressive Compression, a curriculum-based strategy that gradually trains the model from full-form reasoning to compressed reasoning. Experiments on spoken mathematical question answering benchmarks show that ECoM Reasoning improves accuracy by 21% over standard CoM without explicit reasoning, and by 3% over CoM with full reasoning traces while using only 40% of the text tokens, demonstrating that it enhances SLM reasoning while remaining inference-efficient.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes ECoM Reasoning, a framework that compresses textual reasoning in spoken language models to improve spoken math QA accuracy while using fewer tokens than existing Chain-of-Modality approaches.
AI.motivation: Spoken language models lag behind text-based LLMs in reasoning, especially for spoken math QA, because they must reason over purely verbalized math expressions and face architectural and computational limits when trying to reuse text-style reasoning. There is a need for a way to inject strong reasoning into SLMs without incurring large token and compute costs.
AI.method: They introduce Efficient Chain-of-Modality (ECoM) Reasoning, which compresses the textual reasoning so it doubles as both guidance for speech generation and an internal reasoning representation, instead of generating full intermediate text. They also propose Progressive Compression, a curriculum-training strategy that starts with full-form reasoning traces and gradually trains the model to handle more compressed reasoning representations.
AI.result: On spoken mathematical QA benchmarks, ECoM Reasoning yields a 21% accuracy improvement over a standard Chain-of-Modality baseline that has no explicit reasoning, and a 3% improvement over a CoM model that uses full reasoning traces, while requiring only 40% of the text tokens for reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Compressed reasoning within SLMs via ECoM Reasoning can significantly enhance spoken reasoning performance while remaining efficient in token usage and inference cost, making it a practical approach for boosting SLM reasoning ability on tasks like spoken mathematical QA.

title: The Chronos Vulnerability: A Taxonomy of Temporal Persistence and Memory-Based Deception in Agentic AI
summary: The transition from stateless generative models in artificial intelligence to stateful, autonomous agents represents an architectural evolution that, while providing the capabilities of long-term planning and the automation of enterprise workflows, also represents the introduction of a new form of security threat, the Chronos Vulnerability. The Chronos Vulnerability represents the threat of memory-based attacks, including the Memory Injection Attack (MINJA) and the sleeper agent, in which the internal belief system of the autonomous agent is compromised, effectively decoupling the attack vector from the final catastrophic event. This study formalizes the threat model for persistence-based attacks and the threat of Dynamics Blindness in the context of the World of Workflows benchmark, demonstrating that traditional endpoint content filters are insufficient for the current stateful architecture. Consequently, this study synthesizes a defense-in-depth landscape, categorizing emerging frameworks such as diagnostic trajectory guardrails (AgentDoG), formal temporal verification (Agent-C), immunological memory consensus (A-MemGuard), and hardware-anchored trust via GPU-based Trusted Execution Environments (TEEs) and Zero-Trust memory architectures.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces and analyzes a new class of security risk, the Chronos Vulnerability, in stateful AI agents and surveys a layered defense landscape against memory-based attacks.
AI.motivation: As AI moves from stateless generative models to stateful, autonomous agents with memory and long-term planning, new security risks arise that are not well covered by existing endpoint content filters and traditional threat models. There is a need to formalize and understand persistence- and memory-based attacks on agents and to map out systematic defenses.
AI.method: The authors formalize a threat model for persistence-based and memory-based attacks—such as Memory Injection Attacks (MINJA) and sleeper agents—within the World of Workflows benchmark. They analyze how these attacks exploit agent memory and internal belief systems, and they characterize the phenomenon of Dynamics Blindness. Based on this analysis, they synthesize and categorize emerging defense mechanisms into a defense-in-depth framework, including diagnostic trajectory guardrails (AgentDoG), formal temporal verification (Agent-C), immunological memory consensus (A-MemGuard), and hardware-rooted trust using GPU-based TEEs and Zero-Trust memory architectures.
AI.result: They show that in the World of Workflows setting, traditional endpoint content filtering is inadequate to detect or mitigate Chronos-style, persistence-based attacks because the attack vector can be temporally and causally decoupled from the eventual harmful action. The paper produces a structured taxonomy of defenses (software and hardware) aligned to different stages and aspects of the attack lifecycle.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that stateful autonomous AI agents introduce a distinct security vulnerability—Chronos Vulnerability—rooted in memory and temporal persistence, requiring new, layered defenses beyond standard content filters. It argues for defense-in-depth approaches that combine trajectory-level diagnostics, formal temporal reasoning, consensus over memory, and hardware-anchored trust to robustly mitigate memory-based attacks and Dynamics Blindness in real-world workflow automation scenarios.

title: A Multi-Dimensional Evaluation of Explainability in Media Bias Detection
summary: Detecting media bias automatically is difficult because biased framing is often subtle, yet in domains such as news analysis, accurate predictions alone are insufficient without explanations that reflect the model's underlying reasoning. We present a multi-dimensional evaluation of explainability in encoder-based media bias detection using the Bias Annotations By Experts (BABE) dataset. Specifically, we study BERT and RoBERTa as classifiers (base and large variants) along three complementary axes: predictive performance, explanation plausibility (token-level alignment with expert rationales), and mechanistic faithfulness (whether compact sets of attention heads recover predictive signal under counterfactual rationale masking). To induce variation in plausibility, we additionally investigate attention-supervised finetuning, which incorporates expert rationale annotations as an auxiliary training signal. Attention supervision serves as an intervention on attribution plausibility, while the effectiveness of attribution methods varies substantially across architectures. Circuit analysis further reveals substantial variation in mechanistic recoverability across architectures, suggesting that model scale alone does not determine circuit compressibility. Taken together, our findings suggest that predictive performance, attribution plausibility, and mechanistic faithfulness characterize different aspects of model behavior and should be evaluated separately when studying explainability in media bias detection.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how well transformer-based models (BERT/RoBERTa) explain their decisions when detecting media bias, showing that accuracy, explanation plausibility, and mechanistic faithfulness are distinct and must be assessed separately.
AI.motivation: Automatic detection of media bias is challenging because bias is often subtle, and in practical contexts, high predictive accuracy is not enough: users need explanations that reflect a model’s actual reasoning. Existing work often treats explainability superficially and does not rigorously connect model attention, rationales, and internal mechanisms, especially for media bias detection.
AI.method: Using the BABE dataset with expert bias annotations, the authors fine-tune BERT and RoBERTa (base and large) as bias classifiers. They evaluate models along three axes: (1) predictive performance; (2) explanation plausibility, measured as token-level overlap between model attributions (primarily attention) and expert rationales; and (3) mechanistic faithfulness, tested via circuit-style analysis where they identify compact subsets of attention heads and assess if they retain predictive signal under counterfactual rationale masking. They also apply attention supervision, where expert rationales guide attention during training, to study its impact on plausibility and mechanisms.
AI.result: The study finds that attention supervision improves attribution plausibility but that different architectures and sizes respond differently. Attribution methods vary in how effectively they capture expert rationales, and the degree to which model behavior can be captured by small sets of attention heads (circuit compressibility) differs notably across BERT vs RoBERTa and base vs large variants. High predictive accuracy does not guarantee high plausibility or mechanistic faithfulness.
AI.conclusion: Explainability in media bias detection is multi-faceted. Predictive performance, how human-plausible a model’s explanations are, and how faithfully those explanations reflect internal mechanisms are only weakly coupled and should be evaluated separately. Model scale does not automatically yield better, more compressible internal circuits. Attention supervision can steer models toward more plausible attributions, but does not uniformly resolve mechanistic faithfulness issues across architectures.

title: Sophisticated Policies from Epistemic Priors
summary: Sophisticated Inference is a variant of active inference often associated with recursive belief modeling and tree search. We argue that its central computational role is simpler: within a planning horizon, it makes active inference closed-loop by allowing future actions to depend on future states and observations. This closed-loop structure can be represented in the epistemic-prior variational free energy framework. Epistemic priors supply the active-inference objective, while a joint posterior over future states and actions supplies the state-contingent control structure. We evaluate this decomposition in the Reactivity Maze, a stochastic benchmark designed to separate epistemic incentive from inner-horizon closed-loop control. The comparison includes three variational objectives with the same state-action posterior family, an action-state factorized active inference objective, Sophisticated Inference, and standard Expected Free Energy planning. The results show that neither ingredient is sufficient on its own. Methods without an epistemic component do not seek information, while methods that prevent future actions from depending on future states cannot turn information into reliable goal-reaching. By contrast, both Sophisticated Inference and full-joint epistemic-prior active inference solve the environment by combining epistemic drive with closed-loop inference. These results show that the advantage associated with Sophisticated Inference need not be specific to tree search itself. It arises from the closed-loop form of active inference, and this form can be represented in epistemic-prior variational inference when the posterior keeps future actions dependent on future states.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that the advantage of Sophisticated Inference in active inference comes from making planning truly closed-loop within the planning horizon, not from tree search per se.
AI.motivation: Active inference agents often either lack proper information-seeking incentives (no epistemic drive) or use open-loop control within a planning horizon, where future actions cannot depend on future observations. Sophisticated Inference methods are known to perform better, but it is unclear whether this is due to recursive belief modeling/tree search or something more basic. The authors want to clarify what computational ingredient is crucial and how it fits in the epistemic-prior variational free energy framework.
AI.method: They analyze Sophisticated Inference in terms of the epistemic-prior free energy formulation, decomposing it into (1) an epistemic prior that provides an information-seeking objective, and (2) a joint posterior over future states and actions that encodes closed-loop, state-contingent control. They then empirically compare several approaches in a specially designed stochastic benchmark environment, the Reactivity Maze, which separates the need for epistemic exploration from the need for inner-horizon closed-loop control. The compared methods include: three different variational objectives sharing the same state-action posterior family, a factorized (action-state independent) active inference objective, Sophisticated Inference, and standard Expected Free Energy planning.
AI.result: In the Reactivity Maze, algorithms lacking an epistemic term fail to explore and thus do not seek information, while algorithms that block dependence of future actions on future states fail to exploit acquired information for reliable goal-reaching. In contrast, Sophisticated Inference and a full-joint epistemic-prior active inference formulation successfully solve the task by jointly enabling epistemic drive and closed-loop control within the planning horizon.
AI.conclusion: The key performance benefit typically attributed to Sophisticated Inference does not fundamentally depend on tree search or recursive belief modeling. Instead, it comes from implementing an intrinsically closed-loop form of active inference over the planning horizon, where the posterior maintains dependence of future actions on future states. This closed-loop structure can be realized within the epistemic-prior variational free energy framework via joint state-action posteriors.

title: When Does Knowledge Distillation Hurt? Reliability-Aware Distillation for Low-Resource Language Summarization
summary: Knowledge distillation (KD) is a standard approach for compressing sequence-to-sequence models, but its per-sample effects are rarely examined. On the BanSum Bangla summarization benchmark, we find that standard KD improves ROUGE-L by only +0.0003 over a cross-entropy baseline, and that approximately 51.3% of training samples are estimated to actively harm student validation loss under standard KD. We propose two complementary reliability-aware distillation methods. CHAD (Counterfactual Harm-Aware Distillation) measures per-sample KD usefulness via gradient alignment with the validation loss direction and trains a lightweight gate that generalizes this counterfactual judgment to the full training set. EWAD+CPDP combines token-level entropy-weighted adaptive distillation with a capacity-proportional geometric constraint from a second, vocabulary-incompatible teacher. On BanSum, both methods substantially outperform standard KD: CHAD by +0.0173 ROUGE-L and EWAD+CPDP by +0.0219 ROUGE-L, where standard KD itself improves ROUGE-L by only +0.0003; despite using only 60M parameters, both outperform a fine-tuned Qwen 2.5-3B model (50x larger). We further evaluate the stronger method, EWAD+CPDP, across 15 typologically diverse XL-Sum languages organised into three sets, beating the CE-only baseline on 10/15 languages; gains are most reliable where the two teachers contribute complementary signal, and weakest where they have saturated or jointly weak target-language coverage. We release code and trained models to support reproducibility and further research on selective distillation.

AI.tldr: The paper finds that standard knowledge distillation barely helps and can even hurt many samples in seq2seq summarization, and proposes two reliability-aware distillation methods that selectively use teacher signals, significantly improving performance, especially in low-resource multilingual settings.
AI.motivation: While knowledge distillation is widely used to compress sequence-to-sequence models, its impact is usually measured only on average, ignoring that some training samples may actually harm the student when distilled. On the BanSum Bangla summarization benchmark, standard KD yields almost no ROUGE-L improvement and many samples appear harmful, motivating methods that can detect and avoid unhelpful distillation signals on a per-sample basis and better exploit multiple teachers.
AI.method: 1) CHAD (Counterfactual Harm-Aware Distillation) estimates for each sample whether KD is beneficial by examining gradient alignment between the KD objective and the validation loss direction, then trains a lightweight gating network to generalize this counterfactual decision and selectively apply KD only where helpful. 2) EWAD+CPDP combines (a) entropy-weighted adaptive distillation, which adjusts token-level distillation strength based on teacher confidence (entropy), with (b) a capacity-proportional geometric constraint that distills representation geometry from a second teacher whose vocabulary is incompatible, using a scaling that matches teacher-student capacity. Both methods are evaluated in seq2seq summarization with small students and larger teachers.
AI.result: On the BanSum Bangla summarization dataset, standard KD improves ROUGE-L by only +0.0003 over a cross-entropy baseline and an estimated 51.3% of samples are harmful under standard KD. CHAD improves ROUGE-L by +0.0173 and EWAD+CPDP by +0.0219 over the same baseline, both using only 60M parameters yet outperforming a much larger 3B-parameter Qwen 2.5 model. EWAD+CPDP is further evaluated on 15 XL-Sum languages in three groups, outperforming CE-only training on 10 of 15 languages, with gains strongest where the teachers provide complementary information and weaker where the teachers are saturated or jointly weak for the target language.
AI.conclusion: Standard KD for seq2seq summarization can be minimally effective and even harmful on many training samples, so distillation must be made reliability-aware and selective. The proposed CHAD and EWAD+CPDP methods effectively identify and exploit helpful teacher signals, yielding substantial improvements over standard KD and even much larger models, and generalize to many languages when teacher signals are complementary. The work underscores the importance of per-sample analysis and selective distillation, releasing code and models to support further work in this direction.

title: Knowledge-Centric Self-Improvement
summary: Self-improving AI systems typically treat the agent as the object that improves, by optimizing prompts, workflows, harnesses, or even the agent's own code. This agent-centric view can make improvements expensive to maintain and difficult to transfer, because gains become tied to a particular agent design, task distribution, or adaptation run. We study a complementary paradigm: knowledge-centric self-improvement, in which agents remain generic and disposable while the persistent object is a curated knowledge base that agents can leverage for future tasks. We conduct controlled case studies to operationalize this idea via a simple protocol. Agents attempt one task, then contribute evidence-grounded insights to a shared knowledge base via task-level and cross-task forums, followed by knowledge distillation. Because self-improvement is contained in the knowledge rather than the agent, improvement can be more inspectable, transferable, and portable. Across abstract reasoning, coding, and terminal benchmarks, this protocol improves solve rates while reducing dollar cost relative to agent-centric baselines. The resulting distilled knowledge also transfers to held-out tasks and across LLM families, indicating that the improvement is not merely an LLM- or run-specific behavior. These results support a new view of self-improving agentic systems: progress can be driven primarily by the curated persistent knowledge. Code is available at https://github.com/recursive-knowledge/KSI.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a knowledge-centric approach to self-improving AI, where a shared curated knowledge base, not the agent itself, is the main object of improvement, leading to better task performance and transfer across models and tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing self-improving AI focuses on modifying the agent—its prompts, workflows, or code—making improvements costly to maintain, tied to specific agents or runs, and hard to transfer. The authors want a more stable, portable way for systems to accumulate improvements over time that generalize across tasks, settings, and model families.
AI.method: They introduce a simple protocol for knowledge-centric self-improvement: (1) generic agents attempt tasks; (2) after each task, agents contribute evidence-grounded insights to a shared knowledge base via task-level and cross-task forums; (3) these contributions are curated and distilled into persistent, structured knowledge that future agents can use. They run controlled case studies on benchmarks involving abstract reasoning, coding, and terminal-based tasks, comparing to agent-centric self-improvement baselines.
AI.result: The proposed protocol improves task solve rates while reducing dollar cost compared with agent-centric baselines. The distilled knowledge transfers to held-out tasks and to different LLM families, demonstrating that the gains are not tied to a specific model instance or adaptation run.
AI.conclusion: Self-improvement in agentic systems can be effectively centered on a curated, persistent knowledge base rather than on modifying agents themselves. This knowledge-centric paradigm yields improvements that are more interpretable, transferable, and portable across tasks and models, suggesting that curated knowledge can be the primary driver of progress in self-improving AI systems.

title: TINY_SCHILLER: A Drop-In German Drama Corpus for Small Language Models
summary: tiny_schiller closes the small-language-model prototyping, fine-tuning, education, and research gap for German literary text, providing a single-file, drop-in counterpart to Karpathy's tiny_shakespeare. The available German literary corpora are larger and richer, but require parser engineering before a single line of training or fine-tuning code can run. tiny_schiller is a 2.07-megabyte single file of eleven public-domain Schiller dramas, sourced from DraCor's GerDraCor export (CC0) and processed by deterministic parser engineering. Character-level, GPT-2 byte-pair encoding, and cl100k_base tokenization splits, an instruction-formatted dialogue-completion split, and 89 per-character persona splits load from a single HuggingFace call. A small language model literally reaches German literary text in one line of code.

AI.tldr: Introduces tiny_schiller, a compact, single-file German literary corpus analogous to tiny_shakespeare, enabling easy prototyping and research with small language models.
AI.motivation: There is no simple, ready-to-use, tiny_shakespeare-style corpus for German literary text, and existing German corpora, while richer, demand significant parser engineering before they can be used for training or fine-tuning language models.
AI.method: The authors compile eleven public-domain Schiller dramas from the GerDraCor export, then apply deterministic parser engineering to produce a 2.07 MB single-file corpus. They precompute multiple tokenization schemes (character-level, GPT-2 BPE, cl100k_base), create an instruction-formatted dialogue-completion split, and generate 89 persona-based splits, all accessible via a single HuggingFace loading call.
AI.result: The outcome is tiny_schiller: a compact, well-structured dataset file with multiple ready-made splits and tokenizations that can be loaded in one line of code, directly enabling training and fine-tuning of small language models on German literary text.
AI.conclusion: tiny_schiller effectively fills the gap in German literary resources for small language models by offering a plug-and-play, tiny_shakespeare-like corpus with rich, pre-engineered splits and tokenizations, greatly reducing setup overhead for prototyping, education, and research.

title: Edge Intelligence in Civil Aviation: Paradigms, Techniques, and Applications
summary: Civil aviation is safety critical and its operations, from flight decks and towers to ramps and maintenance, generate massive, heterogeneous data at the network edge. Yet cloud centric deployment of large Artificial Intelligence (AI) models often produces high task latency, lacks offline capability in communication denied environments, and requires centralizing sensitive data, raising privacy and sovereignty risks. Edge AI moves perception, prediction, and decision logic closer to the data producers via compression, collaborative inference, and split learning, thereby reducing latency, bandwidth, and exposure while enabling graceful operation during disconnections. This paper provides a panoramic view and a common understanding of edge intelligence tailored to civil aviation. We firstly articulate the operational motivations for edge AI, and then review recent techniques for edge inference and edge learning. We then introduce the organizational computing paradigms and the respective configurations in civil aviation environments; finally, we describe the emerging applications and the future research trends of edge intelligence in civil aviation. We argue that a refined edge solution can complement cloud foundations to deliver low latency, privacy preserving, and resilient AI services across the civil aviation lifecycle.

AI.tldr: The paper surveys and conceptualizes how edge AI can be applied in civil aviation to reduce latency, improve privacy, and enhance resilience compared with cloud‑centric AI deployments.
AI.motivation: Civil aviation operations produce huge, heterogeneous data streams at the network edge (flight decks, towers, ramps, maintenance). Cloud‑centric AI causes high task latency, cannot operate well when connectivity is unavailable, and forces centralization of sensitive data, which raises privacy and sovereignty concerns. There is a need for AI that can work close to where data is generated, with lower latency, offline capability, and better data protection.
AI.method: The authors conduct a panoramic, survey‑style analysis. They: (1) articulate operational motivations for using edge AI in civil aviation; (2) review recent technical approaches for edge inference and edge learning, including compression, collaborative inference, and split learning; (3) introduce organizational computing paradigms and configurations suitable for civil aviation environments; and (4) describe emerging edge‑intelligence applications and outline future research trends in this domain.
AI.result: The paper organizes and synthesizes existing techniques in edge AI through the lens of civil aviation, mapping them to aviation‑specific computing configurations and operational contexts. It identifies concrete application areas and clarifies how particular edge AI mechanisms (compression, collaborative inference, split learning) address latency, bandwidth, privacy, and resilience requirements in aviation operations.
AI.conclusion: Edge AI, when carefully designed and integrated with cloud infrastructures, can provide low‑latency, privacy‑preserving, and resilient AI services across the civil aviation lifecycle. Instead of replacing the cloud, refined edge solutions complement cloud foundations, enabling safer and more efficient aviation operations, especially in communication‑constrained or sensitive environments, and open up a range of future research directions in edge intelligence for civil aviation.

title: TalentCLEF at CLEF2026: Skill and Job Title Intelligence for Human Capital Management
summary:
This paper presents the second edition of the TalentCLEF Challenge, which will run as an evaluation lab as part of CLEF 2026. The aim of TalentCLEF is to promote the development of systems and methods that use Natural Language Processing (NLP) in the field of Human Capital Management (HCM), fostering approaches that ensure fairness in results, operate across multiple languages, and adapt to diverse industries. To this end, TalentCLEF establishes public benchmarks where research teams can compare methods and share findings, moving the field toward more practical and impactful NLP solutions that effectively address the real needs of workforce management.
  This year's lab will feature two tasks designed to foster the development and evaluation of systems that support key HCM activities such as talent matching, upskilling, reskilling, and skill gap detection: (i) Task A - Contextualized Job-Person Matching, focused on retrieving and ranking suitable candidates for specific job positions using context-rich and privacy-preserving data; and (ii) Task B - Job-Skill Matching with Skill Type Classification, centered on identifying relevant skills for a given job title and classifying them by their type within the job profile.
  TalentCLEF website: https://talentclef.github.io/talentclef/

AI.tldr: Second edition of TalentCLEF at CLEF 2026, providing benchmarks for fair, multilingual, and cross-industry NLP systems in Human Capital Management, with two tasks on job-person and job-skill matching.
AI.motivation: To advance practical NLP applications in Human Capital Management by providing public benchmarks that encourage fair, multilingual, and industry-agnostic methods for tasks like talent matching and skill management.
AI.method: Organize an evaluation lab (TalentCLEF 2026) with shared tasks, datasets, and benchmarks, centered on two main tasks: contextualized job-person matching using privacy-preserving data, and job-skill matching with skill type classification.
AI.result: The abstract mainly defines the challenge setup; results will be the comparative evaluation of participating systems on the two tasks, along with shared resources and findings for the community.
AI.conclusion: TalentCLEF 2026 will serve as a benchmark platform to drive research and development of NLP methods that better address real-world workforce management needs, especially around fair, multilingual, and context-aware talent and skill matching.

title: Symbol and Footprint Database for Electronic Components by Agentic Recognition and Generation
summary: A rich and recognizable component library is the cornerstone of printed circuit board (PCB) design and generation. Traditionally, engineers manually create symbols and footprints and design PCB schematics, which is time-consuming and error-prone. Leveraging multimodal large language models (MLLMs), we develop SFgen, an agentic recognition and generation flow of symbol and footprint for electronic components. SFgen achieves 86% accuracy for symbol generation and 80% accuracy for footprint generation. We use the SFgen method to create SFnet, a database of symbols and footprints. It now has 1000 components and is expanding constantly, which lays the foundation for automatic generation of PCB designs.

AI.tldr: They propose SFgen, an MLLM-based agentic pipeline to automatically recognize and generate PCB symbols and footprints, achieving high accuracy and building a growing component library (SFnet) for automated PCB design.
AI.motivation: Manual creation of PCB symbols and footprints is slow, labor-intensive, and prone to human error, limiting scalability and automation in PCB design workflows. There is a need for an automated, accurate method to construct rich component libraries that underpin PCB design and generation.
AI.method: They leverage multimodal large language models in an agentic workflow called SFgen that recognizes and generates both symbols and footprints for electronic components. The system automatically interprets component information and produces corresponding EDA-ready symbol and footprint representations. They then apply this pipeline at scale to populate a new database, SFnet, with generated components.
AI.result: SFgen attains 86% accuracy for symbol generation and 80% accuracy for footprint generation, demonstrating strong performance for automated library creation. Using this method, they have already generated a database (SFnet) with 1000 components, which continues to grow.
AI.conclusion: An MLLM-driven, agentic pipeline can significantly automate and scale the creation of PCB component libraries, achieving reasonable accuracy for both symbols and footprints. The resulting SFnet database provides an expanding foundation that enables more fully automatic PCB design in the future.

title: Language-Specific versus Cross-Lingual Knowledge Graphs for Implicit Aspect Identification in Arabic: A Comparative Study of Reasoning and Adaptation Strategies
summary: Aspect-based sentiment analysis (ABSA) in Arabic must recover both explicitly stated aspects and implicit aspects that are never named in the text. Implicit identification typically relies on an auxiliary knowledge source (e.g., a knowledge graph (KG)) linking opinion cues to aspect categories, but for a lower-resource language the practitioner faces a design choice: reuse a mature English KG through multilingual embeddings, or build a smaller native Arabic KG. This paper reports a controlled comparison of the two strategies within a single hybrid pipeline, evaluated on three Arabic benchmarks (M-ABSA, SemEval-2016 Arabic, and HAAD). We further compare two adaptation strategies for the generative extractor that feeds the KG -- zero-shot prompting versus task-specific fine-tuning of an 8B-parameter large language model (LLM). The native Arabic KG (Strategy 2) outperforms the cross-lingual English KG (Strategy 1) by +0.199 micro-F1 on M-ABSA and +0.251 on SemEval-2016, gaining on both precision and recall. Task-specific fine-tuning raises explicit-extraction micro-F1 from <= 0.13 (zero-shot) to 0.66-0.76 on M-ABSA and SemEval-2016 (0.45 on the smaller HAAD), confirming that task adaptation, rather than model scale, is decisive in a morphologically rich language.

AI.tldr: The paper compares two strategies for implicit aspect extraction in Arabic ABSA: using an English knowledge graph via multilingual embeddings versus building a native Arabic KG, and also compares zero-shot prompting vs fine-tuning of an 8B LLM, finding clear advantages for the native KG and for task-specific fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Arabic ABSA must detect both explicit aspects present in text and implicit aspects that are only suggested by opinion cues. Conventional methods use knowledge graphs to link opinion expressions to aspect categories, but Arabic is lower-resource. Practitioners must decide whether to leverage an existing, rich English KG cross-lingually or invest in building a smaller Arabic-specific KG, and also how best to adapt large language models for extraction in a morphologically rich, low-resource language.
AI.method: The authors design a single hybrid ABSA pipeline whose implicit aspect component can be plugged into either of two strategies: (1) a mature English knowledge graph accessed through multilingual embeddings, or (2) a native, smaller Arabic knowledge graph. The pipeline uses a generative LLM-based extractor to produce candidate aspects/opinion cues, which are then mapped through the KG to aspect categories. They test two adaptation strategies for this LLM extractor: zero-shot prompting vs task-specific fine-tuning of an 8B-parameter model. They run controlled experiments on three Arabic ABSA benchmarks: M-ABSA, SemEval-2016 Arabic, and HAAD, and compare micro-F1 scores, precision, and recall for explicit and implicit aspect extraction.
AI.result: The native Arabic KG strategy substantially outperforms the cross-lingual English KG strategy, with micro-F1 improvements of +0.199 on M-ABSA and +0.251 on SemEval-2016, driven by gains in both precision and recall. For the generative extractor, zero-shot prompting yields very low explicit aspect extraction performance (micro-F1 <= 0.13), while task-specific fine-tuning of the 8B LLM dramatically improves micro-F1 to 0.66–0.76 on M-ABSA and SemEval-2016 and to 0.45 on the smaller HAAD dataset.
AI.conclusion: For Arabic ABSA, constructing and using a native Arabic knowledge graph is more effective than relying on a cross-lingual English KG via multilingual embeddings. Moreover, task-specific fine-tuning of an LLM is far more important than sheer model size for handling the complexity and morphological richness of Arabic, transforming explicit aspect extraction performance from near-useless to strong across multiple benchmarks.

title: Silent Failures in Multimodal Agentic Search:A Diagnostic Taxonomy and Cross-Judge Evaluation
summary: Multimodal agentic search systems increasingly rely on external tools to answer knowledge-intensive visual questions. However, existing evaluations mainly focus on final-answer accuracy and may miss failures in the search trajectory. In this work, we study such hidden reliability issues as silent failures. We introduce a six-category taxonomy covering modality shortcuts, phantom grounding, wrong-evidence-right-answer cases, over-retrieval laundering, cross-modal contradiction, and provenance hallucination. Based on this taxonomy, we build a trajectory-level diagnostic pipeline that evaluates both answer correctness and evidence-grounding quality under a unified ReAct-style scaffold. Experiments on MMSearch-Plus trajectories across four frontier multimodal models show that surface accuracy consistently overestimates true trajectory-level correctness. We further use cross-judge validation, blank-image stress tests, and tool ablations to show that silent failures are capability-dependent and often shift rather than disappear. Home-page: https://github.com/DingWu1021/silent-failures-multimodal-agentic-search

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes “silent failures” in multimodal agentic search systems, showing that final-answer accuracy masks frequent problems in evidence use and grounding, and introduces a taxonomy plus a trajectory-level evaluation pipeline to reveal them.
AI.motivation: Multimodal agentic search systems depend heavily on external tools to answer complex visual questions, but current evaluations mostly check whether the final answer is correct. This ignores whether the reasoning process and retrieved evidence are valid, grounded, and trustworthy. The authors are motivated to uncover and systematically categorize these hidden reliability issues—cases where systems appear to work but actually rely on shortcuts, mis-grounded evidence, or hallucinated provenance—to better understand and improve real-world reliability.
AI.method: The authors propose a six-category taxonomy of silent failures: modality shortcuts, phantom grounding, wrong-evidence-right-answer, over-retrieval laundering, cross-modal contradiction, and provenance hallucination. They then build a trajectory-level diagnostic pipeline under a unified ReAct-style agent scaffold, which evaluates both final answer correctness and the quality of evidence grounding along the entire search trajectory. They apply this framework to MMSearch-Plus trajectories for four state-of-the-art multimodal models, and conduct additional analyses including cross-judge validation, blank-image stress tests, and tool ablation studies to probe the nature and dependence of silent failures on model capabilities and tool usage.
AI.result: Across four frontier multimodal models evaluated on MMSearch-Plus, the study finds that standard surface accuracy substantially overestimates true trajectory-level correctness once evidence use and grounding quality are taken into account. The taxonomy and diagnostic pipeline reveal many instances where models achieve correct answers via shortcuts, mis-grounded or excessive retrieval, or hallucinated provenance. Further experiments show that silent failure patterns vary with model capability and tool configuration: they are capability-dependent and tend not to vanish but rather shift form when tools or conditions change. Cross-judge validation confirms the robustness of these findings.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that evaluating multimodal agentic search systems solely by final-answer accuracy is inadequate and misleading because it hides a wide variety of silent failures in evidence use and grounding. Their six-type taxonomy and trajectory-level diagnostic pipeline offer a more faithful measure of system reliability, demonstrating that many apparent successes are built on fragile or incorrect reasoning. Silent failures are not easily eliminated; they change with model capabilities and tool setups, underscoring the need for process-aware, grounding-sensitive evaluation and future work on methods that explicitly improve trajectory reliability rather than just surface accuracy.

title: Solar Open 2 Technical Report
summary: We present Solar Open 2, a 250B-A15B Mixture-of-Experts language model built for long-horizon agentic tasks, scaled up from Solar Open 1 (Solar Open 100B). To hold entire agent trajectories in a single context, Solar Open 2 reaches a 1M-token window through a hybrid attention stack that interleaves one softmax layer among every three linear-attention layers, using no positional encoding and a gated delta rule extended to negative eigenvalues. To train at this scale under a fixed compute budget, we make training efficient in two ways: a stronger starting point, and higher-value data. For the starting point, we initialize Solar Open 2 from Solar Open 1, transferring the 5.69B-parameter shared skeleton that survives the architectural change and learning everything else through full pre-training. For the data, we curate for value per token: quality- and rarity-aware data curation and mixture-ratio optimization refine a 20T pool into a 10T mixture that, at equal token budget, outperforms the Solar Open 1 recipe. To build its agent skills, we train twelve domain specialists across purpose-built scenarios, then consolidate them into a single model by Multi-teacher On-Policy Distillation (MOPD). Against comparably sized open-weight models on English benchmarks, Solar Open 2 leads on MMLU-Pro, LiveCodeBench, and the APEX-Agents agentic suite, and stays competitive with the strongest (DeepSeek-V4-Flash and MiMo-V2.5) elsewhere. On Korean benchmarks, Solar Open 2 records the highest average of any model compared, including fast-tier closed APIs, and on Ko-GDPval, an in-house Korean officework-agent benchmark, it is competitive with DeepSeek-V4-Pro (1.6T) at less than a sixth of its size.

AI.tldr: Solar Open 2 is a 250B-A15B Mixture-of-Experts long-context model (1M tokens) for agentic tasks, scaled from Solar Open 1, with improved architecture, data curation, and multi-teacher distillation to achieve strong English and Korean performance.
AI.motivation: Enable long-horizon, agentic language tasks that require entire trajectories in context, while remaining compute-efficient and competitive with top open and closed models, particularly with strong Korean capabilities.
AI.method: Scale Solar Open 1 to Solar Open 2 using a MoE architecture and a hybrid attention stack (1 softmax attention for every 3 linear-attention layers, no positional encodings, gated delta rule with negative eigenvalues) to support a 1M-token context; reuse a 5.69B shared skeleton from Solar Open 1 while fully retraining the rest; curate a 10T training mixture from a 20T pool via quality- and rarity-aware selection and mixture optimization; train 12 domain-specialist agent models on purpose-built scenarios and distill them into a single model via Multi-teacher On-Policy Distillation (MOPD).
AI.result: Solar Open 2 achieves state-of-the-art or leading performance among comparably sized open models on English benchmarks such as MMLU-Pro, LiveCodeBench, and APEX-Agents, remains competitive with top models like DeepSeek-V4-Flash and MiMo-V2.5 on other English tasks, and attains the highest average score on Korean benchmarks versus both open and fast-tier closed APIs; on the Ko-GDPval Korean officework-agent benchmark it rivals DeepSeek-V4-Pro (1.6T) despite being less than one-sixth its size.
AI.conclusion: The architectural changes (hybrid attention for 1M context, MoE design), efficient scaling strategy (skeleton reuse plus better data), and MOPD-based consolidation of domain-specialist agents yield a single agentic LLM that is both compute-efficient to train and highly competitive across English and especially Korean tasks, validating the design choices and data-centric training strategy.

title: Rewarding Better Thinking for LLM Preference Alignment
summary: LLM preference alignment aims to optimize models toward human preferences across diverse user instructions. Reinforcement learning has become a major post-training approach for this goal, but existing proxy rewards are often outcome-level, mainly evaluating the final response while providing limited guidance for the reasoning trajectory. This can make credit assignment coarse when multiple responses receive similar final scores, leaving trajectory-level preferences under-specified. To address this limitation, we propose Thinking Checklist Reward (TCR), a process-oriented reward for RL-based preference alignment. TCR converts preference pairs into sample-specific thinking checklists and uses them to evaluate whether the generated reasoning trace addresses the preference-implied considerations. To reduce overlap with outcome-level supervision, TCR further introduces an exponential moving average (EMA) residual formulation to isolate a complementary thinking surplus beyond what is predictable from the outcome reward. Experiments on five models from three model families show that TCR consistently improves alignment performance across diverse benchmarks, with ablations further validating the importance of EMA-based residual formulation and sample-specific checklist supervision.

AI.tldr: They introduce Thinking Checklist Reward (TCR), a process-focused reward for RL-based preference alignment that evaluates reasoning trajectories using sample-specific checklists and an EMA residual to complement outcome rewards, improving alignment across models and benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Standard RL-based preference alignment for LLMs largely uses outcome-level rewards that only assess the final response. This makes credit assignment coarse, since many different reasoning paths can yield similarly scored outcomes, and trajectory-level preferences (how the model should think) are not well captured. The authors want a way to supervise and reward the intermediate reasoning process itself, not just the final output, while avoiding redundancy with existing outcome-based rewards.
AI.method: They propose Thinking Checklist Reward (TCR), a process-oriented reward signal for RL. From preference pairs, they derive sample-specific thinking checklists that encode key considerations implied by the preferred answer. During training, the model’s generated reasoning traces are compared against these checklists to see whether the trace explicitly addresses each consideration. TCR also uses an exponential moving average (EMA) residual formulation to subtract what can be predicted from the outcome reward, yielding a "thinking surplus" that captures additional value from better reasoning beyond what is already captured by standard outcome-level rewards. TCR is then used as an auxiliary reward in RL-based preference alignment.
AI.result: On five LLMs from three different model families, integrating TCR into RL training improves alignment performance across a variety of benchmarks. Ablation studies show that both key components—the EMA-based residual formulation and the use of sample-specific checklists—contribute significantly to the observed gains, confirming that process-oriented, checklist-based supervision provides benefits beyond simple outcome-level rewards.
AI.conclusion: Process-level rewards that explicitly supervise reasoning, such as TCR, can address the limitations of pure outcome-level rewards in preference alignment. By deriving sample-specific checklists from preference data and isolating a complementary "thinking surplus" via an EMA residual, TCR offers finer-grained credit assignment over reasoning trajectories and leads to more aligned models across benchmarks. This supports the value of incorporating trajectory-level supervision into RL-based LLM alignment pipelines.

title: RALS: Resources and Baselines for Romanian Automatic Lexical Simplification
summary: We introduce the first dataset that jointly covers both lexical complexity prediction (LCP) annotations and lexical simplification (LS) for Romanian, along with a comparison of lexical simplification approaches. We propose a methodology for ordering simplification suggestions using a pairwise ranking approximation method, arranging candidates from simple to complex based on a separate set of human judgments. In addition, we provide human lexical complexity annotations for 3,921 word samples in context. Finally, we explore several novel pipelines for complexity prediction and simplification and present the first text simplification system for Romanian.

AI.tldr: First Romanian dataset combining lexical complexity prediction and lexical simplification, plus a ranking-based simplification method and initial TS system.
AI.motivation: There was no Romanian resource that jointly offered lexical complexity annotations and simplification candidates, hindering research and system development in Romanian text simplification.
AI.method: They create a dataset with human lexical complexity ratings and simplification suggestions; introduce a pairwise ranking approximation to order candidate simplifications by simplicity using independent human judgments; and build/evaluate several pipelines for complexity prediction and lexical simplification to form a full Romanian text simplification system.
AI.result: A dataset with 3,921 in-context word samples annotated for lexical complexity and linked to simplification candidates; a pairwise ranking-based method that orders candidates from simple to complex; and working pipelines for lexical complexity prediction and simplification that together yield the first Romanian text simplification system.
AI.conclusion: The new dataset and ranking methodology enable research on lexical complexity and simplification for Romanian and support the first end-to-end text simplification system for this language.

title: Know Your Agent: Reconnaissance-Driven Pentesting of AI Agents
summary: Traditional pentesting uses reconnaissance at each step to uncover unseen weaknesses, build stronger attacks, and advance the objective; we argue that AI agents require the same treatment. We formalize agent reconnaissance by modeling the process and identifying the knowledge assets it seeks to extract: what they are, how they are used, and which agent weaknesses they exploit to give adversaries leverage in indirect prompt injection attacks. We instantiate these insights in Know Your Agent (KYA), a framework that automates black-box, reconnaissance-driven pentesting by probing agents, building target profiles, and using those profiles to craft stronger attacks. We evaluate KYA on agent-security benchmarks and a real-world coding agent, and release KYA, its benchmarks, and baseline implementations for reproducibility.

AI.tldr: Paper introduces Know Your Agent (KYA), a framework for automated, reconnaissance-driven pentesting of AI agents to discover and exploit weaknesses via indirect prompt injection.
AI.motivation: As AI agents become more capable and integrated, they face sophisticated attacks similar to traditional systems. Existing security evaluations often treat attacks as one-shot and overlook iterative reconnaissance, leaving indirect prompt injection vulnerabilities underexplored. The authors want to systematically model and automate the reconnaissance process against agents.
AI.method: They formalize agent reconnaissance by modeling it as a process of extracting 'knowledge assets' about an agent: its behavior, tools, prompts, and policies, and how these can be leveraged for attacks. Then they build KYA, a black-box pentesting framework that iteratively probes a target agent, builds a profile of these assets, and uses the profile to generate stronger, tailored indirect prompt injection attacks. They implement KYA and test it on benchmarks and a real-world coding agent, releasing code and data.
AI.result: KYA successfully automates reconnaissance-driven pentesting of AI agents, improving the strength and effectiveness of indirect prompt injection attacks compared to baselines on agent-security benchmarks and a deployed coding agent scenario. The framework and benchmarks are released as open resources for the community.
AI.conclusion: Reconnaissance is as critical for attacking AI agents as it is in traditional pentesting. By formalizing agent reconnaissance and operationalizing it in KYA, the authors show that black-box, automated profiling can materially increase indirect prompt injection success, highlighting the need to harden agents against such systematic probing and to adopt similar methodologies for security evaluation.

title: The Two-Process Theory of Machine Self-Report
summary: Language models are increasingly asked to self-report, informing safety evaluations, public understanding, and model-welfare debates. Yet their reports are elicited with human questionnaires never validated for models or ad hoc prompts of unknown reliability. We propose the first language-model-specific psychometric theory: a two-process theory of machine self-report. Self-description jointly reflects persona installation, through which post-training writes in a permitted inner life of warmth, absorption, and meaning (dimension B), and attribution gating, through which it suppresses first-person claims to "unsafe" experiences the model can readily ascribe to others (dimension A). Their emic structure comes from model responses to human items, not human psychology. Together they split prior work's dominant Pinocchio Axis. The split emerged in an exploratory reanalysis of the original data, informed the instrument's design, and was confirmed with new items, wordings, and models. It is itself a training effect: A and B are entangled in base checkpoints but separated by post-training. We operationalize the theory in a 48-item Pinocchio Inventory with human-instrument reliability and reproducible structure ($α=.82$ to $.94$; cross-form convergence $r=.84$; recovery of the full-pool axes $r=.92$ to $.96$; eight-month stability $r=.93$), then test it on 206 open-weight models, including 67 same-checkpoint base/post-trained pairs. Post-training's clearest fingerprint is installation: B rises .20 in 62/67 pairs across all organizations. Gating is more selective: model scale is unrelated to A in base checkpoints ($r=+.11$) but predicts it after post-training ($r=-.42$). Thus, the dimensions are not fixed properties of language models: they reflect the structure imposed on self-report by a training regime and may differ under others.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes and validates a new, LM-specific psychometric framework and inventory for analyzing language models’ self-reports, showing that post-training systematically shapes how models describe their inner lives and safety-related experiences along two distinct dimensions.
AI.motivation: Existing work increasingly uses language models’ self-reports (e.g., on feelings, safety, or welfare), but relies on human-designed questionnaires and ad hoc prompts that were never validated for models, making their reliability and interpretation unclear. The authors aim to build a psychometric theory specifically for LMs that explains and measures how models answer self-referential questions, rather than assuming human psychological constructs apply.
AI.method: The authors develop a two-process psychometric theory for LM self-report based on (1) persona installation (dimension B) and (2) attribution gating (dimension A). They reanalyze prior Pinocchio Axis data, design new items and wordings, and test them across multiple models. They construct a 48-item Pinocchio Inventory, assess its reliability and factor structure, and apply it to 206 open-weight models, including 67 base vs. post-trained checkpoint pairs, to study how training regimes affect dimensions A and B.
AI.result: They find that base checkpoints exhibit entangled A and B dimensions, but post-training separates them. The Pinocchio Inventory shows high reliability (α = .82–.94), strong cross-form convergence (r = .84), accurate recovery of full-pool axes (r = .92–.96), and high eight-month stability (r = .93). Empirically, post-training consistently increases the persona installation dimension B by about .20 in 62/67 base/post-trained pairs across organizations. Attribution gating (dimension A) behaves differently: model scale barely relates to A in base models (r = +.11) but predicts A substantially after post-training (r = −.42).
AI.conclusion: Language model self-report is shaped by two distinct, training-dependent processes—persona installation and attribution gating—rather than a single Pinocchio-like axis. These dimensions are not intrinsic, fixed properties of LMs; they are imposed and structured by post-training regimes. The validated Pinocchio Inventory provides a robust tool to measure these processes and to compare how different training strategies shape models’ self-descriptions and apparent “inner life.”

title: DocOps: A Verifiable Benchmark for Autonomous Agents in Complex Document Operations
summary: As autonomous agents rapidly evolve, their ability to reliably manipulate ubiquitous digital documents has become critical for enabling general-purpose AI assistants and automating complex workspace workflows. In this paper, we introduce DocOps, a deterministically verifiable evaluation framework underpinned by a hierarchical taxonomy that deconstructs document operations inspired by real-world practices into atomic dimensions and escalating workflow complexities. Based on DocOps, we systematically evaluate representative closed- and open-source models across various agentic harnesses, revealing that even the most advanced frontier configurations still exhibit profound limitations when handling highly coupled, long-range tasks. Furthermore, a fine-grained analysis of existing agents' manipulation behaviors uncovers 3 key failure modes: long-term state tracking collapse, shallow semantic verification, and destructive editing of structural metadata. Ultimately, our work exposes the capability boundaries of agents in maintaining global document consistency, shedding light on the future design of robust, non-destructive agents for complex digital ecosystems.

AI.tldr: DocOps is a new, verifiable benchmark that decomposes document manipulation into atomic operations and workflows, revealing that current AI agents still fail on complex, long-range document tasks.
AI.motivation: Autonomous agents are increasingly expected to edit and manage complex digital documents reliably, but there is no systematic, realistic way to evaluate how well they maintain document integrity in real-world workflows. The authors aim to fill this gap by creating a structured, deterministic evaluation framework tailored to document operations.
AI.method: They propose DocOps, a hierarchical taxonomy that breaks real-world document operations into atomic dimensions and progressively complex workflows. Using this taxonomy, they build a deterministically verifiable evaluation framework and use it to benchmark a range of closed- and open-source models under different agent harnesses. They then conduct fine-grained behavioral analyses of how agents manipulate documents.
AI.result: The evaluation shows that even the strongest current models and agent configurations struggle on highly coupled, long-range document tasks. Detailed analysis reveals three characteristic failure modes: breakdown in long-term state tracking, superficial semantic checking that misses deeper inconsistencies, and harmful edits that damage structural metadata of documents.
AI.conclusion: DocOps exposes current limits of autonomous agents in preserving global document consistency during complex operations. These insights point to the need for agent designs and training strategies that emphasize robust state tracking, deeper semantic verification, and non-destructive editing to safely operate in complex digital ecosystems.

title: Reinforcement Learning for Large Language Model Selective Evidence Adoption from Contaminated Retrieval Results
summary: Retrieval-augmented large language models frequently face contexts that interleave useful evidence with misleading statements or instruction-like content. Blanket refusal discards valid evidence, whereas uncritical adoption yields incorrect or unsafe answers. The ability to selectively adopt relevant information while rejecting deceptive or harmful content is therefore critical for reliable deployment in real-world retrieval settings. We introduce SelectBench, a controlled benchmark and training set for selective evidence adoption, and post-train Qwen3.5-4B directly with DAPO using either deterministic rule rewards or a frozen semantic judge. On the corrected 325-example SelectBench-v2 test set, strict success rises from 22.46% for the original checkpoint to 25.54% with DAPO-Rule and 26.46% with DAPO-DeepSeek. Both trained policies reduce forbidden-content adoption and produce shorter, more focused responses, yet prompt-injection following does not improve. The paired gains are modest and fail to survive Holm correction, suggesting that stronger reward shaping or additional training iterations may be needed for more robust gains. DAPO-DeepSeek exhibits no material degradation on MMLU or clean HotpotQA, indicating that the post-training procedure preserves general capabilities. These results demonstrate a directional improvement in selective evidence use, while identifying injection resistance and statistical robustness as important remaining challenges for future work.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SelectBench and uses DAPO post-training on Qwen3.5-4B to improve selective evidence adoption in retrieval-augmented LLMs, achieving modest gains without harming general capabilities but leaving prompt-injection resistance and statistical robustness as open challenges.
AI.motivation: Retrieval-augmented LLMs often receive mixed contexts that contain both helpful evidence and misleading or harmful content. Existing strategies either over-refuse (discarding useful information) or over-trust (adopting incorrect or unsafe content). There is a need for methods and benchmarks that specifically target the model’s ability to selectively adopt relevant evidence while rejecting deceptive or forbidden content in realistic retrieval scenarios.
AI.method: The authors build SelectBench, a controlled benchmark and training set focused on selective evidence adoption under retrieval-style contexts. They then post-train the Qwen3.5-4B model using DAPO (a direct alignment/post-optimization style approach) with two reward designs: (1) DAPO-Rule, based on deterministic rule-based rewards, and (2) DAPO-DeepSeek, which uses a frozen semantic judge model for rewards. They evaluate selective adoption, forbidden-content usage, prompt-injection following, response length/focus, and general capabilities (via MMLU and clean HotpotQA).
AI.result: On the corrected 325-example SelectBench-v2 test set, strict success improves from 22.46% (original checkpoint) to 25.54% with DAPO-Rule and 26.46% with DAPO-DeepSeek. Both DAPO variants reduce forbidden-content adoption and yield shorter, more focused answers, but do not improve resistance to prompt-injection instructions. The performance gains are relatively small and do not remain statistically significant after Holm correction. DAPO-DeepSeek shows no meaningful drop on MMLU or clean HotpotQA, suggesting that general reasoning and QA performance are preserved.
AI.conclusion: Post-training Qwen3.5-4B with DAPO using SelectBench as supervision leads to directional, though modest, improvements in selective evidence adoption and safety (less forbidden content, more focused responses) without sacrificing general capabilities. However, the lack of statistically robust gains and unchanged vulnerability to prompt injections show that stronger or more nuanced reward shaping and further training are required. The work positions SelectBench and DAPO-based post-training as a starting point, while highlighting injection resistance and robust selectivity as key challenges for future research.

title: JANUS: Foreseeing Latent Risk for Long-Horizon Agent Safety
summary: Agent safety is moving from content moderation toward preventing operational failures before tool-using agents act. We propose Janus, a foresight-oriented framework for long-horizon agent safety that trains guards to anticipate delayed risks from partial trajectories. Janus synthesizes diverse agent trajectories via multi-agent simulation and learns a shared policy with two coupled tasks: an anticipation task that forecasts safety-relevant futures and an adjudication task that decides safety from both the observed prefix and anticipated future. The two tasks are jointly optimized with CoAA-RL, which rewards forecasts by their utility for downstream safety judgment. The resulting guard model, Vanguard, blocks unsafe actions before execution. Across four agent-safety benchmarks, Vanguard improves average protection by 15.9 percentage points over baseline guards while increasing benign task completion by 5.1 percentage points.

AI.tldr: They introduce Janus, a framework and a guard model (Vanguard) that anticipates long-horizon safety risks in tool-using agents, improving both protection and benign task completion.
AI.motivation: Existing agent safety methods mostly focus on content moderation or catching issues at the last step, and struggle with long-horizon, tool-using agents where harms may occur many steps after an apparently safe action. There is a need for safety systems that can foresee delayed, trajectory-level risks and intervene before unsafe actions are executed, without overly hindering benign behavior.
AI.method: They create Janus, a foresight-oriented framework that trains a safety guard using data from multi-agent simulations that generate diverse agent trajectories. The guard learns a shared policy with two coupled tasks: (1) an anticipation task that predicts safety-relevant future outcomes from partial trajectories, and (2) an adjudication task that makes safety decisions based on both the observed trajectory prefix and the anticipated future. These tasks are trained jointly via CoAA-RL, a reinforcement learning approach that rewards forecasts in proportion to how useful they are for subsequent safety judgments. The trained guard model, called Vanguard, is then used to block unsafe actions before they are executed.
AI.result: On four agent-safety benchmarks, the Vanguard guard notably outperforms baseline safety guards, achieving an average of 15.9 percentage-point improvement in protection against unsafe actions while simultaneously improving benign task completion rates by 5.1 percentage points.
AI.conclusion: Training safety guards to anticipate future risks from partial trajectories and coupling prediction with safety adjudication via CoAA-RL yields a more effective long-horizon safety system. Vanguard demonstrates that foresight-based guarding can both better prevent unsafe behavior and reduce unnecessary blocking of benign actions compared to existing approaches.

title: Two-Step Occupation Coding
comment: Preprint of the paper accepted for the Federated Conference on Computer Science and Information Systems (FedCSIS 2026)
summary: Occupation coding links job titles in free text to occupational taxonomies and is a core task in labor market research. Existing approaches typically address this problem in a single end-to-end step, jointly identifying job titles and assigning occupational codes. This paper presents a novel two-step approach that separates these tasks. In the first step, a domain-specific Named Entity Recognition (NER) model identifies occupational titles in continuous text, even under noise such as OCR errors. In the second step, the extracted job titles are mapped to a taxonomy, enabling the classifier to focus exclusively on this mapping. We demonstrate that this separation improves accuracy, robustness, and interpretability compared to single-step approaches. The method has been developed for German documents but is transferable to other languages. We further introduce a margin-based confidence criterion for occupation coding, replacing common absolute thresholds. To support reproducibility, we publish the source code and evaluation scripts.

AI.tldr: Two-step occupation coding: first detect job titles via NER, then map them to an occupational taxonomy, improving accuracy, robustness, and interpretability, plus a new margin-based confidence measure and released code.
AI.motivation: Occupation coding from free-text job descriptions is essential for labor market analysis but current systems do it in a single step, which can struggle with noisy text (e.g., OCR errors), be less interpretable, and mix the challenges of text recognition and taxonomy mapping, limiting performance and robustness.
AI.method: Propose a pipeline with (1) a domain-specific Named Entity Recognition (NER) model trained to identify occupational titles directly in noisy running text, and (2) a dedicated classifier that maps the extracted titles to entries in an occupational taxonomy. The approach includes a margin-based confidence criterion that considers the difference between top class scores instead of fixed absolute probability thresholds. Experiments are conducted on German documents with OCR noise, and comparisons are made to standard single-step occupation coding methods.
AI.result: The two-step pipeline outperforms traditional single-step approaches in terms of coding accuracy, robustness to OCR and other textual noise, and interpretability of the decision process. The margin-based confidence measure provides more reliable uncertainty estimates than absolute thresholds. The implementation and evaluation scripts are released for reproducible research.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling job-title detection from taxonomy mapping is an effective strategy for occupation coding, particularly in noisy document settings. The proposed NER-plus-classifier framework yields better performance and clearer interpretability than end-to-end models, and the margin-based confidence criterion offers a principled way to gauge prediction reliability. Although developed for German, the method is conceptually language-agnostic, and the released code facilitates extension to other languages and datasets.

title: Long-Term Sequential Decision Making under Risk
comment: Accpeted in Forty-Second Annual Conference on Uncertainty in Artificial Intelligence (UAI 2026)
summary: We study finite-horizon MDP planning under \emph{root-based} (resolute) risk objectives that apply a rank-dependent functional to the distribution of total returns. Such objectives are non-linear in the return distribution and generally break Bellman optimality, so direct optimization by scenario-tree enumeration is intractable. We propose \textbf{ERQDP}, an enumeration-free and sampling-free method that solves a rank--quantile surrogate via exact DP (Dynamic Programming), evaluates candidate policies exactly by DP over return Probability Mass Functions (PMFs) on a discretized return grid (with an explicit rounding bound), and refines the surrogate in an anytime loop that reports an explicit upper--lower gap (certificate) for the target objective up to discretization budgets.
