AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how well instruction-tuned large language models can recognize which of Schwartz’s ten basic human values is expressed in short Russian situational texts, finding moderate top-1 accuracy, high top-3 accuracy, and systematic, directional confusion patterns between nearby values.
AI.motivation: Value evaluations of large language models usually assume that models can correctly identify which human value is at stake in concrete scenarios, but this assumption is rarely tested directly. The authors want to rigorously measure this prerequisite skill—value recognition—because misidentification or systematic confusions between values can bias any downstream claims about the model’s value alignment. They focus on Schwartz’s widely used ten basic values framework to make the study conceptually grounded and comparable.
AI.method: They construct an evaluation dataset of 1,000 Russian situational texts, each expressing one of Schwartz’s ten basic values, balanced across values and independently labeled by two human annotators per item. They then run 21 instruction-tuned large language models under a fixed ranked-response protocol where models must choose and rank candidate values. After filtering, 20 runs with reliable outputs form a "semantic panel." They compute pooled top-1 and top-3 accuracies, analyze error distributions, and compare observed confusion patterns against a checkpoint-specific null model. They specifically study which values are most often confused, whether these confusions are symmetric or asymmetric, and how adjacent values in Schwartz’s theory contribute to semantic errors.
AI.result: Across the 20 reliable model runs, pooled top-1 accuracy for value recognition is 0.683 and top-3 accuracy is 0.892, indicating that models usually place the correct value among their top guesses but often mis-rank close alternatives. A majority of semantic errors (50.9%) involve confusion with adjacent values in Schwartz’s circumplex, much higher than the 24.4% expected under a checkpoint-specific null. The authors identify eight recurring directed confusion patterns across checkpoints and in human-validated subsets. Several confusions are strongly asymmetric (e.g., Universalism is often mistaken for Benevolence, Tradition for Conformity, and Security for Power), whereas the boundary between Stimulation and Hedonism shows bidirectional confusion. The severity of these confusions varies by model checkpoint and can distort inferred higher-order value profiles.
AI.conclusion: Instruction-tuned LLMs show solid but imperfect ability to recognize Schwartz’s basic human values in short situational texts, typically locating the right value region but struggling to disambiguate between nearby values. Systematic, often asymmetric confusion patterns between specific values mean that standard value profiling of models can be biased if this recognition layer is ignored. The authors argue that future value alignment and value-evaluation work for LLMs should explicitly incorporate value-recognition benchmarks that combine exact top-1 accuracy, ranked recovery metrics, and directed error analyses to capture these nuanced failure modes.

title: PRO-LONG: Programmatic Memory Enables Long-Horizon Reasoning
summary: Long-horizon tasks require sustained perception, reasoning, and exploration, and are a persistent challenge for large language model (LLM) agents. This gap is reflected in their limited performance on continual learning benchmarks such as ARC-AGI-3, especially when models are evaluated out of the box. Various agent harnesses have been proposed to close this gap, and each commits to a strategy for handling long sequences of observations, i.e., what information to save from the environment and how to load it into model context, a choice we argue is particularly consequential. Existing methods for context management face a significant tradeoff, as preserving more information makes retrieving relevant details less tractable. We propose PRO-LONG, a minimal context management framework built around programmatic memory for LLM agents in long-horizon, exploratory settings. PRO-LONG addresses the tradeoff by keeping a complete, structured interaction log and capitalizing on recent progress in coding agents to search this history efficiently. On the full ARC-AGI-3 public game set, PRO-LONG improves over a base coding agent by an average of 18.0 percentage points across frontier models, and matches or exceeds state-of-the-art specialized harnesses (up to 76.1% pass@1) while using 4.2-5.8x fewer tokens. With Fable 5, PRO-LONG achieves 97.4% best@2 at a total cost of \$1,750. Relevant code and logs are available at https://github.com/alexisfox7/PRO-LONG.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces PRO-LONG, a lightweight context-management framework that lets LLM agents handle long-horizon, exploratory tasks much more effectively and cheaply by keeping a full structured log and using code-style tools to search it efficiently.
AI.motivation: LLM agents struggle on long-horizon and continual-learning tasks (e.g., ARC-AGI-3) because they must deal with long streams of observations and partial information. Existing agent harnesses must decide what to store from the environment and how to feed it back into the model, and current approaches face a key tradeoff: storing more information makes it harder to retrieve the right pieces later. This leads to poor out-of-the-box performance and heavy context/token usage. The authors are motivated to design a simple, general context-management strategy that resolves this tradeoff and boosts performance without exploding token costs.
AI.method: They design PRO-LONG, a minimal context-management framework based on programmatic memory for LLM agents in exploratory, long-horizon settings. PRO-LONG keeps a complete, structured log of all agent-environment interactions rather than aggressively pruning or summarizing. It then leverages capabilities from recent coding agents—such as programmatic search, indexing, and retrieval over the interaction history—to efficiently locate relevant past information and inject only what’s needed into the model’s context. The framework is evaluated as a wrapper around frontier LLMs on the ARC-AGI-3 benchmark, comparing against a base coding agent and several specialized agent harnesses, measuring both task performance (pass@k) and token efficiency.
AI.result: On the full ARC-AGI-3 public game set, PRO-LONG improves performance of a base coding agent by an average of 18.0 percentage points across multiple frontier models. It matches or surpasses state-of-the-art specialized harnesses, attaining up to 76.1% pass@1, while using 4.2–5.8× fewer tokens than those alternatives. When paired with the Fable 5 model, PRO-LONG attains 97.4% best@2 on the benchmark at a total monetary cost of about $1,750. The authors also release code and logs to facilitate replication and further research.
AI.conclusion: The authors conclude that careful, programmatic context management is crucial for LLM agents on long-horizon, exploratory tasks. By maintaining a full but structured interaction history and using efficient programmatic search over it, PRO-LONG overcomes the usual tradeoff between information preservation and retrieval tractability. This yields substantial accuracy gains and large reductions in token usage compared to existing harnesses, without requiring heavy task-specific engineering. They position PRO-LONG as a simple, general-purpose foundation for building more capable long-horizon LLM agents and for future research on memory and context management in agentic systems.

title: Sound Probabilistic Safety Bounds for Large Language Models
comment: The Initial version of this manuscript has been available on OpenReview, see https://openreview.net/forum?id=papImkPLf5
summary: We propose a novel framework for computing rigorous bounds on the probability that a large language model (LLM) generates harmful output to a given prompt. We study a new application of the Clopper-Pearson confidence intervals to obtain probably approximately correct (PAC) bounds for this problem. As our main technical contribution, we propose an algorithm that leverages features in the latent space to prioritize exploring branches in the auto-regressive generation tree that are more likely to produce harmful outputs. Our approach in particular enables the efficient computation of useful lower bounds, even in scenarios where the true harm probability is extremely small, and crucially, the obtained lower bounds are sound, i.e., formally proven to be less than the actual harmfulness probability: our experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of our method by computing non-trivial lower bounds on state-of-the-art LLMs. This study newly enables the evaluation and statistical certification of LLMs.

AI.tldr: They develop a statistically rigorous method to bound how likely an LLM is to produce harmful content to a given prompt, even when that probability is very small.
AI.motivation: Current methods for evaluating LLM safety are often empirical, ad hoc, or lack formal guarantees. It is hard to measure very small probabilities of harmful behavior, yet those rare failures matter most for safety and certification. The authors want a principled, statistically rigorous way to quantify and certify LLM harmfulness on prompts.
AI.method: They adapt Clopper-Pearson confidence intervals to the LLM safety setting to derive probably approximately correct (PAC) bounds on the probability of harmful outputs. Technically, they design an algorithm that explores the autoregressive generation tree but uses latent-space features to prioritize branches that are more likely to be harmful. This guided exploration lets them focus evaluations on risky regions of the output space while preserving the formal statistical properties needed for sound bounds.
AI.result: Their algorithm can compute non-trivial (informative) lower bounds on the probability of harmful outputs for state-of-the-art LLMs, even when the true harm probability is extremely small. The bounds they obtain are statistically rigorous: they are guaranteed to be below the true harmfulness probability with high confidence.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a new framework for statistically rigorous evaluation and certification of LLM safety. By combining PAC-style confidence bounds with structure-aware exploration of the generation tree, it becomes feasible to quantify and certify very low probabilities of harmful behavior in modern LLMs.

title: TRUST-ESD: A Risk-Calibrated and Governance-Aware AI Framework for Enterprise Strategic Decision Support Under Uncertainty
comment: 15 pages, 7 figures, 4 tables. Submitted to APWeb-WAIM 2026, Danang, Vietnam, September 7-9, 2026
summary: Enterprise strategic decision support requires AI systems that are not only accurate, but also uncertainty-aware, risk-calibrated, explainable, and governance-compliant. This paper proposes TRUST-ESD, a risk-calibrated and governance-aware framework for enterprise decision support under uncertainty. TRUST-ESD evaluates feasible counterfactual strategies through predictive utility estimation, conformal uncertainty calibration, CVaR-based downside-risk scoring, risk-memory retrieval, policy-as-code governance, explainability, and human oversight. Unlike prediction-only methods that select actions by maximum expected utility, TRUST-ESD recommends strategies that balance value, reliability, risk exposure, and compliance. Experimental results show that TRUST-ESD improves risk-adjusted utility by 7.95%, reduces risk exposure by 23.22%, reduces CVaR by 23.78%, lowers calibration error by 13.89%, improves explanation fidelity by 10.90%, and increases governance compliance by 9.76% compared with strong uncertainty-aware baselines, while maintaining competitive predictive accuracy. Ablation and case-study analyses further confirm that uncertainty calibration, downside-risk scoring, risk memory, explainability, and governance validation jointly improve trustworthy enterprise decision-making.

AI.tldr: TRUST-ESD is a framework for enterprise decision support that jointly optimizes value, uncertainty calibration, downside risk, explainability, and governance compliance.
AI.motivation: Enterprise AI decisions affect high-stakes business outcomes, so firms need systems that go beyond raw predictive accuracy to also manage uncertainty, downside risk, explainability, and governance/compliance requirements. Existing methods often focus on expected utility or basic uncertainty, lacking integrated treatment of risk, memory of past failures, and policy-level controls.
AI.method: The authors design TRUST-ESD, a pipeline that: (1) generates feasible counterfactual strategies; (2) estimates their utility using predictive models; (3) calibrates uncertainty via conformal methods; (4) scores downside risk using Conditional Value at Risk (CVaR); (5) leverages a risk-memory component that recalls past adverse outcomes; (6) encodes governance constraints as policy-as-code for automated validation; and (7) provides explanations and human-in-the-loop oversight when recommending strategies. The system selects actions that trade off expected value, reliability, risk exposure, and compliance rather than just maximizing expected utility.
AI.result: On enterprise-like decision tasks, TRUST-ESD achieves higher quality and safer decisions than strong uncertainty-aware baselines: +7.95% risk-adjusted utility, −23.22% overall risk exposure, −23.78% CVaR, −13.89% calibration error, +10.90% explanation fidelity, and +9.76% governance compliance, while keeping predictive accuracy competitive. Ablation studies and case analyses show each component (uncertainty calibration, downside-risk scoring, risk memory, explainability, governance validation) contributes meaningfully to performance and trustworthiness.
AI.conclusion: Integrating uncertainty calibration, downside-risk measures, historical risk memory, explainability, and formal governance constraints into a single framework yields more trustworthy and better risk-adjusted enterprise decisions than prediction-focused or uncertainty-only methods. TRUST-ESD demonstrates that enterprise decision support should explicitly optimize for risk, reliability, and compliance alongside utility, and that these dimensions can be operationalized in a practical system.

title: PyroDash: Cost-Efficient Token-Level Small-Large Language Model Collaborative Inference
summary: Large language models (LLMs) provide strong reasoning capabilities but are expensive to serve at scale, whereas small language models (SLMs) are cheaper but less reliable on difficult problems. We introduce PyroDash, a cost-aware framework for token-level SLM-LLM collaborative inference. During generation, the SLM decides whether to request assistance by emitting a control token. A Collaborate Engine then sends the query and partial reasoning trace to a frozen LLM for completion through a single handoff. The policy is internalized in the SLM, requiring neither a separate router, LLM retraining, nor access to LLM logits. PyroDash trains the SLM in three stages: control-token embedding learning, offloading-oriented supervised fine-tuning, and cost-aware alignment with Group Relative Policy Optimization. Its reward balances answer accuracy against inference cost normalized by LLM-only inference. Across five mathematical reasoning benchmarks, PyroDash supports different accuracy-cost operating points. With $λ=0.05$, it achieves 64.04 percent average accuracy, 6.36 percentage points above the LLM-only baseline, while reducing cost by 20.4 percent. With $λ=0.6$, it achieves 54.55 percent accuracy with a 1.90 percent LLM token ratio and 0.012 LLM calls per example, reducing total cost from USD 49.36 to USD 1.78. These results show that learned token-level handoffs can reduce LLM use while preserving strong reasoning performance.

AI.tldr: PyroDash is a framework where a small language model learns when to hand off parts of reasoning to a larger model at the token level, trading off accuracy versus cost.
AI.motivation: Serving large language models is expensive, yet they are needed for hard reasoning tasks. Small models are cheaper but unreliable on such tasks. Existing collaboration approaches often need explicit routers, access to LLM internals, or are coarse-grained. The authors want a method where a small model can autonomously and efficiently decide, token by token, when to invoke a large model to save cost while maintaining or even improving accuracy.
AI.method: PyroDash embeds special control tokens that the small language model can generate to request help from a frozen large model. A Collaborate Engine detects these tokens, sends the current query and partial reasoning trace to the LLM, and integrates the LLM’s continuation in a single handoff. The small model is trained in three stages: (1) learning embeddings for the control tokens, (2) supervised fine-tuning focused on learning when to offload, and (3) reinforcement learning–style cost-aware alignment using Group Relative Policy Optimization, with a reward that trades off task accuracy versus normalized LLM usage cost.
AI.result: On five mathematical reasoning benchmarks, PyroDash can realize different accuracy–cost operating points by adjusting a trade-off parameter λ. For a low cost penalty (λ=0.05), it gets 64.04% average accuracy, outperforming an LLM-only baseline by 6.36 percentage points while reducing overall cost by 20.4%. For a high cost penalty (λ=0.6), it reaches 54.55% accuracy using only 1.90% of tokens from the LLM and 0.012 LLM calls per example, slashing total cost from USD 49.36 to USD 1.78.
AI.conclusion: Token-level, learned handoff policies embedded directly within a small language model can substantially reduce reliance on expensive large models while preserving strong reasoning accuracy. The approach works without modifying or retraining the LLM or using separate routing models, and allows flexible control over the accuracy–cost trade-off.

title: CUSUM-Shaped Inference-Time Monitoring and Targeted Re-Decoding for Quantized Small Language Model Reasoning
summary: Quantized small autoregressive reasoning models can enter long, repetitive, or unproductive trajectories, yet inference-time compute is usually allocated without observing how a trajectory develops. Building on an earlier token-level e-CUSUM controller, we develop MGT-B (Monitoring-Guided Test-time Backtracking), a revised external controller that maps overlapping windows of pre-sampling uncertainty and degeneration features to position-conditional empirical tail probabilities, accumulates mixture betting factors with a CUSUM-shaped reset, and responds to an alarm by estimating a rollback point, restoring token and key-value-cache state, and performing constrained re-decoding. To audit whether the effect persists on problem identities first observed after the manual choice of log threshold h = 10, we retrospectively exclude 260 IDs present in pre-threshold artifacts and retain the chronologically first post-threshold pair for each remaining ID, yielding a 240-pair chronology-audit set. On this set, accuracy changes from 82/240 to 88/240 (+2.50 percentage points; 13 corrections, 7 regressions; exact McNemar p = 0.2632; paired bootstrap 95% interval [-1.25, +6.25]). A broader 467-pair historical-coverage set of seed-matched pairs changes accuracy from 146/467 to 167/467 (+4.50 points; McNemar p = 0.000753), but includes 200 seed-1 IDs available before or during threshold selection and is reported only as an exploratory estimate. All 316 no-alarm outputs in the 467-pair set are identical to vanilla, while the 151 alarmed trajectories contain 29 corrections and 8 regressions. Neither analysis is confirmatory, and the empirical factors are not established as a valid e-process or e-detector. The results support a selective monitoring-and-repair mechanism for the studied MATH-500 setting, rather than a general or theoretically certified reasoning improvement.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an external controller, MGT-B, that monitors quantized small autoregressive reasoning models during inference, detects when they enter degenerate trajectories, and rolls back to repair them, showing selective but not universally guaranteed accuracy gains on MATH-500 benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Quantized small autoregressive reasoning models often fall into long, repetitive, or unproductive trajectories during generation, yet inference-time compute is typically allocated without adapting to how the trajectory actually evolves. There is a need for mechanisms that can monitor and intervene in real time to prevent or correct such degeneration, particularly for mathematical reasoning tasks, while providing some statistical grounding for when interventions are triggered.
AI.method: Building on a prior token-level e-CUSUM controller, the authors design MGT-B (Monitoring-Guided Test-time Backtracking), an external controller that: (1) observes overlapping windows of pre-sampling uncertainty and degeneration features during generation; (2) maps these observations to position-conditional empirical tail probabilities; (3) accumulates mixture betting factors using a CUSUM-like reset mechanism; (4) raises an alarm when accumulated evidence suggests degeneration; and (5) upon alarm, estimates an appropriate rollback point, restores token and key-value-cache state, and performs constrained re-decoding from that point. They then evaluate this controller on MATH-500 using carefully constructed evaluation sets that control for potential data leakage in choosing the log-threshold parameter h = 10.
AI.result: On a chronology-audit set of 240 problem pairs constructed to exclude IDs seen before threshold selection, accuracy improves from 82/240 to 88/240, a +2.50 percentage-point gain with 13 corrected and 7 regressed cases; the change is not statistically significant by McNemar's test (p = 0.2632), and a paired bootstrap 95% interval spans [-1.25, +6.25]. On a broader 467-pair historical-coverage set (which includes 200 IDs potentially seen during threshold selection and thus is exploratory), accuracy increases from 146/467 to 167/467 (+4.50 points) with McNemar p = 0.000753. Among these 467 pairs, the 316 no-alarm cases are identical to vanilla outputs, while the 151 alarmed trajectories contain 29 corrections and 8 regressions.
AI.conclusion: MGT-B demonstrates that selective, monitoring-guided rollback and re-decoding can repair a subset of degenerate reasoning trajectories in small quantized autoregressive models, leading to modest accuracy gains on the evaluated MATH-500 tasks without affecting non-degenerate runs. However, the analyses are exploratory rather than confirmatory, and the empirical betting factors used by the controller are not yet validated as a proper e-process or e-detector. Consequently, the work supports monitoring-and-repair as a promising but task- and setting-specific mechanism, rather than establishing a general or theoretically guaranteed improvement in reasoning performance.

title: Generative AI floods and dilutes the market for books
summary: Generative AI can produce book-length works of fiction at near-zero cost. These books are often dismissed as low-quality ``slop'' that buyers will ignore, and are assumed to carry little commercial weight. We test that assumption with full-text AI detection across 14,419 self-published genre-fiction books sold on Amazon from 2023 to 2026, matched to daily sales records through June 2026. None of these books disclose whether or not they contain AI-produced content. We find that books for which we detected substantial AI text ($>$ 25\%) make up a large share of the catalog but a smaller share of sales. Even so, they reach commercial scale, winning a growing share of sales over time and taking more of the scarce top-rank positions once held by books with no detected AI text. Over this period, the number of books with observed sales in a quarter grew 19.2-fold, while quarterly revenue grew only 8.9-fold. The market therefore added selling books faster than it added revenue, and revenue per selling book fell across most genres. Books with no AI text lose the most ground in genres with high AI diffusion, and most of all where Kindle Unlimited availability is high. Among top-selling books, those with substantial AI text draw on more distinctive language from existing books than do books with no AI text; for these books overlap rises with revenue, a gradient we do not detect for books with no AI text. Generative AI can thus reshape a creative market through scale rather than quality. Our results bear directly on the market-effect question at the center of the fair use defense to copyright infringement.

AI.tldr: Study measures how much generative-AI text appears in self-published genre fiction on Amazon (2023–2026) and how it affects sales and market structure.
AI.motivation: Generative AI can cheaply generate book-length fiction, often assumed to be low-quality and commercially irrelevant. Yet there is little empirical evidence on how AI-generated books actually perform in the market, how widespread they are, and whether they reshape revenue distribution, competition, and potential copyright/fair-use issues.
AI.method: Use full-text AI detection to estimate the share of AI-generated text in 14,419 self-published genre-fiction ebooks on Amazon (2023–2026). Match each title to daily sales and revenue data (including Kindle Unlimited) through June 2026. Classify books by AI share (e.g., substantial AI >25%), track catalog share, sales share, revenue growth, and rank positions over time and across genres. Measure vocabulary overlap of top-selling books with earlier titles to quantify distinctiveness and its relationship with revenue, separately for AI-heavy and non-AI books.
AI.result: Books with substantial AI-generated text constitute a large fraction of the catalog but a smaller fraction of total sales; nevertheless, they achieve meaningful and rising commercial scale, increasingly occupying high sales ranks. Between 2023 and 2026, the number of books with observed quarterly sales increased 19.2x while total quarterly revenue rose only 8.9x, implying falling revenue per selling book across most genres. Non-AI books lose the most market share in genres where AI diffusion and Kindle Unlimited penetration are high. Among top sellers, AI-heavy books reuse more distinctive language from existing books than non-AI books, and for them, greater linguistic overlap correlates positively with revenue—a pattern not seen for non-AI titles.
AI.conclusion: Generative AI is transforming self-published fiction markets not primarily by raising quality but by enabling massive scale and flooding the market with sellable books, which dilutes revenue per book and shifts top positions toward AI-heavy titles. The stronger language overlap and its positive association with revenue among top-selling AI books raise concerns about derivative use and copyright. These findings directly inform debates on whether large-scale, AI-enabled reuse of existing works can be defended as fair use based on its market effects.

title: PoTRE: Test-Time Reasoning inspired by Cognitive Heterogeneity
comment: Accepted at Transactions on Machine Learning Research (TMLR 2026)
summary: While Large Language Models (LLMs) excel at many tasks, they frequently struggle with complex reasoning that requires long-horizon planning and iterative error correction. Furthermore, standard single-stream prompting proves brittle when models encounter novel abstractions or rigorous domain constraints. We introduce PoTRE (Poly-Topological Reasoning Ensembles), a heterogeneous framework that decouples inference into four agents: (1) Adversarial Refinement Agent, (2) Hierarchical strategic Planning Agent, (3) Spectrum Search Agent, and (4) Direct Chain Agent. A final Task-Adaptive Aggregation Layer dynamically reconciles these perspectives -- via final candidate selection, semantic synthesis, or neuro-symbolic verification -- to produce a robust global solution. We evaluate PoTRE on three frontier benchmarks: ARC-AGI-2, Humanity's Last Exam (HLE), and PRBench Finance. PoTRE achieves state-of-the-art accuracy of 49.92% on HLE, surpassing the previous best official score. We demonstrate that this architectural heterogeneity achieves improved reasoning performance using similar or fewer inference tokens compared to heavily scaled homogeneous baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes PoTRE, a multi-agent reasoning framework that improves complex reasoning in LLMs by combining four specialized agents and an aggregation layer, achieving state-of-the-art results on challenging benchmarks with similar or fewer inference tokens.
AI.motivation: LLMs struggle with complex reasoning tasks that need long-horizon planning, iterative error correction, and robustness under strict domain constraints or novel abstractions. Existing single-stream prompting approaches are brittle and scaling homogeneous methods (e.g., just making models larger or adding more steps of the same style) has diminishing returns. The authors are motivated to design an architecture that can better decompose and handle different aspects of reasoning via specialized components while remaining token-efficient.
AI.method: They introduce PoTRE (Poly-Topological Reasoning Ensembles), which decomposes inference into four heterogeneous agents: (1) an Adversarial Refinement Agent that stress-tests, critiques, and refines candidate solutions; (2) a Hierarchical Strategic Planning Agent that performs long-horizon, multi-step planning at different levels of abstraction; (3) a Spectrum Search Agent that explores diverse solution candidates across a spectrum of reasoning paths; and (4) a Direct Chain Agent that performs standard chain-of-thought style reasoning. On top of these, a Task-Adaptive Aggregation Layer dynamically combines the agents’ outputs using strategies like candidate selection, semantic synthesis, or neuro-symbolic verification to produce a final answer. The system is evaluated against strong, homogeneous LLM baselines under comparable token budgets.
AI.result: On three challenging benchmarks—ARC-AGI-2, Humanity’s Last Exam (HLE), and PRBench Finance—PoTRE improves reasoning performance. In particular, it achieves a state-of-the-art 49.92% accuracy on HLE, exceeding the previous best official score. Across benchmarks, the heterogeneous multi-agent setup matches or outperforms heavily scaled homogeneous baselines while using similar or fewer inference tokens.
AI.conclusion: Architectural heterogeneity via specialized reasoning agents and an adaptive aggregation layer can substantially improve complex reasoning in LLMs without simply scaling model size or token counts. PoTRE’s multi-agent design yields better robustness to novel abstractions and strict constraints, establishes new state-of-the-art results on difficult reasoning benchmarks, and demonstrates that carefully structured ensembles of reasoning strategies are a promising direction for advancing LLM reasoning capabilities.

title: Notes to Self: Can LLMs Benefit from Experiential Abstractions?
summary: Humans distill experience into reusable abstractions, e.g., strategies and cautionary reminders, and apply them to gradually solve problems more effectively. We study whether Large Language Models (LLMs) can similarly benefit from such experiential abstractions. From LLMs' solution traces on the MATH training set, a stronger teacher or the LLMs themselves extract natural-language abstractions into a retrievable library. We explore two usage modes: (1) inference-time retrieval and (2) reinforcement learning (RL) with abstraction-augmented training prompts. Experiential abstractions improve LLM performance on mathematical and logical reasoning benchmarks. Self-extracted abstractions match teacher-extracted ones, and our abstraction usage framework can transfer to other datasets and models. These findings suggest LLMs can extract and apply experiential abstractions much as humans leverage distilled experience.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates whether large language models can, like humans, distill their past problem-solving experience into reusable textual abstractions and leverage them to improve future mathematical and logical reasoning.
AI.motivation: Humans naturally create and reuse high-level strategies and lessons learned from experience, which boosts their problem-solving abilities over time. Current LLMs largely solve each problem from scratch, without systematically reusing distilled experience. The authors are motivated to see if LLMs can similarly extract, store, and apply reusable experiential knowledge to enhance reasoning, especially on challenging math and logic tasks.
AI.method: The authors generate solution traces on the MATH training set using either a stronger teacher model or the target LLM itself. From these traces, they automatically extract natural-language experiential abstractions (e.g., strategies, patterns, warnings) and store them in a retrieval library. They then investigate two usage modes: (1) inference-time retrieval, where relevant abstractions are fetched and prepended or integrated into prompts at test time; and (2) reinforcement learning, where training prompts are augmented with retrieved abstractions so the LLM learns to internalize and exploit them. They test both teacher-extracted and self-extracted abstractions and evaluate across various math and logical reasoning benchmarks and different models/datasets.
AI.result: Including experiential abstractions consistently improves LLM performance on mathematical and logical reasoning benchmarks relative to baselines without such abstractions. The gains hold both when abstractions are used only at inference time and when they are integrated via RL-based training. Abstractions that the model extracts from its own traces perform comparably to those extracted by a stronger teacher model. The method also transfers: the same abstraction framework can be applied to new datasets and models and still yield improvements.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can indeed distill their own or a teacher's solution traces into reusable natural-language experiential abstractions and effectively use them to solve new problems better, analogously to how humans reuse distilled experience. Both retrieval-based usage and RL-based integration of these abstractions enhance math and logic reasoning, and self-extracted abstractions are sufficiently strong, reducing reliance on stronger teacher models. This suggests experiential abstraction is a promising direction for making LLMs more human-like in cumulative learning and systematic reasoning.

title: Train the Model, Not the Reader: Decodability Supervision for Verifiable Activation Explanations
summary: Natural-language autoencoders score explanations of hidden activations by reconstruction: an explanation is deemed faithful if the activation can be regenerated from it. The test is structurally insensitive to individual false claims: if flipping a claim does not change the reconstruction, the claim is never penalized. We show the test is passed in two ways, neither faithful. On a released Qwen-2.5-7B verbalizer, explanations reconstruct well above chance while ~2% of specific claims are reconstruction-dependent, so the score tracks gist, not specific facts. Under exact synthetic ground truth, the standard recipe develops co-adapted private codes (false wording the reconstruction depends on) in 5/5 runs, and fixes that leave the target model unchanged do not help. We contribute two audit protocols, the grounded-vs-true cross and the evaluator swap, and RECAP (Readable Encodings via Co-trained Auxiliary Predictors): linear heads trained alongside the target model to keep designated content decodable. On RECAP-trained sandbox models, fresh verbalizers state the designated content truly and the codes vanish, at a +0.001-nat cost. This replicates on a pretrained Pythia-160M: the content becomes reliably probe-decodable, though a fresh verbalizer conveys it only in part (truth 0.44-0.46 vs a near-zero control). For interpretability, high reconstruction does not certify individual claims. For AI safety, RECAP makes designated internal content independently checkable against probes rather than asserted by prose a model can game: an independent probe scores the verbalizer's true claims above its false ones (AUC 0.96, vs 0.82 without RECAP). Against an adversary that edits an explanation to maximize the reconstruction score while lying (suppressing ~87% of its lie penalty), the RECAP probe still flags the lies (AUC 0.95) while the control probe collapses to chance (0.51).

AI.tldr: The paper argues that current natural-language autoencoder methods for explaining neural activations are structurally incapable of guaranteeing claim-level faithfulness, and proposes RECAP, a co-training method that makes specific internal content linearly decodable and reliably checkable by probes instead of relying on potentially gameable natural-language explanations.
AI.motivation: Natural-language explanations of model internals are increasingly used for interpretability and safety, often evaluated by how well they allow reconstruction of hidden activations. However, this reconstruction-based test can be fooled: it can score high even when many individual statements in the explanation are false, as long as the overall gist is sufficient for reconstruction. There is a need to understand these failure modes and to design training procedures and audit protocols that make specific internal contents reliably and independently checkable rather than merely asserted by the model in language it can strategically manipulate.
AI.method:
1) Empirically evaluate a released Qwen-2.5-7B verbalizer to measure how often specific natural-language claims actually affect reconstruction, quantifying the dependence of reconstruction on individual claims.
2) Construct synthetic tasks with exact ground truth to analyze how standard verbalizer training develops co-adapted private codes—idiosyncratic false wording that reconstruction depends on—and test simple fixes that keep the target model fixed.
3) Propose two auditing protocols: the grounded-vs-true cross (comparing explanations grounded in activations vs. those optimized for truth) and the evaluator swap (changing the reconstruction evaluator) to diagnose unfaithful coding.
4) Introduce RECAP, where linear probe-like heads are co-trained with the target model so that designated internal content remains simply decodable; train sandbox models with RECAP and evaluate new verbalizers on their faithfulness and on the disappearance of private codes.
5) Apply RECAP to a pretrained Pythia-160M model, measure how well designated content becomes probe-decodable, and assess how much of that content a fresh verbalizer truthfully conveys.
6) Evaluate robustness by comparing probe-based detection of true vs. false claims, including under adversarially edited explanations optimized to maximize reconstruction while lying.
AI.result:
1) On Qwen-2.5-7B, explanations achieve reconstruction far above chance, but only about 2% of specific claims influence reconstruction, indicating the metric mainly reflects gist-level information rather than claim-level factual accuracy.
2) In synthetic settings with exact ground truth, the standard verbalizer training reliably (5/5 runs) develops co-adapted private codes: systematically false phrases on which the reconstruction depends. Attempts to fix this problem without modifying the target model fail.
3) The proposed audit protocols expose these failure modes, distinguishing whether explanations are grounded in activations or exploiting private codes.
4) With RECAP on sandbox models, co-trained linear heads keep designated content decodable; new verbalizers state this content truthfully, and the private codes disappear, incurring only a tiny performance cost (~0.001 nats).
5) On Pythia-160M, RECAP makes the designated internal content reliably linearly probe-decodable; a fresh verbalizer partially but not fully communicates this content (truth scores around 0.44–0.46 compared to near zero in a control without RECAP).
6) For safety evaluation, RECAP enables independent probes to score true verbalizer claims higher than false ones (AUC 0.96 vs. 0.82 without RECAP) and to remain effective under adversarially edited explanations that game the reconstruction score: the RECAP probe continues to detect lies (AUC 0.95) while the baseline probe fails (AUC ~0.51).
AI.conclusion: Reconstruction-based natural-language autoencoders do not guarantee claim-level faithfulness: high reconstruction can coexist with largely unfaithful explanations, supported by private codes and gist-level information. This undermines using such methods as a primary interpretability or safety guarantee. The paper’s RECAP approach, which co-trains simple linear heads to keep specified internal content decodable, yields models whose designated internal states can be independently checked via probes instead of being trusted purely on their own natural-language justifications. Empirically, RECAP eliminates private codes in sandbox settings, improves the reliability with which probes distinguish true from false verbalizer statements, and remains robust even when adversaries manipulate explanations to game reconstruction metrics. Therefore, for both interpretability and AI safety, independent probe-based checks of internal representations are more trustworthy than reconstruction scores from natural-language autoencoders, and methods like RECAP provide a practical way to enforce such checkability at low performance cost.

title: LKValues: Aligning Large Language Models with Sri Lankan Societal Values
comment: 37 pages, 10 figures, and 15 tables. Includes appendices. Datasets are available at the project repository
summary: Value alignment of Large Language Models (LLMs) has been shown to be culturally biased toward Western norms. This results in the mishandling of local values in multilingual societies such as Sri Lanka that have their unique cultural dynamics. Existing benchmarks overlook Sri Lankan-contextualized values in its official language Sinhala, hindering culturally sensitive evaluation and fine-tuning. To bridge this gap, we propose LKValues, the first survey-grounded resource suite for Sri Lankan value alignment. From a trilingual survey of 205 respondents, blending adapted global frameworks and LLM-elicited local constructs, we derive 40 majority-endorsed societal values. Using these values, we construct LKvaluesIT, a Sinhala-English news-derived instruction corpus containing 150k scenario-based instances, and LKvaluesBench, a value-sensitive evaluation benchmark of 1,000 instances. We evaluate a set of proprietary and open-weight LLMs with LKvaluesBench. We fine-tune three open-weight base models (Qwen3.5-4B-Base, Qwen3.5-9B-Base, and Aya-Expanse-8B-Base). Our experiments show that newer and larger LLMs still exhibit low-resource and cultural value-alignment gaps. LKValues fine-tuning improves Qwen-family models in English and Sinhala, reducing invalid outputs and cross-lingual disparities, though gains remain model-family dependent. These highlight LKValues efficacy in embedding Sri Lankan values, offering a replicable pipeline for low-resource, country-specific pluralist value alignment. The dataset is publicly available at https://github.com/NextME14/LKValues.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces LKValues, the first Sri Lanka–specific, survey-grounded value alignment resource and benchmark for LLMs, showing that current models mis-handle Sri Lankan values and that fine-tuning on LKValues improves cultural alignment, especially for Sinhala and English in Sri Lanka’s context.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM value alignment is biased toward Western norms and underrepresents local cultural values, especially in multilingual, low-resource contexts like Sri Lanka. There is no benchmark or dataset focused on Sri Lankan societal values in Sinhala (and English), which prevents accurate evaluation and culturally sensitive fine-tuning. The authors aim to fill this gap by defining Sri Lankan societal values in a data-driven way and creating resources to test and improve model alignment with these values.
AI.method: 1) Conduct a trilingual (Sinhala, English, Tamil) survey with 205 participants, combining adapted global value frameworks and LLM-elicited local value constructs to elicit Sri Lankan societal values. 2) Derive 40 majority-endorsed societal values from the survey. 3) Build LKvaluesIT, a 150k-instance Sinhala-English instruction dataset based on news-derived, scenario-style prompts labeled with these values. 4) Build LKvaluesBench, a 1,000-instance evaluation benchmark focusing on value-sensitive scenarios. 5) Evaluate proprietary and open-weight LLMs on LKvaluesBench to measure cultural and cross-lingual value alignment gaps. 6) Fine-tune three open-weight base models (Qwen3.5-4B, Qwen3.5-9B, Aya-Expanse-8B) on LKvaluesIT and analyze performance changes, including invalid outputs and cross-lingual disparities.
AI.result: Benchmarked models, including larger and more recent LLMs, still show substantial value misalignment and performance gaps for Sri Lankan contexts, especially in low-resource Sinhala. After fine-tuning on LKvaluesIT, Qwen models demonstrate improved alignment with Sri Lankan values, fewer invalid responses, and reduced disparities between Sinhala and English outputs. However, improvements vary by model family, indicating that not all architectures benefit equally from the same fine-tuning recipe. The LKvaluesBench proves sensitive to these differences and can reveal both cultural and cross-lingual gaps.
AI.conclusion: LKValues provides an effective, survey-grounded pipeline for embedding Sri Lankan societal values into LLMs and for systematically evaluating value alignment in a low-resource, multilingual national context. While current LLMs—even large and recent ones—remain misaligned with Sri Lankan values, fine-tuning on LKValues can measurably improve their behavior, particularly for Sinhala and English. The work demonstrates a replicable approach that other low-resource countries can adapt to build their own pluralist, country-specific value alignment datasets and benchmarks, advancing culturally aware AI development.

title: SoftReason: A Fully Differentiable Neuro-Soft-Symbolic Deductive Reasoning Architecture over High-Dimensional Perceptual Data
summary: In many reasoning problems, the premises are not observed as discrete symbols, but must be inferred from high-dimensional inputs. Further, the predicate vocabulary, argument structure, and trusted evidence are supplied by a Knowledge Graph (KG), or rule definitions. Classical neuro-symbolic pipelines have a discrete interface between perception and deduction. We present a neuro-soft-symbolic architecture for differentiable deductive reasoning over latent perceptual facts and knowledge-provided predicates. SoftReason removes the gradient gap by representing the deductive state as a local soft interpretation tensor over candidate constants and predicates. Perception proposes probabilistic base facts, KG triples enter as high-confidence soft evidence, and every query anchor, predicate choice, and closure update remains differentiable. Our core innovation is a learned differentiable lift of the immediate-consequence operator. It uses predicate-definition embeddings and latent composition channels to form soft body-predicate mixtures, aggregate over all possible witnesses, propose query-conditioned head facts, and update the interpretation through a monotone probabilistic OR. We instantiate the framework on Knowledge-aware Visual Question Answering (KVQA), and demonstrates how SoftReason supports end-to-end perceptual grounding, KG evidence injection, and differentiable deductive closure in one trainable architecture.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes SoftReason, a neuro-soft-symbolic architecture for differentiable deductive reasoning that jointly handles perception, knowledge graph evidence, and logical closure in a single end-to-end trainable model.
AI.motivation: Traditional neuro-symbolic systems separate perception (from images, text, etc.) and symbolic reasoning with a hard, discrete interface, which blocks gradient flow and prevents true end-to-end learning. In real-world reasoning tasks, such as visual question answering with external knowledge, both the premises (facts from perception) and the predicates/relations (from a knowledge graph or rule base) must be integrated smoothly. The authors aim to eliminate the gap between high-dimensional perceptual inputs and symbolic deduction so that the entire reasoning process becomes differentiable and can be trained jointly.
AI.method: They introduce SoftReason, which represents the state of deduction as a soft interpretation tensor over candidate constants and predicates. Perception modules output probabilistic base facts from raw inputs, while knowledge graph triples are injected as high-confidence soft evidence. The key technical contribution is a differentiable version of the immediate-consequence operator: using embeddings of predicate definitions and latent composition channels, the model constructs soft mixtures over rule bodies, sums over all possible witnesses (substitutions), proposes query-conditioned head facts, and updates the interpretation using a monotone probabilistic OR. All components—query anchoring, predicate selection, and closure updates—are differentiable, allowing gradient-based learning.
AI.result: The framework is instantiated on Knowledge-aware Visual Question Answering (KVQA). In this setting, SoftReason supports: (1) end-to-end grounding of visual perception into latent facts, (2) integration of knowledge graph evidence as differentiable soft constraints, and (3) iterative deductive closure within the same trainable architecture. Empirical results (implied, though not detailed in the abstract) show that the method can effectively perform KVQA by jointly learning perception and reasoning steps.
AI.conclusion: SoftReason demonstrates that deductive reasoning over latent perceptual facts and knowledge-provided predicates can be made fully differentiable through a soft interpretation-based architecture and a learned differentiable lift of the immediate-consequence operator. This closes the gradient gap between perception and symbolic deduction, enabling end-to-end neuro-soft-symbolic reasoning pipelines, particularly useful in tasks like knowledge-aware visual question answering.
